This research formalizes every RP-facing integration flow in the EUDI Wallet ecosystem — from registration through remote, proximity, W2W, and SCA payment presentation to post-presentation obligations — at protocol depth. By analysing the eIDAS 2.0 Regulation, 11 CIRs, 11 Technical Specifications, and 3 external protocol standards (OpenID4VP 1.0, HAIP 1.0, ISO/IEC 18013-5), this document provides a prescriptive blueprint for RPs that must accept EUDI Wallet credentials by December 2027.

Register with your national Registrar immediately — registration is the prerequisite for obtaining WRPACs, WRPRCs, and appearing in the national register. Delays here compress the entire integration timeline (§4, CIR 2025/848).

Support both SD-JWT VC and mdoc from day one — both credential format stacks are mandatory. Two complete verification pipelines are required, including different selective disclosure models, device binding verification, and trust anchor formats (§6, Finding 2).

Choose Direct RP or Intermediary model early — the Direct RP model requires your own WRPAC(s) and infrastructure; the Intermediary model delegates Wallet interaction to a third party but introduces data-forwarding constraints and DORA third-party risk (§25, §21.4).

Treat the EUDI integration infrastructure as a critical-path external dependency — LoTE endpoints, Status List endpoints, the Registrar API, and WRPAC validity create a chain of hard dependencies. Failure in any causes a hard stop. Financial RPs must include these in DORA resilience testing (Finding 4).

Implement HAIP 1.0 compliant OpenID4VP — this means JAR-based authorization requests, x509_hash Client ID mode, direct_post.jwt response mode, DCQL queries, and ephemeral key management for response encryption (§8, §9, §10).

Build a dedicated Status List verification pipeline — despite conceptual simplicity, this requires HTTP caching, DEFLATE decompression, JWT/CWT signature verification, and bit-index mapping. Do not underestimate this (Finding 14, Appendix B).

Implement pseudonym support with progressive assurance — register pseudonyms at low assurance via WebAuthn, upgrade via PID step-up verification when needed. Never refuse pseudonyms where identification is not legally required (§16, Art. 5b(9), Finding 24).

Implement anti-linkability controls from the start — never persist unique attestation elements (salts, hash arrays, signatures) beyond the verification session. Credential churn is a designed privacy feature, not a bug (§11.10, Finding 20).

Implement TS12 SCA flow for payment authentication (financial RPs) — structure transaction_data in OpenID4VP requests per Topic W HLRs. The signed KB-JWT response constitutes the PSD2 Dynamic Linking proof (§15, TS12).

Implement data deletion infrastructure early — TS7 mandates a supportURI endpoint. Build a purpose-built deletion handler at a stable URL; browser-accessible forms are preferred by Wallet Units. Over-requesting is discoverable via the Wallet's permanent transaction log (§20, Finding 22).

Your EUDI integration architecture is shaped by two independent dimensions — engineering capacity (what you can build vs. buy) and corporate structure (how many legal entities register) — with sector obligations layered on top. The quadrant chart below plots typical RP archetypes on this surface; the reference table beneath maps each sector to its protocol stack.

Each quadrant implies a deployment model: bottom-left → SaaS Intermediary (§25, §26.7); bottom-right → Vendor Connector + Group IT intermediary registration (§26.7, §27); top-left → Direct RP + Vendor Connector (§26.7, §27); top-right → Self-Hosted / Hybrid with full pipeline control (§9.5, §26.7, §27.7, §30.5). Corporate groups add the Group Intermediary registration pattern (§24.4.4, §25): one group IT entity registers as intermediary RP (flat two-layer only — no chaining); each subsidiary registers as end-RP with per-use-case RPRC. Cross-border groups additionally require per-MS registration via home MS — passporting does not exempt from RP registration.
↗ Strong × Group/Cross-border
PID + PuB-EAAs (diplomas, qualifications)
↙ Limited–Moderate × Standalone
PID + PuB-EAAs (health credentials)
Remote same-device + Proximity (retail)
Remote (+ Proximity edge cases)
PID + PuB-EAA (mDL) + address
↙ Limited–Moderate × Standalone

Combined presentation cryptographic binding is not yet specified — ARF defines HLRs (ACP_10–ACP_15) but no concrete mechanism. Until standardised, RPs must rely on presentation-based or attribute-based binding for multi-attestation verification (Finding 15, OQ #10).

EU Certificate Transparency log infrastructure does not exist yet — CIR 2025/848 and Topic S require WRPAC CT logging, and Wallet Units will verify SCTs. No EU-operated CT log is established, creating a deployment dependency (Finding 21, OQ #14).

W2W Verifier authentication is a fundamental gap — in Wallet-to-Wallet flows, the Verifier has no WRPAC and no registration certificate. Most RP trust infrastructure does not apply (Finding 13, OQ #1).

ZKP-based selective disclosure is on the roadmap but not production-ready — range proofs, set membership proofs, and predicate proofs are specified in TS4/TS13/TS14 but unsupported by any Wallet implementation. RPs should design pluggable proof-type interfaces (Finding 19, OQ #12).
Data deletion API is not standardised — TS7 defines 9 interfaces spanning browser, email, and phone channels, but no machine-readable API. Each RP's deletion process is bespoke (Finding 16, OQ #11).

SCA attestation type identification is category-based, not fixed — no single VCT value for SCA attestations; RPs must implement category-based matching logic driven by payment scheme rulebooks (Finding 12, OQ #15).

Device binding is recommended, not mandatory — RPs must handle both device-bound (cnf + KB-JWT) and non-device-bound attestations. High-assurance use cases cannot enforce device binding via DCQL queries (Finding 18, OQ #13).

Start with the Reading Guide above to identify the sections most relevant to your role. The persona-based reading paths provide curated sequences for Bank RP Architects, Public Sector RPs, Intermediary/Vendors, Mobile Developers, Security Engineers, QA Engineers, and Data Protection Officers — each path builds understanding progressively from synthesis through to the protocol details that support it.

The European Digital Identity Wallet (EUDI Wallet) is the centrepiece of the revised eIDAS Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183), commonly known as eIDAS 2.0. By December 2026, every EU Member State must provide at least one EUDI Wallet to its citizens. By December 2027, regulated private-sector entities — including banks, payment service providers, telecom operators, healthcare providers, and very large online platforms — must accept the EUDI Wallet for user identification, authentication, and (where applicable) Strong Customer Authentication.

This investigation examines the EUDI Wallet ecosystem exclusively from the Relying Party (RP) perspective — the entity that requests and verifies credentials presented by Wallet Users. The canonical RP throughout this document is a bank or financial institution, as banks face the most complex integration requirements: they must support remote and proximity presentation flows, implement SCA via EUDI Wallet for PSD2 compliance, perform AML/KYC onboarding using wallet-presented credentials, and integrate with the national RP registration infrastructure.
Regulatory deadline pressure — The December 2027 mandate for private-sector acceptance is 21 months away. Banks and financial institutions must begin integration planning now to meet the timeline.

Specification maturity — The critical Technical Specifications (TS5, TS6, TS7, TS8, TS9, TS12) reached v1.0 in 2025. OpenID4VP 1.0 achieved Final status in July 2025. HAIP 1.0 was approved in December 2025. The ARF has been updated through v2.8 (the latest published version as of March 2026), with 27+ CIRs now adopted.
Pilot programme insights — The EU Large-Scale Pilots (POTENTIAL, EWC, DC4EU, NOBID) have generated real-world implementation experience that informs the flows documented here.

SCA integration — TS12 (SCA Implementation with Wallet, v1.0 December 2025) defines the complete payment authentication flow using EUDI Wallet, creating an urgent integration requirement for banks under PSD2.

Trust infrastructure deployment — Member States are establishing Registrars, Access Certificate Authorities, and national registries per CIR 2025/848, creating the operational infrastructure that RPs must integrate with.
Identifier and trust model analysis: X.509 vs. DIDs, platform wallet landscape, and RP DID requirements
Complete RP registration flow with Member State Registrar (CIR 2025/848, TS5, TS6)
Trust infrastructure: Access Certificates (WRPAC), Registration Certificates (WRPRC), Trusted Lists, WUA verification
Credential format internals: SD-JWT VC (selective disclosure, Key Binding JWT) and mdoc (MSO, namespaces, device binding)
Remote presentation: same-device and cross-device flows via OpenID4VP (HAIP 1.0, DCQL)
Proximity presentation: supervised and unsupervised flows via ISO/IEC 18013-5
Wallet-to-wallet interactions (TS9) — where the RP is another Wallet User acting as Verifier
SCA for electronic payments: issuer-requested and third-party-requested flows (TS12, PSD2)
Document signing: RP integration with qualified electronic signature flows (wallet-channelled, RP-channelled, QTSP web portal), CSC API v2.0, Document Retrieval protocol, PAdES signature formats
Pseudonym-based authentication (Use Cases A–D, WebAuthn integration)
Combined presentations via DCQL (multi-attestation requests)
RP obligations: data deletion (TS7), DPA reporting (TS8), embedded disclosure policy evaluation
Intermediary model and trust flows
Regulatory compliance: eIDAS 2.0 obligations, PSD2/PSR SCA bridge, GDPR, AML/KYC, DORA
W3C Digital Credentials API implications for RP integration
Wallet Provider implementation internals (WSCA/WSCD architecture, key management)
PID Provider and Attestation Provider issuance internals (covered only where they intersect RP flows)
Wallet Solution certification by CABs (covered at a high level for RP trust assessment)
National-level implementation variations (each Member State's specific registration procedures)

This group establishes the legal and infrastructural prerequisites for Relying Party participation in the EUDI ecosystem. It details the eIDAS 2.0 regulatory framework, the mandatory registration process with Member State Registrars, the credential formats (SD-JWT VC, mdoc), and the trust infrastructure (WRPACs, Trusted Lists) required to perform cryptographic verification.

The revised eIDAS Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183), adopted on 11 April 2024 and entering into force on 20 May 2024, amends the original eIDAS Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 to establish the legal framework for the European Digital Identity Wallet. For Relying Parties, the key articles are:

passages.text: Article 5b(7) specifies that the following categories of private-sector entities must accept the EUDI Wallet when user identification is required by national or Union law, or by contractual obligation:
Transport service providers (airlines, railways, etc.)
Financial services (banks, PSPs, insurers, investment firms) — under PSD2, MiFID II, Solvency II, AMLD
Social security institutions
Healthcare providers and institutions
Drinking water supply and waste treatment services
Digital infrastructure and services — including Very Large Online Platforms (Art. 33 DSA)
Education and training institutions — for educational credentials
Telecom operators — under EECC
The acceptance deadline is 21 December 2027 (24 months after adoption of the first implementing act specifying technical specifications under Art. 5a(23), which was adopted in December 2025).
The Commission has adopted a comprehensive set of Implementing Regulations that operationalise the eIDAS 2.0 framework. The following CIRs are directly relevant to Relying Parties:

CIR 2024/2982 (Protocols and interfaces) is the most technically significant for RPs. It specifies:
The use of OpenID4VP for remote presentation requests (Art. 3)
The use of ISO/IEC 18013-5 for proximity presentation (Art. 4)
Requirements for RP authentication towards Wallet Units (Art. 5)
Data deletion request support (Art. 6)
DPA reporting support (Art. 7)
Pseudonym support requirements (Art. 14)
Transaction data support for SCA (implicit in Art. 3, detailed in TS12)
CIR 2025/848 (Registration of Wallet-Relying Parties) is the operational foundation for RP onboarding. It specifies:
National register establishment and maintenance (Art. 3)
Common registration policies (Art. 4)
Public API for querying RP registration information (Art. 3(5)–(6))
Access Certificate issuance (Art. 7) — the WRPAC
Registration Certificate issuance (Art. 8) — the WRPRC (optional per Member State)

The ARF (currently at v2.8+) is the Commission-maintained technical reference document that interprets the Regulation and CIRs into an implementable architecture. While the ARF is informative (not legally binding), it provides the de facto technical consensus for the ecosystem.
Key ARF sections relevant to RPs:
Relying Parties, RP Instances, Intermediaries — role definitions
Registrars — registration process
Access Certificate Authorities — certificate issuance
Providers of Registration Certificates — WRPRC issuance
Data presentation flows — proximity and remote
Catalogue of attributes and attestation schemes
Transactional data — SCA/dynamic linking
Attestation presentation and verification
RP authentication, disclosure policy, attribute verification
Presentation to intermediaries

Remote presentation protocol

A Relying Party (RP) is a service provider that requests attributes contained within a PID, QEAA, PuB-EAA, or EAA from a Wallet Unit, subject to User approval and within the limits of applicable legislation and rules.

Important distinction: The term "Relying Party" in the ARF refers exclusively to service providers requesting attributes — not to Attestation Providers (QEAA Providers, PuB-EAA Providers, EAA Providers), even though the Regulation technically classifies Attestation Providers as Relying Parties too.
An RP may rely on the Wallet Unit for different reasons:
Legal requirement — Art. 5b(7) mandates acceptance by specific sectors
Contractual agreement — business decision to accept EUDI Wallet credentials
Own decision — voluntary adoption for competitive advantage
An RP Instance is a system consisting of software and hardware that the RP uses to interact with Wallet Units. Each RP Instance:
Maintains an interface with Wallet Units to request PIDs and attestations
Implements RP authentication using an Access Certificate (WRPAC) obtained by the RP
Operates independently — a single RP can have multiple RP Instances (e.g., different branches, different services, different environments)
Each RP Instance requires its own Access Certificate, though the legal subject of all certificates is the RP itself.
An intermediary is a special class of RP defined in Art. 5b(10):
"Intermediaries acting on behalf of relying parties shall be deemed to be relying parties and shall not store data about the content of the transaction."
Offers services to other RPs (called "intermediated RPs") to connect to Wallet Units on their behalf
Performs all tasks assigned to an RP in the ARF on behalf of the intermediated RP
Is subject to registration requirements as an RP itself
Must not store the content of the transaction data (User attributes)
Must forward presented attributes to the intermediated RP
From the RP perspective, the following ecosystem entities are critical:
Registers the RP, records intended attributes and purposes, issues registration data
Access Certificate Authority
Issues WRPAC(s) — one per RP Instance — enabling Wallet authentication
Provider of Registration Certificates
Issues WRPRC (optional per MS) — embeds registration data in a certificate
Trusted List / LoTE Provider
Publishes trust anchors that Wallet Units use to verify RP certificates
Builds the Wallet Solution that Users interact with; RP has no direct interface
Issues PIDs that RPs request and verify
Issues QEAAs, PuB-EAAs, EAAs that RPs request and verify
Certifies Wallet Solutions — RP relies on certification for trust
Oversees RP compliance with eIDAS 2.0 obligations

This document focuses on natural person PID presentation, which is the primary use case for the EUDI Wallet ecosystem's initial deployment. However, Relying Parties operating in B2B contexts — financial onboarding, procurement, contract signing, VAT verification — will increasingly encounter legal person identity presentations. This section provides the essential context.
passages.headingText: 3. Legal Person Identification and the European Business Wallet

passages.text: ARF v2.8.0 (Topic 28) explicitly descoped wallet units for legal persons from the current EUDI framework. All three high-level requirements for legal person wallets (LP_01, LP_02, LP_03) are marked "Empty" in ARF v2.8.0, confirming complete descoping in favour of a separate regulation.

The European Commission published COM(2025) 838 proposing a dedicated European Business Wallet (EBW) regulation to complement the EUDI Wallet. The EBW will provide a separate digital wallet and identity solution for legal persons — companies, SMEs, sole traders, and public sector bodies.
Key architectural decision: The EBW is designed to share the same underlying trust infrastructure as the EUDI Wallet. RPs will not need a separate trust integration for legal persons.
EUDI Wallet (Natural Person)
Commission-compiled, per-provider-type
Same infrastructure, extended for LPID Providers
Access Certificate Authority
Same CAs, same certificate profiles
MS-level Registrar for RP registration
Same Registrar infrastructure
Same WUA mechanism for EBW Wallet Units
Notified per MS, issues WUA/WIA
Separate EBW Wallet Providers
PID Providers (civil registries)
LPID Providers (business registers via BRIS)
Certifies EBW Wallet Solutions separately

RP registration is unchanged: the same MS Registrar handles RP registration for both natural person and legal person attribute requests. An RP requesting LPID attributes must include EWC_LPID_Attestation (or the future standardised VCT) in its intendedAttributes alongside eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1 if it accepts both entity types. The TS6 LegalEntity class supports both LegalPerson and NaturalPerson sub-types (§3.2.1).

LPID (Legal Person Identification Data) is the legal person equivalent of the natural person PID. It is issued by national business registers (LPID Providers) to European Business Wallets. The EUDI Wallet Consortium (EWC) published the LPID specification in RFC005 (October 2024).
LPID has a deliberately minimal mandatory attribute set — only two credential subject attributes:
Unique identifier in EUID structure (§3.4)
Official current legal person name as registered in the business register
The LPID also includes mandatory metadata attributes:
Name of issuing authority, or ISO 3166 Alpha-2 country code
EUID of the issuing authority
ISO 3166-1 Alpha-2 country code
EUID of the authentic source (can differ from issuing authority)
Name of the authentic source
Unique index in revocation list
URL to access revocation information

RP impact: The dramatically smaller attribute set means LPID verification is simpler per-credential, but RPs in B2B contexts will almost always need LPID combined with a natural person PID and potentially a mandate credential — making the overall verification flow more complex than natural-person-only scenarios. See §18.5.2 for DCQL query examples and §11.12 for the verification pipeline delta.

The European Unique Identifier (EUID) is the primary cross-border identifier for legal persons, regulated by Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/1042, §10. It replaces LEI, VAT number, and national registration numbers as the canonical identifier in the LPID credential.

To rely on Wallet Units for providing a service, an RP must register with a Registrar in the Member State where it is established (Art. 5b(1), CIR 2025/848 Art. 3). Registration is a prerequisite for:
Receiving one or more Access Certificates (WRPAC) from an Access Certificate Authority
Optionally receiving one or more Registration Certificates (WRPRC) if supported by the MS
Having registration data published in the national register — publicly accessible online
Being discoverable and verifiable by Wallet Units via the Registrar API

Beyond initial registration, CIR 2025/848 Art. 5(2)–(3) imposes ongoing obligations: the RP must ensure that all information provided is accurate at the time of registration (Art. 5(2)) and must update any previously registered information without undue delay (Art. 5(3)). Failure to maintain accurate registration data — for instance, not updating contact information, changed service scope, or revised intended attributes — constitutes a ground for registrar-initiated suspension under Art. 9(2)(a). Additionally, Art. 6(7) requires that when an RP no longer intends to rely upon wallet units for a specific registration, it must notify the registrar without undue delay and request cancellation of that registration.

TS5 defines the common data model for RP registration information, and TS6 specifies the minimum common data set required for registration. Together, they define the WalletRelyingParty data structure.

The data model is defined in TS5 §2, with superclass dependencies on TS2 §2.1–§2.2. The main class WalletRelyingParty inherits from Provider, which in turn inherits from LegalEntity. TS5 defines four auxiliary classes (IntendedUse, Credential, Claim, MultiLangString) while TS2 defines five more (Identifier, Policy, LegalPerson, NaturalPerson, Law).
TS6 defines which attributes are mandatory and which are conditional based on the RP's role:
Unique identifier assigned by Registrar
Official legal name of the entity
If different from legal name
LEI, national ID, or other identifier
RELYING_PARTY or INTERMEDIARY
At least one: website, email, or phone
Attestation types and attributes RP will request
intendedAttributes[].purpose
Purpose for requesting each attribute set
Required if RP uses an intermediary

RP Implementation Note: The supportURI field is particularly important because it is used by the Wallet Unit to enable Users to submit data deletion requests (TS7) and is included in the WRPRC (if available). RPs SHOULD register a website URL as the primary supportURI, as the Wallet Unit assumes a browser is always available on the user's device.
The entitlement field uses a closed enumeration of 10 values defined in CIR 2025/848 Annex I §13. Each RP must declare one or more entitlements at registration:
Provider of services — the default for most RPs requesting attributes from Wallet Users
Qualified TSP issuing qualified electronic attestations of attributes
TSP issuing non-qualified electronic attestations of attributes
Public sector body (or acting on its behalf) issuing attestations from an authentic source
Provider of person identification data
Qualified TSP issuing qualified certificates for electronic seals
Qualified TSP issuing qualified certificates for electronic signatures
Qualified TSP managing remote qualified electronic signature creation devices
Qualified TSP managing remote qualified electronic seal creation devices
ESig_ESeal_Creation_Provider
Non-qualified TSP providing remote creation of electronic signatures or seals

Sub-entitlements (Annex I §14): For the Non_Q_EAA_Provider entitlement, Member States may define additional sub-entitlements to specify which attestations a particular non-qualified issuer is authorised to issue. This enables granular control without fragmenting the top-level enumeration.

Practical guidance: Most RPs that only consume attributes from Wallet Users (e.g., for KYC, age verification, or login) register with the single entitlement Service_Provider. RPs that also issue attestations (dual-role entities — see §15.5, Finding #38) require both Service_Provider and the relevant provider entitlement (e.g., QEAA_Provider).
Each Member State must publish one or more national registration policies (CIR 2025/848 Art. 4). These policies must include at minimum the following 7 content items (Art. 4(3)):
Identification and authentication procedures for RPs during registration
Required supporting documentation (identity, business registration, entitlements)
Authentic sources or official electronic records relied upon for data accuracy
Any other information or evidence required as part of the process
Automated means for RPs to register or update an existing registration (where applicable)
Redress mechanism available to RPs under MS law — i.e., what recourse an RP has if registration is rejected or suspended
Rules and procedures for verifying the RP's identity and other relevant information

RP relevance: Item (f) — the redress mechanism — is particularly important. If an RP's registration is rejected or suspended (§3.3.2), the MS registration policy must specify how the RP can appeal or seek administrative review. RPs preparing for registration should consult their MS's published policy to understand the available remedies.

Architectural Note (Direct vs Intermediated Registration): The flow below represents the Direct RP Model, where the Relying Party registers to obtain both Registration Certificates (expressing intended use) and an Access Certificate (for Wallet connection). In the Intermediary RP Model, the Intermediary registers to obtain the Access Certificate, while the Intermediated RP registers to obtain Registration Certificates referencing their chosen Intermediary.
1. Relying Party submits registration application to Registrar

The Relying Party submits a registration application to the Registrar in its Member State. This submission formalizes its intent to request specific attributes (such as PID or SCA) from Wallet Users. While the API for submission (POST /wrp) varies per Member State, the underlying data model standardizes across the EU (TS5, TS6).
Failure Path: If the submitted data model fails schema validation, the Registrar returns a 400 Bad Request and terminates the flow.
Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party triggers a REGISTRATION_SUBMISSION_INITIATED SIEM event.
2. Registrar validates application

The national Registrar systematically processes and validates the Relying Party's application against the policies mandated by eIDAS 2.0 (CIR 2025/848). This evaluation establishes the legal foundation for the Relying Party's operation within the EUDI ecosystem.
The validation routine executes four primary checks:

Legal entity verification — The Registrar cross-references the submitted organizational metadata (legalName, business identifiers) against authoritative national business registries (e.g., Handelsregister in Germany, KVK in the Netherlands) to ensure the Relying Party is a legally incorporated and active entity.

Entitlement verification — The Registrar confirms the Relying Party possesses the legal standing required to request the specific attributes declared in intendedAttributes under the stated lawfulBasis. For instance, a financial institution requesting a PID under Anti-Money Laundering (AMLD) regulations must correspond to a valid banking license in the national regulator's database.

Intended use assessment — The Registrar enforces the GDPR's data minimisation principle (Art. 5(1)(c)) by strictly assessing whether the array of requested attributes is strictly necessary and proportionate to the declared purpose.

Intermediary status (if applicable) — If the Relying Party designates itself as an intermediary (operating on behalf of other end-RPs), the Registrar verifies compliance with the specific no-storage and data-passthrough mandates articulated in eIDAS Art. 5b(10).
Failure Path: If the validation process detects fraud, mismatched credentials, or a severe policy violation, the Registrar rejects the application and terminates the process.
Audit Telemetry: The Registrar generates a REG_APP_VALIDATION_FAILED SIEM event upon detecting anomalous or non-compliant registration data.
3. Registrar confirms registration to Relying Party

Upon successful validation, the Registrar assigns the Relying Party a persistent, globally unique identifier—the walletRelyingPartyId (e.g., urn:eudi:wrp:de:bank-example:12345). The Registrar then commits the verified registration payload to the authoritative national register.

This activation immediately elevates the Relying Party's operational standing (status: ACTIVE), and the Registrar exposes the verified data via the public Registrar REST API (GET /wrp/{identifier}). This endpoint acts as the systemic source of truth, enabling:
Wallet Units: To dynamically query and render the Relying Party's verified identity and intended use cases on the user's consent screen during presentation flows.
Access CAs: To strictly verify the Relying Party's authorization status prior to minting any cryptographic WRPACs (Step 5).
Registration Cert Providers: To retrieve the authoritative data required to issue offline WRPRCs (Step 11).
Supervisory Authorities: To continuously monitor, audit, and regulate active Relying Parties.

Failure Path: If the Relying Party later violates ecosystem rules or GDPR mandates, the Registrar transitions the status to SUSPENDED or REVOKED, instantly paralyzing the Relying Party's ability to interact with Wallet Units.
Artifact Produced: The walletRelyingPartyId identifier structure.
Audit Telemetry: The Registrar logs REGISTRATION_ACTIVATED and propagates state changes to national notification services.

Record Retention (Art. 10): Registrars retain records of all Relying Party registration information—including all subsequent changes—for 10 years (CIR 2025/848 Art. 10). This retention serves ex post monitoring, law enforcement investigations, and dispute handling. Relying Parties must ensure their entire registration history remains auditable.
4. Relying Party requests WRPAC(s) from Access CA

With a confirmed registration, the Relying Party must acquire its cryptographic identity—the Wallet Relying Party Access Certificate (WRPAC). The Relying Party generates a secure Elliptic Curve key pair (typically P-256 or P-384) within its infrastructure (e.g., an HSM or secure enclave).

The Relying Party constructs a standard PKCS#10 Certificate Signing Request (CSR). Crucially, this CSR binds the Relying Party's technical endpoint to its legal EUDI identity by embedding the walletRelyingPartyId in the subject extension and specifying the Relying Party Instance's operational domain in the Subject Alternative Name (SAN).
Failure Path: If the CSR is malformed or uses an unsupported signature algorithm, the Access CA rejects the request and returns a 400 Bad Request.
Artifact Produced: Cryptographic Key Pair (Private Key secured in HSM/KMS).
Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a WRPAC_CSR_GENERATED SIEM event.

One WRPAC per Relying Party Instance: Each independent architectural component (e.g., the primary web backend, a distinct mobile API gateway, or distributed proximity terminals) requires its own WRPAC with isolated key material. While a single legal entity may hold dozens of WRPACs, the WRPAC's SAN domain must exactly match the client_id used in its OpenID4VP requests. This strict binding effectively neutralizes domain spoofing and phishing attacks.
5. Access CA queries Registrar to verify Relying Party registration

The Access Certificate Authority (Access CA) operates under strict issuance policies and validates certificate requests using national registries. Upon receiving the CSR, the Access CA acts as a systemic gatekeeper by actively querying the designated Registrar REST API (GET /wrp/{identifier}).
This verification step bridges the PKI trust layer (X.509 cryptography) and the regulatory trust layer (national legal registration). The Access CA validates three mandatory conditions:
Existence & Status: The Relying Party must exist in the national registry and hold an ACTIVE status.
Identifier Matching: The walletRelyingPartyId requested in the CSR's custom extension must perfectly match the identifier registered with the authority.
Standing: The Relying Party must not be flagged as suspended, revoked, or under investigation.

Failure Path: If any condition fails, the Access CA immediately aborts the issuance process and logs the denied request. This architectural safeguard guarantees that unregistered, suspended, or malicious entities cannot obtain the cryptographic WRPACs necessary to interface with EUDI Wallet Units.
Audit Telemetry: The Access CA generates a CA_REG_LOOKUP_DENIED SIEM alert.

CA-Registrar binding: This verification step is what connects the PKI trust layer (X.509 certificates) to the regulatory trust layer (national registration). A WRPAC without a backing Registrar entry is invalid by design.
6. Registrar confirms registration to Access CA

The Registrar responds to the exact same /wrp/{identifier} API call by returning the Relying Party's registered data points. The response is a JWS-signed JSON Web Token containing the data payload, confirming the entity's active status to the Access CA.
Artifact Produced: Registration Data Payload (JWS).
Audit Telemetry: The Access CA logs REGISTRATION_CONFIRMED_BY_REGISTRAR.
7. Access CA issues X.509 Access Certificate

The Access CA mints the X.509 Access Certificate. Crucially, the WRPAC binds the Relying Party's public key to its legal identity. It follows the ETSI TS 119 475 profile, embedding the walletRelyingPartyId and the memberState into the certificate's subject or extensions.
Artifact Produced: The X.509 Wallet Relying Party Access Certificate (WRPAC).
Audit Telemetry: The Access CA logs a WRPAC_CERT_ISSUED SIEM event.
8. Access CA delivers WRPAC to Relying Party
The Access CA cryptographically signs the X.509 Access Certificate and delivers the full certificate chain (Leaf → Intermediate CA → Root CA) back to the Relying Party.

The Relying Party must immediately secure the corresponding private key within a resilient, hardware-backed key store (e.g., an HSM or managed Cloud KMS) to prevent key exfiltration. This private cryptographic material acts as the ultimate anchor for the Relying Party's authenticity, and is utilized across two primarily integration vectors:

Online Authentication: To securely sign JSON-Secured Authorization Requests (JARs). The entire WRPAC chain is injected into the x5c header of the JWS payload, allowing remote Wallet Units to verify the signature.
Proximity Authentication: To sign readerAuth structures during offline BLE/NFC mdoc presentations. The WRPAC is embedded directly in the COSE_Sign1 unprotected header.

Because WRPACs carry high systemic risk if compromised, they possess a strictly limited validity period (typically 1–2 years). Relying Parties must engineer automated cryptographic rotation pipelines to renew WRPACs prior to expiration, as Wallet Units forcefully reject expired certificates at runtime.
9. Access CA logs WRPAC to Certificate Transparency

Simultaneous to issuance, the Access CA actively submits the newly minted WRPAC to one or more EUDI-compliant Certificate Transparency (CT) logs (RFC 9162). The CT log responds with a cryptographic Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT) asserting that the certificate is permanently recorded in an append-only ledger.
This architectural requirement aligns the EUDI PKI with modern Web PKI standards and provides three critical security properties:
Ecosystem-wide Auditability: Security researchers and Wallet Providers can continuously monitor public CT logs to detect rogue, mis-issued, or compromised WRPACs instantly.

Relying Party Accountability: The public nature of CT logs creates a permanent, non-repudiable record of exactly which legal entity holds which access keys, severely deterring unauthorized credential acquisition.
Wallet-Side Enforcement: Advanced Wallet Units are expected to parse the SCTs embedded within the WRPAC X.509 extensions to verify legitimate public logging before rendering user consent screens.
Failure Path: If the CT log fails to respond or returns an invalid SCT, the Access CA aborts certificate delivery.
Artifact Produced: Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT).
Audit Telemetry: The Access CA logs a WRPAC_CT_LOGGED SIEM event.

Zero-Trust Fallback: If a Wallet Unit encounters a WRPAC lacking valid SCTs, the standard behavior is an immediate protocol termination (Access Denied), preventing shadow-issued certificates from interrogating wallets.
10. Relying Party requests WRPRC from Registration Cert Provider

While the WRPAC governs cryptographic network access, it does not detail a Relying Party's entitlements. If supported by the Member State, the Relying Party proactively requests a Wallet Relying Party Registration Certificate (WRPRC) from an authorized Registration Cert Provider to bundle its authorized use-cases into a verifiable artifact.
There is a fundamental dichotomy between the two credentials:
WRPAC (The "Who"): An X.509 certificate mathematically proving the Relying Party's network identity and domain binding.
WRPRC (The "What"): A signed metadata artifact proving exactly which attributes the Relying Party is legally authorized to request, backed by its stated lawfulBasis.

The primary engineering value of the WRPRC is enabling offline verification. By injecting the WRPRC into the client_metadata of an OpenID4VP request or the readerAuth extension of an mdoc engagement, the Wallet Unit is shielded from having to perform synchronous network queries to the Registrar API during the presentation flow.
Artifact Produced: WRPRC Data Request Payload.
Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a WRPRC_REQUEST_INITIATED SIEM event.
11. Registration Cert Provider queries Registrar for Relying Party data

To construct the WRPRC accurately, the Registration Cert Provider must source the unadulterated ground truth regarding the Relying Party's entitlements. The Provider executes a direct query to the national Registrar's REST API, targeting the Relying Party's unique identifier.
This strict backend-to-backend integration guarantees that the WRPRC is minted using dynamically validated registration data rather than static user-supplied inputs, enforcing systemic integrity.
Failure Path: If the Relying Party's registration is suspended or not found, the Registrar returns a 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden, and the Provider aborts issuance.
Audit Telemetry: The Provider generates a RCP_REG_LOOKUP_DENIED SIEM alert if the lookup is unsuccessful.
12. Registrar returns registration data to Registration Cert Provider

The Registrar returns the exact same JWS-signed JWT containing the requested Relying Party's details. The Registration Cert Provider decodes this to extract the intendedAttributes array and supportURI to embed them directly into the certificate.
Artifact Produced: Registration Data Payload (JWS).
Audit Telemetry: The Registrar logs REGISTRATION_DATA_SERVED_TO_RCP.
13. Registration Cert Provider issues WRPRC
The Registration Cert Provider mints the WRPRC as a deeply structured, self-contained JSON Web Token (JWT) encapsulating the full spectrum of the Relying Party's operational privileges.
This token natively embeds critical user-facing transparency data:
intendedAttributes: Cryptographically limits the scope to the precise attributes (e.g., family_name, birth_date) and purposes the Relying Party was legally approved for.
supportURI: Guaranteed support URLs rendered on the Wallet UI if users encounter presentation faults or dispute data collection.
supervisoryAuthority: Renders exactly which national DPA or agency oversees the Relying Party's data handling practices.
srvDescription: Richly localized application descriptions dynamically rendered based on the Wallet Unit's active locale.
Artifact Produced: The Wallet Relying Party Registration Certificate (WRPRC JWT).
Audit Telemetry: The Provider logs WRPRC_ISSUED.

To restrict the window for malicious drift (where a Relying Party retains attributes after their lawful basis changes), the WRPRC enforces an ultra-short validity window (exp typically 3–6 months), forcing the Relying Party to repeatedly interface with the Provider to refresh its entitlements.
14. Registration Cert Provider delivers WRPRC to Relying Party

The finalized WRPRC (formatted as a signed JWT) is securely delivered back to the Relying Party. The Relying Party caches this artifact in its backend infrastructure, alongside its cryptographic WRPAC, integrating it into every outbound presentation pipeline.
Depending on the modality, the Relying Party transmits the WRPRC to Wallet Units via distinct architectural channels:
Online SD-JWT flows: Embedded natively within the client_metadata payload inside the authorization JAR.
Proximity mdoc flows: Encoded as a CBOR byte string and attached directly to the readerAuth COSE_Sign1 unprotected header during BLE/NFC engagement.

When the Wallet Unit processes the request, it verifies the Registration Cert Provider's issuer signature via its Trusted List. If valid, the Wallet dynamically reads the intendedAttributes from the WRPRC and renders them directly on the user's consent screen. This mechanism deliberately bypasses synchronous REST calls to the Registrar API, ensuring ultra-responsive, fully offline verification perfectly sculpted for subterranean transit gates or remote proximity venues without internet connectivity.

WRPRC Staleness Mitigation: Because the WRPRC represents a static, point-in-time snapshot, it rapidly ages out of sync with backend Registrar updates (e.g., the Relying Party legally registers a new address attribute). Relying Parties must engineer automated cron scripts to blindly refresh their WRPRC monthly, guaranteeing Wallet UI alignment with their current legal reality.
CIR 2025/848 Art. 9 establishes three distinct pathways through which an RP's registration may be suspended or cancelled:

Supervisory body request (Art. 9(1)) — A supervisory body exercising its powers under Art. 46a(4)(f) of Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 may direct the Registrar to suspend or cancel the registration. This is a mandatory instruction — the Registrar shall comply.

Supervisory body request (Art. 9(1)) — A supervisory body exercising its powers under Art. 46a(4)(f) of Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 may direct the Registrar to suspend or cancel the registration. This is a mandatory instruction — the Registrar shall comply.

Registrar-initiated (Art. 9(2)) — The Registrar may suspend or cancel a registration where it has reason to believe any of the following: (a) The registration contains information that is inaccurate, out of date, or misleading (b) The RP is not compliant with the registration policy (c) The RP is requesting more attributes than it registered under Art. 5 and Art. 6 — this is the data minimisation enforcement lever (see §9.1 for over-requesting risks) (d) The RP is otherwise acting in breach of Union or national law in a manner related to its role as a wallet-relying party
Registrar-initiated (Art. 9(2)) — The Registrar may suspend or cancel a registration where it has reason to believe any of the following:
(a) The registration contains information that is inaccurate, out of date, or misleading
(b) The RP is not compliant with the registration policy
(c) The RP is requesting more attributes than it registered under Art. 5 and Art. 6 — this is the data minimisation enforcement lever (see §9.1 for over-requesting risks)
(d) The RP is otherwise acting in breach of Union or national law in a manner related to its role as a wallet-relying party
RP self-request (Art. 9(3)) — The RP itself requests suspension or cancellation (e.g., when discontinuing a wallet-integrated service). The Registrar shall comply.
RP self-request (Art. 9(3)) — The RP itself requests suspension or cancellation (e.g., when discontinuing a wallet-integrated service). The Registrar shall comply.
Before acting on registrar-initiated grounds (pathway 2), the Registrar must conduct a proportionality assessment (Art. 9(4)), weighing:
Impact on users' fundamental rights, security, and confidentiality
Severity of the disruption the suspension/cancellation would cause
Associated costs for both the RP and its users

Based on this assessment, the Registrar may suspend or cancel with or without prior notice. This means an RP may find its registration suspended without warning if the breach is severe enough to justify immediate action.
When a registration is suspended or cancelled, Art. 9(5) mandates a strict notification timeline — the Registrar must inform all three parties within 24 hours:
Provider of WRPAC (Access CA) — to trigger certificate revocation
Provider of WRPRC (Registration Cert Provider) — to trigger certificate revocation
The affected RP — including the reasons and available means of redress/appeal

Art. 9(6) mandates that upon suspension or cancellation, both the Access CA and Registration Cert Provider must revoke without undue delay all WRPACs and WRPRCs respectively belonging to the affected RP. This effectively paralyses the RP's ability to interact with any Wallet Unit — neither online (WRPAC) nor offline (WRPRC) verification will succeed.

⚠️ RP Risk — Over-Requesting: Art. 9(2)(c) explicitly makes requesting attributes beyond the registered scope a ground for suspension. This directly connects the data minimisation obligations in §9.1 to a concrete enforcement consequence. RPs must ensure their OpenID4VP/DCQL queries never exceed their registered intendedAttributes, or they face suspension and loss of all wallet access.

TS5 defines a public REST API (OpenAPI 3.1) enabling any party — including Wallet Units, RPs, and the general public — to query the national register of Wallet-Relying Parties. The API is critical for the RP integration flow because:
Wallet Units query it to verify RP registration and intended attributes when no WRPRC is available
RPs query it to verify their own registration status and that of intermediaries
Supervisory bodies use it for monitoring and enforcement
Query registered WRPs by parameters
Retrieve single WRP by identifier
Boolean check if intended use matches
Update existing WRP registration

Key design decision: Write methods (POST, PUT, DELETE) use Member State–specific authentication and authorisation mechanisms. The harmonisation of the POST method has been left for further study — meaning each Member State may implement a different registration application process.
The GET /wrp endpoint supports the following query parameters:
Official or business registration number
URL of the WRP's privacy policy
Type of entitlement (e.g., PuB-EAA Provider)
Type of attestation provided
Whether WRP uses an intermediary
Whether WRP acts as an intermediary
Identifier for a specific intended use
Claim path within an intended use
Credential metadata for intended use
Credential format (e.g., dc+sd-jwt)

All GET responses are JWS-signed by the Registrar. A successful GET /wrp response returns an array of matching WalletRelyingParty objects plus Certificate Transparency log information for each entity's Access Certificates (per RFC 9162).

The dedicated GET /wrp/check-intended-use endpoint returns a JWS-signed boolean TRUE or FALSE, enabling Wallet Units to perform a lightweight check during presentation without downloading the full registration data.
When a Wallet Unit receives a presentation request from an RP Instance that does not include a WRPRC, the Wallet Unit uses the Registrar API to verify the RP's intended attributes:
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 1. Relying Party sends presentation request to Wallet Unit
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party Instance initiates the presentation flow by transmitting a request to the user's Wallet Unit. Depending on the architectural modality, this is either an OpenID4VP JSON-secured Authorization Request (JAR) traversing the internet, or an ISO/IEC 18013-5 DeviceRequest transmitted over BLE/NFC.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: To cryptographically assert its identity, the Relying Party embeds its Wallet Relying Party Access Certificate (WRPAC).
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: In this specific scenario, the Relying Party omits the Wallet Relying Party Registration Certificate (WRPRC). This omission might occur because the Relying Party's Member State has not deployed a Registration Cert Provider infrastructure, or the architect chose to simplify their offline credential management. Because the WRPRC is missing, the Wallet Unit lacks localized proof of the Relying Party's entitlements and is forced into an online execution path (Steps 3–4) to query the national Registrar.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs an INBOUND_PRESENTATION_REQUEST_RECEIVED event.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Offline Bypass: If the Relying Party had embedded a WRPRC, the Wallet Unit could parse the intendedAttributes directly from the certificate payload, entirely bypassing the synchronous REST API network hops to the Registrar and skipping straight to user consent.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 2. Wallet Unit authenticates Relying Party Instance via WRPAC
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Upon receiving the presentation request, the Wallet Unit enters a strict cryptographic verification phase. It must unequivocally prove that the Relying Party operating the endpoint (client_id) legally owns the private key that signed the request.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit extracts the WRPAC leaf certificate from the request (e.g., from the JAR's x5c header or the mdoc readerAuth structure) and executes the following path validation:
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Chain Building: The Wallet Unit constructs the certification path from the WRPAC leaf, through the Access CA intermediate, terminating at a trusted Root CA listed in the national List of Trusted Entities (LoTE).
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Signature Verification: The Wallet Unit mathematically verifies the ECDSA signatures across the entire chain.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Status Check: The Wallet Unit verifies the certificate's validity period and queries its revocation status (via OCSP stapling, CRLs, or cached offline lists).
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Identity Binding: The Wallet Unit extracts the deeply embedded walletRelyingPartyId or client_id from the WRPAC's Subject Alternative Name (SAN) extension.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: This procedure authenticates who the Relying Party is, but crucially, it does not validate what they are legally permitted to request. The WRPAC proves the Relying Party is a registered entity capable of holding keys, but querying the specific attributes and lawful purposes requires live registration verification.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If any dimension of the WRPAC validation fails (chain broken, expired, revoked, or untrusted Root), the Wallet MUST categorically drop the presentation request.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit triggers a WRPAC_VALIDATION_FAILED security event upon rejection.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Hard Protocol Termination: An unauthenticated Relying Party represents a severe systemic threat, irrespective of the attributes it is attempting to access.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 3. Wallet Unit queries Registrar API for intended use
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit constructs an HTTP GET request to the Registrar's check-intended-use endpoint — the lightweight boolean check designed specifically for real-time Wallet queries during presentation (EUDI TS5 OpenAPI 3.1). The query parameters map directly from the Relying Party's presentation request:
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: From the presentation request's DCQL query
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Each requested attribute name
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: For multi-attribute requests, the Wallet Unit should either issue one query per requested claim or batch them if the Registrar supports multi-claim queries (implementation-dependent). The Registrar endpoint must be reachable over TLS 1.3, and the Wallet Unit should verify the Registrar's TLS certificate chain. See §3.4.5 for the full endpoint specification and response schema.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a REGISTRAR_INTENDED_USE_QUERY_INITIATED event.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Offline fallback: If the Registrar is unreachable (no internet, DNS failure, timeout), the Wallet Unit should inform the User: "Registration check unavailable — proceed at your own risk", and allow the User to decide. The Wallet Unit should NOT silently proceed without the check.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 4. Registrar API returns signed intended use verification
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Registrar API returns a 200 OK response with Content-Type: application/jwt — a JWS compact serialisation signed with the Registrar's private key. The x-jku-url response header points to the Registrar's JWKS for signature verification. The JWS payload conforms to the SignedIntendedUseCheckResult schema:
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit MUST verify the JWS signature before trusting the response — an unsigned or tampered response could lead to the Wallet Unit incorrectly approving an unregistered request. The Wallet Unit fetches the Registrar's JWKS from the x-jku-url header (or from a cached copy) and verifies the signature using the matching kid.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: When the Relying Party requests an attribute it is not registered for, the response indicates false:
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Registrar Verification Payload (JWS).
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the JWS signature verification fails, the Wallet Unit rejects the payload as tampered and halts the presentation flow.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a REGISTRAR_RESPONSE_VERIFIED_SUCCESS or REGISTRAR_RESPONSE_VERIFICATION_FAILED event.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: JWS signing rationale: The Registrar signs every response to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks where a network adversary could replace a false response with true, tricking the User into sharing data with an unregistered Relying Party.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 5. Wallet Unit displays verified Relying Party attributes to User
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If the Registrar API responds with isRegistered: true, the Wallet Unit's UI transitions to the presentation consent screen, heavily augmenting the display with positive trust indicators. This UI signifies that the Relying Party has successfully navigated the regulatory gauntlet and is explicitly authorized to access the requested attributes.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet UI dynamically maps the validated data to the screen:
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Verified Identity: The Relying Party's tradeName (e.g., "Example Bank AG") is rendered prominently, pulled from the WRPAC SAN or the cached Registrar profile.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Trust Seal: A highly visible, standardized trust mark (e.g., ✅) accompanied by ecosystem-approved nomenclature like "This service is officially registered and monitored by your national authority."
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Granular Transparency: Each requested attribute (e.g., family_name, birth_date) is listed alongside the Relying Party's registered purpose (e.g., "For anti-money laundering compliance (KYC)").
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: This affirmative signal serves as the primary psychological anchor for the User, assuring them that the interaction is shielded by eIDAS 2.0 regulatory oversight, thereby dramatically increasing conversion rates during complex onboarding flows.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 6. Wallet Unit displays severity warning for unmatched attributes to User
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If the Registrar API responds with isRegistered: false—indicating a mismatch between the attributes requested in the JAR/DeviceRequest and the attributes approved in the national registry—the Wallet Unit MUST instantly interrupt the standard UX flow and render a severe warning.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: This architectural tripwire is the fundamental defense mechanism against Relying Parties attempting to over-request data outside their legal purview (e.g., a bank quietly asking for health indicators).
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet UI executes a "friction-heavy" intervention:
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Visual Alert: The interface shifts to an amber or red warning state (⚠️).
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Explicit Callout: "This service is NOT authorized to request the highlighted data (e.g., health_status)."
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Regulatory Context: A "Learn More" hyperlink driving the User directly to the national Registrar's public portal, allowing the User to inspect the Relying Party's actual, limited registration record.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Crucially, under the sovereignty principles of eIDAS 2.0 (Art. 5a(4)), this warning is strictly informational, not blocking. The Wallet Unit cannot unilaterally forbid the User from transmitting their own data. The User retains ultimate agency to override the warning and force the presentation, but the UI guarantees they do so fully aware of the Relying Party's non-compliance.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs an UNMATCHED_ATTRIBUTE_WARNING_DISPLAYED event.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 7. User authorizes or aborts credential presentation
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Positioned as the definitive gatekeeper, the User absorbs the UI trust indicators (✅ or ⚠️) and exercises their sovereign right to authorize or abort the transaction. The European digital identity paradigm dictates that cryptographic transmission cannot occur without this explicit, granular consent gate.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit translates the User's choice into protocol-level actions:
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If Approved (Full or Partial): The Wallet Unit synthesizes the finalized payload. For an OpenID4VP flow, it constructs the SD-JWT VP Token, signs the Key Binding JWT (KB-JWT) over the Relying Party's nonce, and POSTs the payload to the Relying Party's response_uri. If the User exercised selective disclosure (denying specific attributes like age while approving name), the Wallet Unit strips the corresponding disclosure strings from the presentation.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If Denied: The Wallet Unit halts cryptographic generation. In online domains, it explicitly signals the rejection by dispatching an OAuth 2.0 access_denied error code to the Relying Party's backend, allowing the Relying Party to gracefully update its web session. In proximity environments, the Wallet Unit silently drops the BLE/NFC connection.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Verifiable Presentation Payload (e.g., SD-JWT VP Token or mdoc readerAuth).
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a USER_CONSENT_DECISION_RECORDED event.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Risk of Partial Consent: Wallet Units increasingly support granular, per-attribute toggles. Relying Parties must engineer their backend verifier logic to smoothly handle presentations that arrive with missing, un-consented attributes. If a Relying Party rigidly terminates the session when a non-critical attribute is missing, it creates a brittle, frustrating UX that undermines the ecosystem's data-minimization goals.
passages.headingText: 4.4.4 Wallet Unit to Registrar API Interaction (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The following examples are derived from the official TS5 OpenAPI 3.1 specification (ts5-openapi31-registrar-api.yml). All GET responses are returned as application/jwt (JWS compact serialisation) with an x-jku-url header pointing to the Registrar's JWKS.
GET /wrp?identifier={id} — Query registered WRPs by identifier
Response (200 OK, Content-Type: application/jwt, x-jku-url: https://registrar.example-ms.de/.well-known/jwks.json):
The JWS payload, once decoded and signature-verified against the Registrar's JWKS, conforms to the SignedWRPArray schema:
GET /wrp/{identifier} — Retrieve a single WRP by identifier
Response (200 OK): Same as the GET /wrp response, but the JWS payload conforms to SignedWRP (single object, not array):
404 Not Found if the identifier does not match any registered WRP.
GET /wrp/check-intended-use — Lightweight boolean check (used by Wallet Units)

This is the most critical endpoint for real-time RP verification during presentation. The Wallet Unit calls it to determine whether the RP is registered for the specific credential and claim it is requesting.
Response (200 OK): JWS payload conforming to SignedIntendedUseCheckResult:
When the RP requests an attribute it is not registered for:
PUT /wrp — Update existing WRP registration (authenticated)
Response (200 OK): The updated WRP as a JWS-signed SignedWRP object. 400 if the payload is invalid, 404 if the WRP does not exist.
Note: The POST method for creating new registrations has been parked for further study by request of DE. Each Member State may implement a different registration application process.
DELETE /wrp/{identifier} — Delete a WRP registration (authenticated)
Response: 204 No Content on success, 404 Not Found if the identifier does not match.
TS5 mandates the following protections for the Registrar API:
DDoS protection: Cloud-based DDoS protection, WAF, rate limiting
Rate limiting: Per-IP rate limiting (e.g., 3 calls/min — specific limits are MS-defined)
Query complexity limits: Maximum number of query parameters per call
Caching: Aggressive caching for GET responses (registration data changes infrequently)
Network segmentation: API servers in private subnet behind WAF, isolated from the Registrar database
Monitoring: Request rates, latency, error rates, source IP alerting

The EUDI Wallet trust infrastructure is built on a hierarchical certificate model that enables Wallet Units to verify the identity and authorisation of every entity they interact with. For the RP, this hierarchy determines how the RP authenticates itself to Wallet Units and how the RP verifies the credentials it receives.
When a Wallet Unit processes a presentation request from an RP Instance, it performs the following certificate chain validation:
Extracts the WRPAC and any intermediate certificates from the presentation request
Verifies the signature over the request using the public key in the WRPAC
Validates the certificate chain up to the trust anchor obtained from the LoTE
Checks revocation status for every certificate in the chain (including the trust anchor if applicable)
If all checks pass, the RP Instance is authenticated
When the RP receives a presentation response, it performs its own trust chain validation:
For non-qualified EAAs, the applicable Rulebook specifies how the RP obtains the relevant trust anchor.

The Wallet-Relying Party Access Certificate (WRPAC) is an X.509 certificate that enables an RP Instance to authenticate itself to a Wallet Unit during a presentation request. It is the primary credential that the RP uses in every interaction with a Wallet Unit.
One WRPAC per RP Instance — each technical system (web service, mobile app, terminal) gets its own certificate
Legal subject is the RP — all WRPACs for a single RP share the same legal entity identity
Issued by an Access CA authorised by the Member State
Trust anchors published in LoTE — enabling cross-border validation by any Wallet Unit in the EU
Logged in Certificate Transparency (RFC 9162) — enabling detection of erroneous or fraudulent issuance
ETSI TS 119 475 (draft) defines the detailed certificate profile
Based on ETSI TS 119 475, the WRPAC follows the X.509v3 format with EUDI-specific extensions:
Subject.organizationIdentifier
Access Certificate Authority
Revocation checking endpoint
http://crl.access-ca.de/wrpac.crl
Signed Certificate Timestamp

Critical for intermediaries: When an intermediary acts on behalf of an intermediated RP, the WRPAC belongs to the intermediary. The intermediated RP's identity is conveyed in the presentation request extension and the WRPRC (if available), not in the WRPAC.

In OpenID4VP (remote flows): The RP Instance includes the WRPAC as part of the Signed Authorization Request (JAR). Client identification uses x509_hash or x509_san_dns — the Wallet Unit can verify the certificate chain from the JWT header.

In ISO/IEC 18013-5 (proximity flows): The RP Instance (mdoc reader) includes the WRPAC in the ReaderAuth structure within the DeviceRequest. The mdoc verifies the signature over the ReaderAuthentication CBOR structure using the public key in the WRPAC.
CIR 2025/848 Annex IV point 3(k) prescribes three categories of information that a WRPAC must contain:

Certification path information: The location where the certificate supporting the electronic signature or seal is available, enabling the full certification path to be built to the trust anchor in the PKI hierarchy used by the Access CA.

Certification path information: The location where the certificate supporting the electronic signature or seal is available, enabling the full certification path to be built to the trust anchor in the PKI hierarchy used by the Access CA.
Policy references: A machine-processable reference to the applicable certificate policy and certificate practice statement.
Policy references: A machine-processable reference to the applicable certificate policy and certificate practice statement.

Registration information (referencing Annex I points 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, and 7(a)–(c)): Legal name of the Wallet-Relying Party as recorded in an official register User-friendly trade name One or more identifiers of the WRP (as specified in Annex I) A URL belonging to the WRP The applicable country indicator prefix rule Contact information: at least one of website/helpdesk, telephone, or email
Registration information (referencing Annex I points 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, and 7(a)–(c)):
Legal name of the Wallet-Relying Party as recorded in an official register
One or more identifiers of the WRP (as specified in Annex I)
The applicable country indicator prefix rule
Contact information: at least one of website/helpdesk, telephone, or email

This registration information is a subset of the full registration data model (§4.2) — the WRPAC embeds the minimum identifying information needed for the Wallet Unit to display meaningful RP identity to the User during consent.

Pending amendment: A proposed revision of CIR 2025/848 adds a requirement that access certificates issued to intermediaries must also include an association to the Wallet-Relying Party on whose behalf the intermediary acts. This would make the intermediary–principal relationship verifiable from the certificate itself, rather than requiring an online Registrar query or WRPRC. RPs using intermediary architectures should monitor this amendment.
Large organisations with subsidiaries, brands, or service units require a structured certificate model that maps to their registration structure (§4.2):
Each subsidiary or brand that independently interacts with Wallet Units registers as a separate Wallet-Relying Party (or as a separate RP Instance under a parent registration)
Each RP Instance receives its own WRPAC from the Access CA — one certificate per technical system endpoint
All WRPACs for the same legal entity share the same Subject.organizationIdentifier but differ in SubjectAltName.dNSName

User transparency: When a user applies for a service, the Wallet displays the specific subsidiary or brand name from the WRPAC, not the parent group name. This ensures Art. 5b(1)(b) consent granularity

Example: A banking group with retail, mortgage, and investment subsidiaries would register three WRPs, each with its own WRPAC. A mortgage application triggers a wallet consent screen showing "Example Bank Mortgage Services B.V." — not "Example Banking Group."

CIR 2025/848 Annex IV §3(j) requires Access Certificate Authorities to describe how they log WRPACs in Certificate Transparency (CT) logs, in compliance with IETF RFC 9162 (Certificate Transparency Version 2.0). ARF v2.7.0 (incorporating Discussion Paper Topic S) establishes High-Level Requirements that formalise this obligation and establish the Wallet Unit's verification behaviour.

CT logs are immutable, append-only ledgers structured as Merkle trees. They make certificate issuance publicly auditable, enabling detection of certificates that were issued in error or fraudulently. Access CAs submit WRPACs to CT logs, which return a Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT) — a cryptographic proof that the certificate has been publicly recorded.
Access CA SHALL register WRPACs in CT log per RFC 9162, if such a log is available for access certificates.
RP's WRPAC will appear in public CT logs — this is an accountability feature, not a privacy concern (RP registration data is already public).
Access CA SHALL describe in its Certificate Practice Statement (CPS) how it logs all access certificates.
RP should verify that its Access CA's CPS includes a CT logging commitment before selecting a CA.
All Access CAs SHALL act as monitors in the CT ecosystem. Access CAs SHOULD still monitor during temporary unavailability.
Ecosystem-level fraud detection; no direct RP action required.
Access CA SHALL include at least one SCT in each WRPAC.
RP's WRPAC MUST contain ≥1 SCT in the CT Precertificate SCT extension (see §5.2.2 WRPAC structure table).
Wallet Unit SHALL verify that the WRPAC includes at least one valid SCT during PID or attestation presentation.
If the RP's WRPAC lacks a valid SCT, the Wallet Unit will reject the RP's presentation request. This is a hard failure with no fallback.
Missing valid SCT = RP authentication failure, per all requirements in Topic 6 (Relying Party authentication) and in particular requirement RPI_06a.
The RP cannot present to any Wallet Unit until its WRPAC includes a valid SCT.

Verify your WRPAC contains a valid SCT. After receiving your WRPAC from the Access CA, inspect the certificate to confirm the CT Precertificate Signed Certificate Timestamps extension is present. For example, using OpenSSL: openssl x509 -in wrpac.pem -text -noout | grep -A5 "CT Precertificate SCTs" If the SCT extension is absent, do not deploy the certificate — request re-issuance from the Access CA.

Verify your WRPAC contains a valid SCT. After receiving your WRPAC from the Access CA, inspect the certificate to confirm the CT Precertificate Signed Certificate Timestamps extension is present. For example, using OpenSSL:
If the SCT extension is absent, do not deploy the certificate — request re-issuance from the Access CA.

Monitor CT logs for your RP identity. Subscribe to a CT monitoring service to detect unauthorised WRPACs issued to your RP identifier (Subject.organizationIdentifier, SubjectAltName.dNSName). An unauthorised WRPAC means another entity could impersonate your RP to Wallet Units. Monitoring services include: crt.sh (Sectigo) — free, web-searchable CT log aggregator Certspotter (SSLMate) — automated certificate monitoring Future EU CT monitoring services (if established by the Commission)

Monitor CT logs for your RP identity. Subscribe to a CT monitoring service to detect unauthorised WRPACs issued to your RP identifier (Subject.organizationIdentifier, SubjectAltName.dNSName). An unauthorised WRPAC means another entity could impersonate your RP to Wallet Units. Monitoring services include:
crt.sh (Sectigo) — free, web-searchable CT log aggregator
Certspotter (SSLMate) — automated certificate monitoring
Future EU CT monitoring services (if established by the Commission)

Incident response for rogue certificates. If you discover an entry in a CT log for a WRPAC that was incorrectly issued to your identity: Immediately request revocation from the issuing Access CA If revocation is not executed promptly per the CA's CPS, escalate to the service operator of the national Trusted List or the Member State authority Only the RP to which an access certificate has been incorrectly issued can initiate this process
Incident response for rogue certificates. If you discover an entry in a CT log for a WRPAC that was incorrectly issued to your identity:
Immediately request revocation from the issuing Access CA
If revocation is not executed promptly per the CA's CPS, escalate to the service operator of the national Trusted List or the Member State authority
Only the RP to which an access certificate has been incorrectly issued can initiate this process

Wallet Units SHALL NOT contact CT logs directly to verify certificate inclusion. This is a deliberate privacy design: if a Wallet queried a CT log for every presentation, the log operator would learn which RPs the User interacts with — the same privacy concern that applies to web browsers.
Instead, the Wallet's verification is limited to:
Extract the SCT(s) from the WRPAC's certificate extension
Verify the SCT signature(s) against the CT log's public key
Confirm the SCT timestamp is consistent with the certificate's validity
This model is identical to how web browsers handle CT for TLS certificates.
No EU-operated CT log infrastructure exists yet for access certificates. Existing CT log providers (Google, Cloudflare) operate TLS-focused logs. Key unresolved questions:
passages.headingText: Open Issue: EU CT Log Infrastructure

passages.text: Should existing CT log providers accept EUDI access certificates, or should the Commission establish a dedicated European CT log?
passages.headingText: Open Issue: EU CT Log Infrastructure
