passages.text: Which standard version should be used — RFC 9162 (V2.0, referenced in CIR 2025/848) or RFC 6962 (V1.0, widely implemented)?
passages.headingText: Open Issue: EU CT Log Infrastructure

passages.text: How many independent CT logs must each WRPAC be registered in? (Web PKI best practice: at least 2)
passages.headingText: Open Issue: EU CT Log Infrastructure

passages.text: These questions are tracked as Open Question #15 in §36.
passages.headingText: Open Issue: EU CT Log Infrastructure

passages.text: Cross-references: §5.2.2 (WRPAC structure — SCT row), §31.2 (alert triggers — WRPAC SCT and rogue certificate alerts), §29.2 (threat catalogue — WRPAC Private Key Compromise).
passages.headingText: Open Issue: EU CT Log Infrastructure

passages.text: The Wallet-Relying Party Registration Certificate (WRPRC) is an optional certificate that embeds the RP's registration data in a signed, portable format. While the WRPAC proves the RP's identity, the WRPRC proves its authorisation — specifically, which attributes it registered to request and for what purposes.
Optional per Member State — CIR 2025/848 Art. 8 states Member States may authorise CAs to issue WRPRCs
Contains registration data — intended attributes, purposes, support URI, supervisory authority
Multiple WRPRCs per RP — one per intended use (service/purpose)
Enables offline verification — Wallet Units can verify the RP's registered scope without querying the Registrar API
ETSI TS 119 411-8 (draft) defines the detailed format
Issued by a Provider of Registration Certificates — separate from the Access CA
Always available (offline-capable)
Requires internet connection
Point-in-time snapshot; must be revoked if policy changes
Cryptographic signature verification
One certificate per intended use
No Registrar contacted → no metadata leakage
Registrar sees query → potential tracking
Optional; not all MS will issue
Mandatory; all MS must provide API
When both are available, the Wallet Unit prioritises the WRPRC for offline verification efficiency.
Based on TS6 (Common Set of RP Information) and CIR 2025/848 requirements, the WRPRC contains at minimum:
RP's user-friendly name and unique identifier
Intended use description (human-readable)
Attributes the RP is authorised to request for this intended use
URL to the RP's privacy policy
supportURI — contact endpoints for data deletion requests (website, email, phone)
supervisoryAuthority — DPA contact information (name, URI, email)
Registrar identifier and URL

Data Exchange Format (Annex V §5): CIR 2025/848 Annex V §5 mandates that the WRPRC uses signed JSON Web Tokens (JWT, IETF RFC 7519) and CBOR Web Tokens (CWT, IETF RFC 8392) as its data exchange format. This is a critical architectural distinction from the WRPAC, which is an X.509 certificate: the WRPRC is a signed token, not an X.509 artifact. RP developers must implement JWT parsing (for online SD-JWT flows) and CWT/CBOR parsing (for proximity mdoc flows) to process WRPRCs received from Wallet Units.

The Wallet Unit Attestation (WUA) is a signed information object issued by the Wallet Provider to the Wallet Unit during activation. However, there is a critical design decision in the ARF that RPs must understand:

WUAs are NOT presented to Relying Parties. They are presented only to PID Providers and Attestation Providers during issuance. This is a deliberate privacy-preserving design: RPs do not have a business need to know the WSCA/WSCD properties of a Wallet Unit, and exposing WUAs to RPs would enable linkability.
This means that an RP cannot directly verify whether a Wallet Unit has been revoked by checking a WUA. Instead, the RP relies on two indirect mechanisms:

PID credential validity: PID Providers are legally required (CIR 2024/2977, Art. 5.4(b)) to revoke a PID when the Wallet Unit is revoked. If the RP verifies the PID is not revoked (via Status List), it can trust the Wallet Unit is valid.
Attestation validity: Attestation Providers may similarly revoke attestations when a Wallet Unit is revoked.
1. Wallet Unit delivers presentation response to Relying Party Instance
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit delivers the presentation response to the Relying Party, containing the requested Person Identification Data (PID) and any accompanying attestations (e.g., QEAAs, PuB-EAAs). The response format depends on the presentation channel:
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Online (OpenID4VP): The response arrives as an encrypted JWE (direct_post.jwt) containing a vp_token field. For SD-JWT VC format, the vp_token holds a tilde-delimited SD-JWT presentation (Issuer-JWTDisclosuresKB-JWT). For mdoc format, it holds a CBOR-encoded DeviceResponse. See §9.2 step 19 (same-device) or §10.2 step 22 (cross-device) for the full decryption flow.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Proximity (ISO 18013-5): The response arrives as an AES-GCM encrypted DeviceResponse over BLE/NFC, containing IssuerSigned + DeviceSigned CBOR structures. See §13.4 step 16 for the supervised flow or §13.10 step 11 for the unsupervised flow.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: This step is the entry point for the Relying Party's verification pipeline — everything from step 2 onward must succeed for the Relying Party to trust the presented attributes. The verification order is important: issuer signature first (step 2), then revocation (step 3), then device binding (step 5), to ensure each layer validates before building on the next.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Inbound Presentation Response (Encrypted JWE or CBOR).
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs an INBOUND_PRESENTATION_RESPONSE_RECEIVED event.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 2. Relying Party Instance verifies PID issuer signature
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party cryptographically verifies the PID's issuer signature to confirm it was legitimately issued by a notified PID Provider. The verification method depends on the credential format:
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: SD-JWT VC: Parse the Issuer-JWT header, extract the x5c certificate chain (or resolve the kid via the issuer's JWKS endpoint). Verify the ES256 (P-256 ECDSA) signature over the JWT payload. The leaf certificate must chain to the PID Provider's trust anchor obtained from the national LoTE (§5.5.3).
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: mdoc: Extract the issuerAuth COSE_Sign1 from the MSO. Verify the signature using the issuer's X.509 certificate from the x5chain unprotected header. Validate the certificate chain against the LoTE trust anchor for the PID Provider. Check the MSO validityInfo (validFrom, validUntil).
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: In both formats, the Relying Party must resolve the correct LoTE by identifying the PID Provider's Member State (from the certificate's issuer field or the JWT iss claim) and fetching the corresponding LoTE entry. For cross-border presentations, the Relying Party may need to consult multiple Member State LoTEs. See §18.5.7 step 3 for the per-credential verification detail including certificate chain building.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If signature verification fails, the Relying Party MUST reject the entire presentation. A forged or tampered PID cannot be trusted, and the cascading verification steps (revocation, device binding) are meaningless without a valid issuer signature.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Decoded PID Payload.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party triggers a PID_ISSUER_SIGNATURE_INVALID or PID_ISSUER_SIGNATURE_VALID analytic event.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Why drop immediately: An invalid signature implies either malicious tampering or a severe misconfiguration on the issuer side. The Relying Party must halt to prevent ingesting poisoned data.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 3. Relying Party Instance checks PID revocation status
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party queries the PID Provider's Token Status List endpoint (IETF draft-ietf-oauth-status-list) to ensure the presented PID has not been suspended or revoked. The Relying Party extracts the status claim from the PID credential:
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party fetches the Status List Token JWT from the uri, decompresses the DEFLATE-compressed bitstring, and checks the bit at index idx. A value of 0 means VALID; 1 means REVOKED/SUSPENDED. See §B.3.1 for the complete Status List verification procedure with decompression and bit extraction detail.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the token status returns 1 (REVOKED), the Relying Party halts the verification pipeline and rejects the presentation.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a PID_STATUS_CHECK_INITIATED event, capturing the target URI.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: PID revocation as Wallet Unit health signal: This check is more significant than a simple credential validity check — see step 4 for why.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 4. Status List confirms PID validity to Relying Party Instance
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Status List confirms the PID's status is VALID (bit = 0). This carries a deeper trust implication than typical credential validity: PID Providers are legally obligated (CIR 2024/2977 Art. 5.4(b)) to cascade-revoke PIDs when the underlying Wallet Unit Attestation (WUA) is revoked or the Wallet Unit is compromised. This means a valid PID serves as indirect cryptographic proof that the Wallet Unit itself remains trustworthy. The Relying Party can therefore trust that:
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit's WUA has not been revoked by its Wallet Provider
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The PID Provider has not detected any compromise of the User's device
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The WSCA/WSCD binding is still intact (the PID Provider would revoke if notified of key compromise)
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: This is why the Relying Party does not need to verify the WUA or WIA directly — the PID acts as a trust proxy for the entire Wallet Unit health chain.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Status List Reference Token (JWT).
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a PID_STATUS_LIST_VERIFIED_VALID event.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Revocation propagation delay: There is a window between WUA revocation and PID cascading revocation, bounded by the PID Provider's status list refresh interval (typically 15 minutes to 1 hour). For ultra-high-assurance scenarios (e.g., qualified electronic signature creation), Relying Parties should perform additional checks beyond PID status — see §15 (SCA attestations) for complementary verification.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 5. Relying Party Instance verifies device binding
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party verifies the presenter's proof of possession — cryptographic evidence that the credential was presented by the device that originally received it from the PID Provider. This prevents credential cloning, forwarding, and replay attacks. The verification method depends on the format:
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: SD-JWT VC (KB-JWT): The Relying Party verifies the Key Binding JWT appended after the final ~ delimiter. It checks: signature — verify ES256 signature using the cnf.jwk public key from the Issuer-JWT payload aud — must match the Relying Party's client_id (typically the SAN or x5t#S256 from the WRPAC) nonce — must match the nonce from the original presentation request (anti-replay) sd_hash — must match SHA-256(base64url(Issuer-JWT~Disclosure1~...~DisclosureN)) (binds the KB-JWT to the specific selective disclosure set) iat — issued-at timestamp must be recent (within a configurable window, typically ≤ 300 seconds)
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: SD-JWT VC (KB-JWT): The Relying Party verifies the Key Binding JWT appended after the final ~ delimiter. It checks:
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: signature — verify ES256 signature using the cnf.jwk public key from the Issuer-JWT payload
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: aud — must match the Relying Party's client_id (typically the SAN or x5t#S256 from the WRPAC)
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: nonce — must match the nonce from the original presentation request (anti-replay)
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: sd_hash — must match SHA-256(base64url(Issuer-JWT~Disclosure1~...~DisclosureN)) (binds the KB-JWT to the specific selective disclosure set)
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: iat — issued-at timestamp must be recent (within a configurable window, typically ≤ 300 seconds)
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: mdoc (DeviceAuth): The Relying Party verifies the deviceSignature COSE_Sign1 over the DeviceAuthentication CBOR structure, which includes the SessionTranscript. See §13.10 step 13 for the detailed verification process.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: mdoc (DeviceAuth): The Relying Party verifies the deviceSignature COSE_Sign1 over the DeviceAuthentication CBOR structure, which includes the SessionTranscript. See §13.10 step 13 for the detailed verification process.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If device binding fails, the Relying Party MUST instantly reject the presentation. Failure indicates credential forwarding, cloning, or a relay attack.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a DEVICE_BINDING_VERIFICATION_FAILED security alert on failure, or DEVICE_BINDING_VERIFIED_SUCCESS if the proof of possession holds.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Criticality of Device Binding: This is the most critical security check — it proves that the credential holder is the exact same cryptographic entity that received the credential from the issuer during activation.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 6. Relying Party Instance verifies attestation signatures & revocation
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If additional attestations were presented alongside the PID (e.g., a QEAA for professional qualifications, a PuB-EAA for address data, or an mDL), the Relying Party processes each one through the same verification pipeline (steps 2–5). For each attestation:
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Issuer signature — verify against the appropriate LoTE trust anchor (QEAA → QEAA Trusted List; PuB-EAA → QTSP Trusted List; see §5.5.2 for the LoTE type mapping)
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Revocation — query the attestation-specific Status List endpoint
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Device binding — verify the KB-JWT or DeviceAuth
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If any supplementary attestation fails signature, revocation, or binding checks, the Relying Party evaluates whether the presentation can proceed partially or must be rejected entirely based on the service's data dependencies.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs ATTESTATION_VERIFICATION_COMPLETED for each evaluated artifact.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Attestation revocation asymmetry: Unlike PID Providers, Attestation Providers are NOT obligated to cascade-revoke when the WUA is revoked (ARF HLR AS-WP-38-019). They MAY choose to do so, but are only required to publish their cascading revocation policy. This means that after a Wallet Unit compromise, the PID becomes invalid immediately (step 3–4), but EAAs may remain valid depending on each Attestation Provider's individual policy. Relying Parties performing high-assurance verifications should check the PID status as the primary trust signal — see the note following step 7 for the cascading revocation asymmetry detail.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 7. Relying Party Instance verifies combined presentation binding
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: In multi-attestation scenarios (where the vp_token contains more than one credential), the Relying Party must verify that all presented credentials belong to the same User and were presented from the same Wallet Unit. This prevents a "credential cocktail" attack where attributes from different Users or devices are combined:
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: SD-JWT VC: All credentials must contain the same cnf.jwk public key. Since the KB-JWT for each credential is signed with this key, identical cnf.jwk values prove the same device key — and therefore the same Wallet Unit — holds all credentials.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: mdoc: All DeviceResponse documents must reference the same deviceKey in their MSO deviceKeyInfo.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Cross-format (SD-JWT VC + mdoc): The Relying Party must determine whether the SD-JWT's cnf.jwk (JWK format) and the mdoc's deviceKey (COSE_Key format) represent the same underlying key. See §18.5.8 for the cross-format key matching algorithm.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If a WSCA binding proof is available (future feature — ARF v2.8 §7.6.3), the Relying Party should additionally verify it to confirm hardware-level key co-residency. See §18.5.7 steps 9–10 for the detailed identity matching and WSCA proof verification logic.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a MULTI_CREDENTIAL_PRESENTATION_BOUND event upon successful validation of holistic cryptographic binding.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: When step 7 is not needed: If the presentation contains a single credential (e.g., PID only), this step is skipped — there is nothing to cross-bind against.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Cascading revocation asymmetry — RP awareness: The indirect trust model described above has an asymmetry that RPs should understand. When a Wallet Provider revokes a WUA (e.g., device compromise, user death), the PID Provider is legally required (CIR 2024/2977 Art. 5.4(b)) to cascade-revoke the associated PID immediately. However, Attestation Providers are not obligated to cascade-revoke — they MAY choose to revoke but are only required to publish their cascading revocation policy (ARF HLR AS-WP-38-019). This means that after a WUA revocation, a user's PID will become invalid within the PID Provider's status list refresh cycle, but their EAAs (e.g., a qualification attestation or an address credential) may remain valid depending on the issuing Attestation Provider's policy. RPs performing high-assurance verifications (e.g., AML/KYC onboarding per §22) should verify the PID's revocation status as the primary Wallet Unit health signal, since PID cascading revocation is mandatory. RPs that also rely on EAA revocation status for trust decisions should check the Attestation Provider's published cascading revocation policy to understand the gap.
passages.headingText: 5.4.2 Implications for RP Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Similarly, the Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA) — a short-lived (< 24 hours) attestation about the Wallet Instance — is presented only to Providers, not to RPs.
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: However, this design is subject to active policy debate. The current CIR 2024/2982 mandates that wallets support protocols for RPs to request WUAs for wallet authentication. A proposed amendment of CIR 2024/2982 would replace this with a narrower mechanism: RPs would receive Wallet Instance Attestations instead of Wallet Unit Attestations. The WIA attests only to the integrity of the wallet instance (not the full WSCA/WSCD properties), with content specified in OID4VP Appendix E including the eudi_wallet_info claim. This proposal is motivated by data minimisation: WUAs expose WSCD-level details that RPs have no business need to know, and exposing them would enable linkability across presentations.
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: Non-EUDI wallet trust: RPs that also interact with non-EUDI wallets — platform wallets (Apple Wallet, Google Wallet) or commercial/SSI wallets (Lissi, walt.id, etc.) — must maintain separate trust framework profiles. The trust model differs fundamentally by wallet category (see §7.4 for the full landscape analysis):
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: Where Art. 5f applies, RPs must accept wallets provided in accordance with the Regulation
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: Trust anchors from LoTE; verification via EUDI trust chain (§5.5)
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: Platform wallets (Apple Wallet, Google Wallet)
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: Platform-specific trust model (IACA for Apple, Android Credential Manager for Google)
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: No eIDAS acceptance obligation; platform-specific terms of service
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: Separate integration per platform; X.509 PKI but non-EUDI issuer CAs (§7.4.1–§7.4.2)
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: Organisation-specific policy; may use EUDI trust chain for EUDI flows, DID-based trust for EBSI/SSI flows
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: No automatic eIDAS acceptance obligation from this category alone
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: Configurable admission criteria; may dual-stack EUDI + SSI trust (§7.4.3)
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: RPs should not attempt to apply the same verification pipeline to all three categories. A certified EUDI Wallet is verifiable through the LoTE chain; platform and commercial wallets require bilateral or platform-specific trust establishment.
passages.headingText: 5.4.3 Wallet Instance Attestation (WIA): Also Not Seen by RPs

passages.text: The trust anchor discovery mechanism in the EUDI Wallet ecosystem uses a two-tier architecture:

Common Trust Infrastructure — Maintained by the European Commission. Contains the URLs of all Trusted Lists and LoTEs across all Member States. Any entity in the ecosystem can discover all trust anchors by starting from this common infrastructure.

Common Trust Infrastructure — Maintained by the European Commission. Contains the URLs of all Trusted Lists and LoTEs across all Member States. Any entity in the ecosystem can discover all trust anchors by starting from this common infrastructure.
Trusted Lists and LoTEs — Published by Member State Trusted List/LoTE Providers. Each LoTE contains the trust anchors (public key + entity identifier) for the entities notified by that Member State.
Trusted Lists and LoTEs — Published by Member State Trusted List/LoTE Providers. Each LoTE contains the trust anchors (public key + entity identifier) for the entities notified by that Member State.

Operational portal: The European Commission publishes these LoTEs through the EFDA (eIDAS Dashboard) at eidas.ec.europa.eu/efda/wallet. The portal provides separate list views for Wallet Providers, PID Providers, Access Certificate Providers, and Registration Certificate Providers. The LoTE data model is defined by ETSI TS 119 602 V1.1.1 (2025-11) — Lists of Trusted Entities; Data Model — which generalises the Trusted List format of ETSI TS 119 612 with entity-type–specific profiles (Annex H provides the JSON serialisation). RPs implementing LoTE consumption for trust anchor discovery (§5.5.3) should reference both the EFDA portal as the authoritative source of list URLs and ETSI TS 119 602 for the data model specification.
Access Certificate Authorities
N/A (Wallet Units use this to verify WRPAC)
Verify PID issuer signatures
QEAA Providers (qualified status)
Verify QEAA issuer signatures
PuB-EAA Providers (notified)
Discover PuB-EAA Provider identity; actual verification uses QTSP certificate
Qualified Trust Service Providers (Art. 22)
Verify PuB-EAA Provider certificates
Providers of Registration Certificates
N/A (Wallet Units use this to verify WRPRCs)
N/A (Providers use this to verify WUA/WIA)

The table above lists all trust infrastructure types, but conflates two distinct ETSI specifications — LoTEs (ETSI TS 119 602) and Trusted Lists (ETSI TS 119 612) — which serve different entity categories. The following decision table maps an RP's credential acceptance profile to the exact trust infrastructure components required. See §5.5.7 for the additional OID-FED dimension.
RP Credential Acceptance Profile
Simplest profile — LoTEs only
PID + QEAAs (e.g., professional qualifications)
Both specifications required
PID + PuB-EAAs (e.g., driving licence, diplomas)
Both specifications required
PID + non-qualified EAAs (e.g., loyalty cards, custom attestations)
LoTE + potentially OID-FED trust chains; depends on the EAA Provider's trustedAuthorities type
Full acceptance (PID + QEAA + PuB-EAA + non-qualified EAA)
✅ QEAA TL + PuB-EAA TL + QTSP TL
⚠️ Possible for non-qualified
All three trust infrastructure types
Italian IT-Wallet interop (§5.5.8)
✅ As above per credential types
✅ Required for RP trust establishment
Full OID-FED trust chain resolution for RP onboarding

LoTE (TS 119 602): The new eIDAS 2.0 format. Covers entity types introduced by eIDAS 2.0 — PID Providers, Wallet Providers, Access CAs, Registration Cert Providers. JSON serialisation (TS 119 602 Annex H). Published by Member State LoTE Providers.

Trusted Lists (TS 119 612): The legacy eIDAS 1.0 format. Covers Qualified Trust Service Providers (QTSPs) and their derivative entity types (QEAA Providers, PuB-EAA Providers). XML serialisation. Published by Member State Trusted List Providers.

OID-FED: Dynamic trust chain resolution protocol. Currently permitted only for non-qualified EAAs (TS11 constraint) and mandated by Italy's IT-Wallet for RP trust establishment. Not required for the mandatory interoperability core.

Engineering implication: A PID-only RP (the simplest profile) needs only a single LoTE consumer — no Trusted List parsing, no OID-FED resolution. Each additional credential type adds trust infrastructure dependencies. RPs should explicitly map their target credential acceptance profile to this table during architecture design to avoid over-engineering or under-provisioning their trust anchor pipeline.
1. Relying Party Backend discovers LoTE URLs via Member State registry
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: As a one-time bootstrapping step, the Relying Party queries the centralised European Common Trust Infrastructure — maintained by the European Commission — to discover the authoritative URLs for all Member State Trusted Lists and Lists of Trusted Entities (LoTEs). The Common Trust Infrastructure acts as the DNS root equivalent for the EUDI trust ecosystem: every trust anchor in the system can be traced back through this single entry point.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party should call this endpoint during initial deployment and periodically thereafter (e.g., weekly) to detect newly participating Member States or URL changes. The response is signed by the European Commission's own key, which the Relying Party must pre-configure as a root trust anchor — this is the one trust decision the Relying Party makes without external validation.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a CTI_DISCOVERY_INITIATED event to track trust bootstrapping.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 2. Common Trust Infrastructure returns List of all LoTE/Trusted List URLs
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Common Trust Infrastructure responds with the official directory mapping each Member State to its respective LoTE publication endpoints. The response includes separate URLs for each LoTE type (§5.5.2):
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party typically needs only the PID Provider LoTE and the QEAA/QTSP Trusted Lists — the Access CA LoTE and Wallet Provider LoTE are used by Wallet Units and Providers respectively, not by Relying Parties (see §5.5.2 table). For cross-border scenarios, the Relying Party should fetch LoTEs from all Member States whose citizens it expects to serve.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Common Trust Infrastructure Discovery Document (JSON).
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a CTI_DISCOVERY_SUCCESS event containing the enumeration of available Member State lists.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 3. Relying Party Backend fetches PID Provider LoTE
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party sends an HTTP GET to the specific Member State's PID Provider LoTE endpoint to download the list of authorised PID Providers and their associated public keys. The LoTE is published in the OpenID Federation format (OID-FED), where each entity is represented as an Entity Statement JWT:
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party should include conditional request headers (If-None-Match / If-Modified-Since) to avoid downloading unchanged LoTEs on subsequent refreshes. For Relying Parties operating across multiple Member States, this step is repeated for each relevant jurisdiction — e.g., a Dutch bank serving German customers fetches both the NL and DE PID Provider LoTEs.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a LOTE_FETCH_INITIATED event indicating the target jurisdiction.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Latency consideration: LoTE fetches add network latency to the bootstrapping process. Relying Parties should fetch LoTEs asynchronously during startup and cache them aggressively, rather than fetching on-demand during presentation verification.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 4. LoTE Provider returns signed LoTE containing PID Provider trust anchors
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The LoTE Provider returns a cryptographically signed document (the LoTE) containing the official trust anchors (public keys and entity identifiers) for all active, legitimate PID Providers in that jurisdiction. OpenID Federation is the standard format, where the payload is an Entity Statement JWT:
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The jwks.keys array contains the PID Provider's public keys used for signing PIDs. The trust_marks array contains attestations from the Member State that this entity is a notified PID Provider. The exp timestamp defines when this Entity Statement expires — the Relying Party must re-fetch before expiry.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: LoTE Entity Statement (OID-FED JWT).
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Multiple PID Providers per MS: A Member State may have multiple notified PID Providers (e.g., Germany could designate Bundesdruckerei and a second provider). The LoTE contains Entity Statements for all of them. The Relying Party must store all trust anchors and select the correct one at verification time (step 7) based on the iss claim in the presented PID.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 5. Relying Party Backend verifies LoTE signature
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Because the LoTE serves as the absolute root of trust for all subsequent User presentations, the Relying Party must cryptographically prove that the downloaded OID-FED Entity Statement was genuinely signed by the authoritative Member State LoTE Provider and has not suffered transit tampering or DNS hijacking.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Relying Party extracts the kid from the JWT header and resolves the LoTE Provider's public key (either pre-configured via the Common Trust Infrastructure or fetched via .well-known/openid-federation).
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The verification routine must strictly evaluate:
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Mathematical Signature: Verify the JWS signature (usually ES256 or RS256) over the payload.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Temporal Validity: Ensure the current system time falls strictly between the iat (Issued At) and exp (Expiration) claims.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Issuer Integrity: Confirm the iss claim exactly matches the officially expected LoTE Provider URI.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the LoTE signature fails validation, the Relying Party MUST instantly reject the payload. Caching an unverified LoTE allows an attacker to inject rogue PID Provider trust anchors, entirely subverting the eIDAS 2.0 trust model.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party triggers a LOTE_SIGNATURE_VERIFICATION_FAILED security alert upon rejection.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Zero-Trust Fallback: In a failure scenario, the Relying Party should retain its previously cached (known-good) LoTE until the issue resolves.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 6. Relying Party Backend caches trust anchors locally
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Once the LoTE signature is verified, the Relying Party unpacks the jwks array from the Entity Statement and securely ingests the trust anchors into its highly-available local cache (e.g., Redis, DynamoDB, or a secure hardware enclave).
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Caching is an absolute architectural necessity. Relying Parties MUST NOT attempt to fetch the LoTE synchronously during a live User presentation, as this introduces unacceptable latency, points of failure, and privacy-leaking metadata to the Member State.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The cache architecture must fulfill highly specific technical constraints:
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Indexed Lookups: Keys must be instantly retrievable via an O(1) lookup using a composite identifier (provider_issuer_uri, key_id).
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: TTL Enforcement: Cached keys must inherit the exp timestamp of the parent LoTE Entity Statement. If the cache entry expires, the Relying Party must suspend verifications for that provider until a fresh LoTE is fetched.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Differential Updates: The Relying Party's cron job (fetching the LoTE hourly or daily) must perform a diff against the cache to detect newly added keys, rotated keys, or revoked/removed providers.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a TRUST_ANCHOR_CACHE_UPDATED event to ensure all distributed verification nodes are aware of the new keys.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Provider Revocation Handling: If a previously trusted PID Provider is entirely removed from a newly published LoTE (indicating suspension or compromise), the Relying Party's caching logic must instantly purge or blacklist that provider's keys, immediately halting acceptance of their credentials.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 7. Relying Party Backend looks up trust anchor for PID Provider
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: During the live millisecond-window of a User presentation (§9.2 or §13.4), the Relying Party must dynamically pair the incoming credential with its root of trust. The Relying Party extracts the unverified issuer identifier directly from the presented credential and probes its local cache.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: SD-JWT VC Framework: The Relying Party parses the unverified Issuer-JWT payload to extract the iss (Issuer) claim. It combines this URI with the kid from the JWT header to execute a targeted cache lookup.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: ISO 18013-5 mdoc: The Relying Party extracts the issuerAuth COSE_Sign1 object. Because mdoc relies on X.509, the Relying Party typically extracts the Issuer Distinguished Name (DN) from the embedded x5chain leaf certificate and cross-references it against cached Country Signing Certificate Authority (CSCA) roots.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the lookup fails (cache miss or expired anchor), the Relying Party usually terminates the verification protocol.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party triggers a TRUST_ANCHOR_LOOKUP_FAILED event.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Foreign Cache Miss Mechanics: In highly distributed cross-border scenarios, a cache miss might simply indicate the Relying Party hasn't fetched a specific foreign Member State's LoTE yet. Advanced Relying Party implementations may trigger a deferred asynchronous LoTE fetch, though this drastically delays the User UX.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 8. Relying Party Backend verifies PID signature using the trust anchor
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Armed with the fiercely guarded, locally cached trust anchor, the Relying Party executes the definitive cryptographic verification over the User's presented PID.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Online (SD-JWT): The Relying Party inputs the cached JWK public key and the presented Issuer-JWT into its cryptographic library to verify the ES256 (P-256) signature. This mathematically proves that the exact byte-sequence of the JWT was approved by the PID Provider.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Proximity (mdoc): The Relying Party inputs the cached CSCA public key to validate the x5chain up to the root, and subsequently verifies the COSE_Sign1 signature enveloping the Mobile Security Object (MSO).
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: A mathematically successful verification unequivocally proves three systemic truths:
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Origin Integrity: The PID was minted by a legally recognized, Member State–notified PID Provider.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Data Integrity: The core attributes (Name, DOB) have not suffered a single bit of tampering since issuance.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Chain of Trust: The unbroken cryptographic link connects the User's credential perfectly back through the PID Provider, the MS LoTE, and ultimately to the European Commission's Common Trust Infrastructure.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Upon success, the Relying Party safely proceeds to revocation checking and device-binding (PoP) verification.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a PID_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED_SUCCESS event, linking the presentation identifier to the trusted issuer.
passages.headingText: 5.5.3 RP Trust Anchor Retrieval Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: LoTE status changed to Invalid → RP should reject PIDs from this Provider
passages.headingText: 5.5.4 Trust Anchor Lifecycle Events That Affect RPs

passages.text: QEAA Provider loses qualified status
passages.headingText: 5.5.4 Trust Anchor Lifecycle Events That Affect RPs

passages.text: Trusted List updated → RP should reject new QEAAs from this Provider
passages.headingText: 5.5.4 Trust Anchor Lifecycle Events That Affect RPs

passages.text: LoTE updated → Wallet Units stop trusting WRPACs from this CA → RP must obtain new WRPAC from different CA
passages.headingText: 5.5.4 Trust Anchor Lifecycle Events That Affect RPs

passages.text: Wallet Provider LoTE updated → PID/Attestation Providers stop issuing → existing PIDs remain valid until revoked
passages.headingText: 5.5.4 Trust Anchor Lifecycle Events That Affect RPs

passages.text: WRPAC revoked → RP Instance can no longer authenticate to Wallet Units
passages.headingText: 5.5.4 Trust Anchor Lifecycle Events That Affect RPs

passages.text: RP operational requirement: RPs must implement periodic LoTE/Trusted List refresh (at minimum daily, recommended more frequently) to ensure they are using current trust anchors for verifying presented credentials. Stale trust anchors could lead to accepting credentials from suspended Providers.
passages.headingText: 5.5.4 Trust Anchor Lifecycle Events That Affect RPs

passages.text: The preceding sections (§5.5.1–§5.5.4) document how RPs use Lists of Trusted Entities (LoTEs) to discover trust anchors. The LoTE format — application/entity-statement+jwt fetched from /.well-known/openid-federation — is defined by OpenID Federation 1.0 (OID-FED), a protocol standardised by the OpenID Foundation. However, OID-FED is far more than a data format. It is a complete protocol for establishing automated, dynamic trust between entities through hierarchical chains of cryptographically signed metadata statements. This section explains the protocol itself, enabling readers to understand the architectural context behind the LoTE format already used throughout §5.5.
passages.headingText: 5.5.5 OpenID Federation: The Protocol Behind LoTE Entity Statements

passages.text: Why this matters for RPs: The EUDI ecosystem currently uses OID-FED as a data format (Entity Statement JWTs for LoTEs) within the ETSI Trusted List trust model. However, TS11 explicitly permits full OID-FED trust chain resolution for non-qualified EAAs (§5.5.7), and Italy's IT-Wallet (§5.5.8) already mandates it for RP trust establishment. RPs planning for non-qualified EAA acceptance or cross-border interoperability with OID-FED–based Member States need to understand the protocol, not just the format.
passages.headingText: 5.5.5 OpenID Federation: The Protocol Behind LoTE Entity Statements

passages.text: A self-signed JWT published by every federation entity at /.well-known/openid-federation. Contains the entity's metadata, public keys (jwks), authority_hints, and Trust Marks.
An RP participating in an OID-FED federation MUST publish its Entity Configuration. The Wallet Instance fetches this to discover the RP's metadata and verify its trust chain.
A signed JWT issued by a superior entity (Trust Anchor or Intermediate) about a subordinate. Contains the subordinate's public keys, and may include metadata policies and Trust Marks.
The Trust Anchor or Intermediate issues a Subordinate Statement about the RP, attesting that it is a registered member of the federation.

An ordered sequence of Entity Statements: [Leaf Entity Configuration, Subordinate Statement(s), Trust Anchor Entity Configuration]. Each signature is verifiable using the keys from the next statement in the chain.
A Wallet Instance constructs this chain to verify the RP's identity before responding to a presentation request.
The root authority of a federation. Its public keys are pre-configured by all participants. Issues Subordinate Statements for its direct subordinates.
In the EUDI context, the National Trust Anchor (e.g., AgID for Italy) serves this role. Its public keys are distributed out-of-band.
An entity between the Trust Anchor and a Leaf, forming part of the hierarchical trust chain.
A Member State may delegate RP onboarding to sector-specific Intermediates (e.g., banking sector registrar).
A signed JWT attestation of conformance to specific criteria (e.g., "this RP is authorised to request PID attributes"). Issued by a Trust Mark Issuer accredited by the Trust Anchor.
Trust Marks enable fine-grained RP authorisation — e.g., whether an RP may interact with minors, or which credential types it may request.
Rules applied by superiors in the trust chain that constrain or modify a subordinate's metadata. Uses operators like subset_of, one_of, add, default.
The Trust Anchor can enforce policies on RPs — e.g., limiting which VP formats or attribute scopes an RP may declare.
The trust chain resolution process (OID-FED §11) enables a Wallet Instance to verify an RP's federation membership at runtime:
The Wallet Instance wants to establish trust with the RP.
Wallet fetches the RP's Entity Configuration from https://rp.example.org/.well-known/openid-federation.
Wallet extracts authority_hints from the Entity Configuration — the URLs of the RP's immediate superiors in the federation hierarchy.

For each authority hint, the Wallet: (a) fetches the superior's Entity Configuration from /.well-known/openid-federation; (b) fetches the Subordinate Statement about the RP from the superior's Fetch endpoint (/fetch?sub=https://rp.example.org); (c) verifies that the RP's Entity Configuration signature matches the keys in the Subordinate Statement.
Repeats step 4 upward until reaching a Trust Anchor whose public keys the Wallet already possesses.
Validates the entire chain: signature integrity, temporal validity (iat/exp), issuer-subject binding.
Applies metadata policies from each Subordinate Statement (cascading from Trust Anchor downward) to derive the RP's final metadata.
Optionally validates Trust Marks.

Latency implication: Trust chain resolution requires multiple sequential HTTP fetches — one per level of hierarchy. A single-level federation (RP → Trust Anchor) requires 2 fetches; with an Intermediate, 3. This makes the optional Resolver endpoint (/resolve) architecturally important for latency-sensitive use cases, and explains why the Italian specification allows RPs to include pre-built trust chains in presentation request JWT headers for offline verification.
GET /.well-known/openid-federation
Self-published metadata and keys
Fetch (Subordinate Statement)
GET /fetch?sub=<entity_id>
Return Subordinate Statement about a subordinate
GET /resolve?sub=<entity_id>&trust_anchor=<ta_id>
Resolve trust chain and return final metadata
POST /status?sub=...&trust_mark_id=...

The following sequence diagram illustrates how a Wallet Instance verifies an RP's federation membership using OID-FED trust chain resolution. This is the alternative trust model to WRPAC-based (X.509) RP verification — currently mandated only in the Italian IT-Wallet (§5.5.8) and permitted for non-qualified EAA trust contexts (§5.5.7).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 1. Relying Party sends signed presentation request to Wallet Instance
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Relying Party initiates the flow by sending a credential presentation request to the Wallet Instance. In the OID-FED trust model, the Relying Party's client_id uses the openid_federation scheme (rather than x509_hash used in the WRPAC model). The request is signed with the Relying Party's federation entity key. The Wallet Instance does not yet trust this Relying Party — it must resolve the trust chain before processing the request.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Inbound Presentation Request (OAuth 2.0).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs an INBOUND_PRESENTATION_REQUEST_RECEIVED event.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 2. Wallet Instance fetches Relying Party's Entity Configuration
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Wallet Instance sends an HTTP GET to the Relying Party's /.well-known/openid-federation endpoint. This is the standard OID-FED discovery mechanism — every entity in the federation publishes its Entity Configuration at this well-known path. The request uses the Accept: application/entity-statement+jwt media type.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs an RP_ENTITY_CONFIGURATION_FETCH_INITIATED event to track trust discovery.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 3. Relying Party returns Entity Configuration JWT
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Relying Party responds with its self-signed Entity Configuration — a JWT containing: metadata (the Relying Party's declared capabilities, including openid_credential_verifier with VP formats and requested scopes), jwks (the Relying Party's public keys), authority_hints (URLs of superior entities in the federation hierarchy), and trust_marks (optional attestations of Relying Party authorisation). The Wallet Instance uses authority_hints in the next step to walk upward through the trust hierarchy.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Relying Party Entity Configuration (JWT).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 4. Wallet Instance verifies Relying Party Entity Configuration self-signature
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Wallet Instance verifies that the Entity Configuration JWT was signed by one of the keys declared in its own jwks field. This is a self-consistency check — it proves the JWT was not tampered with in transit, but does not yet establish trust. Trust is established only after the full chain (steps 5–14) is validated.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the self-signature fails, the Wallet Instance immediately terminates the flow as the Entity Configuration is corrupt or forged.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs an RP_ENTITY_CONFIGURATION_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event or failure alert.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 5. Wallet Instance extracts authority_hints from Relying Party Entity Configuration
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Wallet Instance parses the authority_hints array from the Relying Party's Entity Configuration. Each entry is the URL of a superior entity (Intermediate or Trust Anchor) that has issued a Subordinate Statement about the Relying Party. In a single-level federation, this points directly to the Trust Anchor. In the Italian model, it typically points to an Intermediate (e.g., a sector-specific registrar).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 6. Wallet Instance fetches Intermediate Entity Configuration
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Wallet Instance sends an HTTP GET to the Intermediate's /.well-known/openid-federation endpoint. This retrieves the Intermediate's own Entity Configuration, which contains its public keys (needed to verify its Subordinate Statements) and its own authority_hints (pointing to the Trust Anchor).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs an INTERMEDIATE_ENTITY_CONFIGURATION_FETCH_INITIATED event.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 7. Intermediate returns its Entity Configuration JWT
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Intermediate responds with its self-signed Entity Configuration. The Wallet Instance extracts the Intermediate's jwks (for verifying the Subordinate Statement about the Relying Party) and authority_hints (for continuing the chain walk upward to the Trust Anchor).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Intermediate Entity Configuration (JWT).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 8. Wallet Instance fetches Subordinate Statement about Relying Party from Intermediate
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Wallet Instance queries the Intermediate's Fetch endpoint (/fetch?sub=https://rp.example.org) to obtain the Subordinate Statement — the signed JWT that attests the Relying Party is a registered member of the federation under this Intermediate. This is the critical attestation that links the Relying Party to the federation hierarchy.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs an INTERMEDIATE_SUBORDINATE_STATEMENT_FETCH_INITIATED event.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 9. Intermediate returns Subordinate Statement about Relying Party
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Intermediate responds with a signed Subordinate Statement JWT. This contains: the Relying Party's public keys (which must match the keys in the Relying Party's Entity Configuration from step 3), optional metadata_policy rules (constraints on the Relying Party's metadata, such as allowed VP formats or scope restrictions), and optional Trust Marks. The Wallet Instance verifies this JWT using the Intermediate's keys from step 7.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Relying Party Subordinate Statement (JWT).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 10. Wallet Instance fetches Trust Anchor Entity Configuration
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Continuing upward, the Wallet Instance fetches the Trust Anchor's Entity Configuration from /.well-known/openid-federation. The Wallet Instance typically has the Trust Anchor's public keys pre-configured (distributed out-of-band by the Member State). This is the final HTTP fetch in the chain walk.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs a TRUST_ANCHOR_ENTITY_CONFIGURATION_FETCH_INITIATED event.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 11. Trust Anchor returns its Entity Configuration JWT
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Trust Anchor responds with its Entity Configuration. The Wallet Instance verifies this JWT using its pre-configured Trust Anchor public keys — this is the root of trust anchoring the entire chain.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the signature does not match the pre-configured keys, the Wallet Instance immediately rejects the chain.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs a TRUST_ANCHOR_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event or failure alert.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 12. Wallet Instance fetches Subordinate Statement about Intermediate from Trust Anchor
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Wallet Instance queries the Trust Anchor's Fetch endpoint to obtain the Subordinate Statement attesting the Intermediate's membership in the federation. This completes the chain: Relying Party → Intermediate → Trust Anchor.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs a TA_SUBORDINATE_STATEMENT_FETCH_INITIATED event.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 13. Trust Anchor returns Subordinate Statement about Intermediate
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: The Trust Anchor responds with a signed Subordinate Statement about the Intermediate, containing the Intermediate's public keys. The Wallet Instance verifies this using the Trust Anchor's keys from step 11.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Intermediate Subordinate Statement (JWT).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 14. Wallet Instance validates the complete trust chain
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: With all chain components assembled — [Relying Party Entity Configuration, Subordinate Statement from Intermediate, Intermediate Entity Configuration, Subordinate Statement from Trust Anchor, Trust Anchor Entity Configuration] — the Wallet Instance performs comprehensive validation: (a) signature integrity at each level, (b) temporal validity (iat/exp) for every JWT, (c) issuer-subject binding (each iss must match the expected superior, each sub must match the expected subordinate), (d) key consistency (the Relying Party's keys in the Subordinate Statement must match those in its Entity Configuration).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If any aspect of the chain validation fails (e.g., expired timestamp or inconsistent keys), the Wallet Instance aborts the presentation immediately.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs a TRUST_CHAIN_VALIDATION_COMPLETED event.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 15. Wallet Instance applies metadata policies from Trust Anchor downward
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Metadata policies from each Subordinate Statement are applied in cascade — from Trust Anchor downward through Intermediates to the Relying Party. Operators like subset_of, one_of, add, and default constrain the Relying Party's declared metadata. For example, the Trust Anchor might enforce vp_formats to include only dc+sd-jwt with ES256/ES384, overriding any broader format declarations by the Relying Party.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 16. Wallet Instance derives Relying Party's final metadata
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: After policy application, the Wallet Instance has the Relying Party's final metadata — the effective set of capabilities the Relying Party is authorised to use within the federation. This includes: which VP formats the Relying Party may accept, which attribute scopes it may request, which client authentication methods it supports, and its organisation information (name, logo, contacts). The Wallet Instance uses this metadata to evaluate the presentation request from step 1.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs a RP_FINAL_METADATA_DERIVED event.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: 17. Wallet Instance validates Trust Marks (optional)
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: If the Relying Party's Entity Configuration or Subordinate Statements contain Trust Marks, the Wallet Instance may optionally validate them by querying the Trust Mark Status endpoint (/status). Trust Marks provide fine-grained authorisation signals — e.g., "this Relying Party is certified for LoA High interactions" or "this Relying Party may interact with minors". Trust Mark validation is optional because federation membership (established in steps 14–16) already provides baseline trust.
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: Comparison with WRPAC-based RP verification: In the standard EU-wide model (§5.2, §11.1), the Wallet verifies the RP using its WRPAC — an X.509 certificate containing the RP's registration data. This is a single certificate chain verification against a pre-cached Access CA trust anchor. The OID-FED model replaces this with the multi-hop trust chain resolution described above. The key trade-off: OID-FED provides richer metadata discovery and policy enforcement but requires more HTTP fetches and online connectivity (unless the RP includes a pre-built trust_chain in its presentation request).
passages.headingText: 5.5.6 Trust Chain Resolution: Wallet Verifying an RP (OID-FED Model)

passages.text: OID-FED and ETSI Trusted Lists are complementary, not competing, trust frameworks in the EUDI ecosystem. Their applicability depends on the credential type and the use case. The following tables clarify when each applies.
passages.headingText: 5.5.7 When OpenID Federation Applies: Credential Type Trust Framework Map

passages.text: XML (TS 119 612) or JSON (TS 119 602 Annex H)
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: Hierarchical (Trust Anchor → Intermediates → Leaves)
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: Flat published list (LoTE Provider publishes all entities)

passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: Metadata policies cascade through Subordinate Statements

passages.text: No metadata policy mechanism
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: Subordinate Statement expiry or unavailability
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: Trust Chain can be embedded in JWT trust_chain header
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists
