passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: Entity updates its Entity Configuration; superior re-issues Subordinate Statement
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: LoTE Provider re-publishes the list
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: O(depth) HTTP fetches per trust chain resolution
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: O(1) list download per jurisdiction
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: XAdES or JAdES (depending on format)
passages.headingText: Structural Comparison: OID-FED Trust Chains vs ETSI Trusted Lists

passages.text: ETSI Trusted Lists (mandatory)
Mandated by CIR 2025/848 and ARF
ETSI Trusted Lists (mandatory)
Qualified status requires QTSP Trusted List
ETSI Trusted Lists (mandatory)
Notification to European Commission
Non-qualified EAA Provider trust
OID-FED (permitted) or ETSI TS 119 602 LoTE
TS11 allows OID-FED for non-qualified only
ARF mandates access certificates
OID-FED trust chain resolution
SPID/CIE legacy + IT-Wallet design (§5.5.8)
ETSI Trusted Lists (mandatory)
Notification to European Commission

TS11 constraint: The trustedAuthorities field in TS11 attestation schemas supports three trust framework options: X.509 Authority Key Identifier (RFC 5280 §5.2.1.1), ETSI Trusted Lists (ETSI TS 119 612), and OpenID Federation. However, TS11 explicitly restricts OID-FED: "OpenID Federation MAY only be used in context of non-qualified EAA types." This means RPs accepting non-qualified EAAs must be prepared for OID-FED trust chains, while PID/QEAA/PuB-EAA trust remains exclusively ETSI-based.

Italy's IT-Wallet implementation is the only EUDI Large Scale Pilot that uses OpenID Federation as its complete trust infrastructure — not merely as a data format (as all other Member States do for LoTEs), but as the full protocol for RP onboarding, trust establishment, and metadata discovery. This makes it the most significant real-world reference for OID-FED in the EUDI ecosystem.
passages.headingText: 5.5.8 National Precedent: Italian IT-Wallet and Full OID-FED Trust Infrastructure

passages.text: All entities (Trust Anchor, Intermediates, Wallet Providers, Credential Issuers, Relying Parties) are required to publish an Entity Configuration at /.well-known/openid-federation.

RP onboarding is federation-based: the RP publishes its Entity Configuration (signed with its Federation Entity Private Key), and the Trust Anchor (or an Intermediate) issues a Subordinate Statement attesting the RP's membership. Metadata policies in the Subordinate Statement constrain allowed VP formats (e.g., dc+sd-jwt with ES256/ES384).
Wallet Instance RP verification uses the OID-FED trust chain resolution process described in §5.5.6.
Trust Marks provide fine-grained RP authorisation — including public/private RP classification and whether an RP may interact with minors.
Italy operates both trust models simultaneously:
passages.headingText: Dual Trust Path: OID-FED and ETSI Coexistence

passages.text: ETSI TS 119 612 XML Trusted Lists
passages.headingText: Dual Trust Path: OID-FED and ETSI Coexistence

passages.text: ETSI TS 119 602 Annex H LoTE (JSON)
passages.headingText: Dual Trust Path: OID-FED and ETSI Coexistence

passages.text: RPs, Wallet Providers, Credential Issuers
passages.headingText: Dual Trust Path: OID-FED and ETSI Coexistence

passages.text: Entities listed in national Trusted Lists are also registered in the national Federation Registry. Key validation can occur through both mechanisms — verification against the Trusted List and verification through federation endpoints. This dual-path approach provides redundancy and enables gradual migration without abandoning the established ETSI model.
passages.headingText: Dual Trust Path: OID-FED and ETSI Coexistence

passages.text: When an Italian Wallet User interacts with an RP from a Member State that uses the standard WRPAC/X.509 model (e.g., Germany), a trust model mismatch occurs — the Italian Wallet expects OID-FED trust chains, but the foreign RP presents an X.509 WRPAC. The Italian specification resolves this through the OpenID4VP client_id scheme mechanism:
client_id scheme = openid_federation → Wallet performs OID-FED trust chain resolution (§5.5.6)
client_id scheme = x509_hash → Wallet performs X.509 WRPAC verification, with RP metadata conveyed in client_metadata instead of via Entity Configuration
This dual-scheme approach ensures cross-border interoperability, but adds implementation complexity for both RPs and Wallet Providers that must support both trust models.
An RP participating in the Italian OID-FED federation publishes the following Entity Configuration (decoded JWT payload):

Forward-looking note: If other Member States adopt OID-FED for RP trust establishment (following the Italian model), the EU could establish a cross-federation Trust Anchor where the European Commission's /.well-known/openid-federation serves as the root, with Member State Trust Anchors as Intermediates. This would parallel the Italian model at EU scale but requires political agreement on EU-wide OID-FED adoption, harmonisation of metadata types, and consensus on metadata policy cascading rules.

When an RP receives a non-qualified EAA — e.g., a gym membership attestation, a loyalty card credential, or an employer attestation — the trust path differs from PID/QEAA verification. TS11's trustedAuthorities framework permits three trust mechanisms for non-qualified EAA Providers:
passages.headingText: 5.5.9 Non-Qualified EAA Issuer Trust: Three Verification Paths

passages.text: RP resolves the EAA Provider's Trust Chain via /.well-known/openid-federation → Subordinate Statements → Trust Anchor (§5.5.6)
passages.headingText: 5.5.9 Non-Qualified EAA Issuer Trust: Three Verification Paths

passages.text: RP must trust the Trust Anchor governing the non-qualified EAA Provider; full OID-FED trust chain resolution required
passages.headingText: 5.5.9 Non-Qualified EAA Issuer Trust: Three Verification Paths

passages.text: RP verifies the EAA Provider's public key against the national EAA Provider LoTE (Annex H format)
passages.headingText: 5.5.9 Non-Qualified EAA Issuer Trust: Three Verification Paths

passages.text: Same as PID Provider verification (§5.5.3) — LoTE-based trust anchor lookup
passages.headingText: 5.5.9 Non-Qualified EAA Issuer Trust: Three Verification Paths

passages.text: No trustedAuthorities field; the EAA Provider's key is not anchored to an external trust framework
passages.headingText: 5.5.9 Non-Qualified EAA Issuer Trust: Three Verification Paths

passages.text: RP must make its own trust decision — no external trust anchor; highest risk
passages.headingText: 5.5.9 Non-Qualified EAA Issuer Trust: Three Verification Paths

passages.text: Engineering implication: RPs accepting non-qualified EAAs must implement three distinct trust verification paths — a significant increase in verification pipeline complexity compared to PID-only RPs (which need only the ETSI LoTE path). The RP's verification logic must branch on the trustedAuthorities type field in the attestation schema to determine which trust path to follow. This branching logic should be implemented as a pluggable module to accommodate future trust framework additions.
passages.headingText: 5.5.9 Non-Qualified EAA Issuer Trust: Three Verification Paths

passages.text: RPs must handle credential lifecycle events gracefully:
PID re-issuance (new cnf key)
The cnf.jwk in the new PID differs from the old PID. Any RP-side binding to the old key (e.g., cached device key) becomes invalid.
RP should match users by PID attributes (e.g., personal_identifier), not by cnf key alone. Update stored device key on each successful presentation.
Expired attestation is rejected by RP verification pipeline
RP should inform the User with a clear message (e.g., "Your credential has expired — please re-issue it from your Wallet Provider"). Do not silently fail.
Wallet Unit migration (device change)

Device-bound credentials (PIDs, most QEAAs) are re-issued with new cnf keys. Non-device-bound attestations (Topic Z) are restored directly from the Migration Object without re-issuance — same attestation instance, same binding (TS10 v1.1, nonDeviceBoundCredentials).

RP-side pseudonym bindings (WebAuthn, §16) may be lost if not backed up. RP should support account recovery via alternative verification (§5.6.1). For non-device-bound attestations, no RP-observable change occurs.
PID Provider rotation (new signing key)
New PIDs are signed with a new key from the same or different Provider
RP should rely on LoTE-based trust anchor validation (§5.5), not on cached specific signing keys.

Key design principle: Do not treat the cnf.jwk (device-bound key) as a stable user identifier. It changes when credentials are re-issued or the user migrates to a new device. Use PID attributes or pseudonym credentials for persistent user identification.

Wallet migration — when a User changes device or Wallet Provider — is a credential lifecycle event that the RP must handle transparently. The RP has no direct interaction with the underlying Migration Object (ARF Topic 34, TS10 v1.1); migration is inferred from observable cryptographic changes in subsequent presentations.
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Device-bound vs non-device-bound: two migration paths. The RP's handling differs based on whether the presented attestation was device-bound:
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Non-Device-Bound Attestations
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Listed in ListOfCredentials (metadata only)
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Included in full as nonDeviceBoundCredentials
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Re-issued via OID4VCI from original Provider
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Directly copied to new Wallet Unit
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: ✅ Yes — new WSCA key pair, new cnf.jwk
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: ❌ No — same attestation instance
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Yes — different cnf.jwk, different status.idx
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Transparent — automatic restoration
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: PID is always re-issued first (Mig_07)
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: PID is always device-bound — N/A
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: RP migration detection and decision tree. The following flowchart captures the RP's decision logic when a returning user's passkey authentication fails — the primary signal that migration (or device loss) may have occurred:
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Passkey succeeds → No action needed. Migration is invisible if the user has a synced passkey (BE=1, BS=1) or never migrated. See §16.11.1 for synced vs device-bound passkey characteristics.
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Passkey fails + PID personal_identifier matches → Migration handling. The RP updates the device key, re-registers a passkey, and restores assurance level. See §16.11.2 for the full account recovery sequence diagram.
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Passkey fails + no PID match → New user. Standard new-account or rejection flow applies.
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Passkey fails + no Wallet → Fallback. Standard non-EUDI recovery methods (email, SMS OTP) apply.
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Progressive assurance restoration after migration. After re-binding a pseudonym via PID step-up (§16.11.2), the RP should restore the account's effective assurance level to at least Substantial, since PID verification just occurred during recovery. If the RP stored previous verification results (e.g., identity_verified: true, verification_method: qeaa_presentation) indexed by personal_identifier rather than by device key, those results can be carried forward — restoring the account to High assurance without requiring the user to re-present the QEAA. See §16.13 for the progressive assurance lifecycle.
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Transaction log continuity. The Migration Object includes the User's complete transaction log (TS10 v1.1), preserving the history of all RP presentations from the old Wallet Unit. This means the User retains the ability to exercise data deletion rights (TS7) for presentations made prior to migration. RPs must be prepared to receive TS7 deletion requests referencing transactions from a previous Wallet Unit.
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: Cross-references: §11.5 (re-issued PID handling), §11.9 (credential churn by design), §16.11.1 (synced vs device-bound passkeys), §16.11.2 (account recovery flow), §16.13 (progressive assurance lifecycle), §16.13.3 (edge cases including wallet migration).
passages.headingText: 5.6.1 Wallet Migration: Consolidated RP Handling Guide

passages.text: SD-JWT VC (Selective Disclosure JSON Web Token Verifiable Credential) is one of two mandatory credential formats in the EUDI Wallet ecosystem (alongside mdoc). It is the primary format for remote (over-the-internet) presentation flows.
An SD-JWT VC consists of three parts, serialised as a single string separated by tildes (~):
Contains the credential metadata and hashed attribute references; signed by the PID/Attestation Provider
Base64url-encoded [salt, claim_name, claim_value] arrays — one per selectively-disclosed attribute
Proves the presenter (Wallet Unit) possesses the private key bound to the credential; signed by the Wallet Unit

Specification layering — SD-JWT vs. SD-JWT VC: SD-JWT VC is an application profile built on top of the base SD-JWT specification (RFC 9901, finalised November 2025). RFC 9901 defines the general-purpose selective disclosure primitive — the _sd hash mechanism, Disclosures, and optional Key Binding JWT. SD-JWT VC (draft-ietf-oauth-sd-jwt-vc-15) layers credential-specific semantics on top: the mandatory vct claim (credential type identifier), the dc+sd-jwt media type, the status claim for revocation, and Issuer Metadata / Type Metadata discovery. Key Binding is optional in RFC 9901 but effectively required in the EUDI context (device binding via WSCA). In the EUDI ecosystem, all credentials use the SD-JWT VC profile — bare SD-JWTs (without vct and dc+sd-jwt typing) are not valid credential presentations. RPs should validate the vct presence and typ header as part of their verification pipeline (§11.3).
The decoded payload of the Issuer-signed JWT for a PID:
passages.headingText: 6.2 SD-JWT VC Structure: Decoded Issuer JWT

passages.text: vct — Verifiable Credential Type: eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1 for PID
passages.headingText: 6.2 SD-JWT VC Structure: Decoded Issuer JWT

passages.text: cnf.jwk — Confirmation key: the public key of the Wallet Unit (device binding)
passages.headingText: 6.2 SD-JWT VC Structure: Decoded Issuer JWT

passages.text: status — Status List reference for revocation checking
passages.headingText: 6.2 SD-JWT VC Structure: Decoded Issuer JWT

passages.text: _sd — Array of hashes of the selectable disclosures
passages.headingText: 6.2 SD-JWT VC Structure: Decoded Issuer JWT

passages.text: _sd_alg — Hash algorithm used (sha-256)
passages.headingText: 6.2 SD-JWT VC Structure: Decoded Issuer JWT

passages.text: When the RP requests only family_name and age_over_18, the Wallet Unit:
passages.headingText: 6.3 SD-JWT VC Selective Disclosure in Practice

passages.text: Includes only the corresponding Disclosures (not all of them)
passages.headingText: 6.3 SD-JWT VC Selective Disclosure in Practice

passages.text: The RP can verify these Disclosures by hashing them and comparing with the _sd array in the Issuer JWT
passages.headingText: 6.3 SD-JWT VC Selective Disclosure in Practice

passages.text: Attributes NOT included in the Disclosures remain hidden — the RP sees only their hashes
passages.headingText: 6.3 SD-JWT VC Selective Disclosure in Practice

passages.text: Example Disclosures included:
passages.headingText: 6.3 SD-JWT VC Selective Disclosure in Practice

passages.text: Decoded: ["_26BcTrkBMGDiiYwRdagLA", "family_name", "Müller"] and ["eluV5Og3gSNII8EYnsxA_A", "age_over_18", true]
passages.headingText: 6.3 SD-JWT VC Selective Disclosure in Practice

passages.text: Note: For ZKP-based age verification with unlinkability guarantees, see §19 (EU Commission ZKP Blueprint). ZKP proofs allow age verification without revealing any attribute value, only the predicate result.
passages.headingText: 6.3 SD-JWT VC Selective Disclosure in Practice

passages.text: The Key Binding JWT proves the presenter controls the private key referenced in cnf.jwk. Its payload:
aud — The RP's identifier (prevents replay to a different RP)
nonce — The nonce from the presentation request (prevents replay)
sd_hash — Hash of the entire SD-JWT (binds the KB-JWT to the specific presentation)

mdoc (mobile document) is the credential format defined by ISO/IEC 18013-5, originally for mobile driving licences (mDL). In the EUDI Wallet ecosystem, it is the mandatory format for proximity presentation flows and is also supported for remote flows via ISO/IEC 18013-7.
mdoc uses CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation) encoding, which is more compact than JSON and optimised for constrained environments (NFC, BLE).
An mdoc consists of two main structures:
Unlike SD-JWT VC (which uses hash-based selective disclosure), mdoc achieves selective disclosure by omission:
The MSO contains digests (hashes) of ALL data elements
The Wallet Unit includes only the IssuerSignedItem entries for the attributes the User approves
The RP can verify each received item by recomputing its hash and checking against the MSO digest
Items NOT included are invisible to the RP — the RP only sees their digest IDs
In proximity flows, the Wallet Unit provides a DeviceAuth structure:
The SessionTranscript binds the response to the specific session, preventing replay. The RP verifies the signature using the deviceKey from the MSO.
BLE / NFC / Wi-Fi Aware + ISO 18013-5
passages.headingText: 6.10 Format Selection: SD-JWT VC vs. mdoc

passages.text: Proximity flows (face-to-face)
passages.headingText: 6.10 Format Selection: SD-JWT VC vs. mdoc

passages.text: Via ISO 18013-7 over OpenID4VP
passages.headingText: 6.10 Format Selection: SD-JWT VC vs. mdoc

passages.text: Hash-based (_sd array + Disclosures)
passages.headingText: 6.10 Format Selection: SD-JWT VC vs. mdoc

passages.text: Omission-based (exclude IssuerSignedItems)
passages.headingText: 6.10 Format Selection: SD-JWT VC vs. mdoc

passages.text: Status List or issuer-defined
passages.headingText: 6.10 Format Selection: SD-JWT VC vs. mdoc

passages.text: Conditional — all steps (signature, disclosures, KB-JWT) execute offline if Issuer trust anchor is pre-cached; only revocation check requires connectivity
passages.headingText: 6.10 Format Selection: SD-JWT VC vs. mdoc

passages.text: Native (designed for offline; MSO verification is self-contained)
passages.headingText: 6.10 Format Selection: SD-JWT VC vs. mdoc

passages.text: RP implementation note: RPs MUST support both formats. For remote flows, the RP typically receives SD-JWT VCs. For proximity flows, the RP receives mdoc. For remote flows, the RP may also receive mdoc via ISO 18013-7 encapsulation in OpenID4VP. The DCQL query language allows the RP to express format preferences.
passages.headingText: 6.10 Format Selection: SD-JWT VC vs. mdoc

passages.text: Attestation Rulebooks define the complete lifecycle and presentation rules for specific attestation types. They are maintained in the EUDI Attestation Rulebooks Catalog. Each Rulebook follows a standardised template (v1.4, March 2026) covering attributes, encoding, usage obligations, trust anchors, and revocation — see Chapter structure in §6.12.
Rulebook Inventory (as of March 2026):
Core identity — every RP needs this
Driving licence — transport, insurance, rental RPs
Legal person identity — B2B onboarding, procurement (§6.15)
TBD (ePrescription, HPC, Patient Summary)
High-impact — pharmacies, hospitals, cross-border health
TBD (Digital Diploma, Microcredential, EDCL)
Cross-border qualification verification, employer onboarding
TBD (Digital Travel Credential)
mdoc (ICAO 9303 / ISO 23220-2)
Border control, hotel check-in, vehicle rental — separate regulatory track (COM/2024/670)
Cross-border workers, posted worker verification
Payment-specific — banks, PSPs (§15)

Published vs. expected: Only PID and mDL Rulebooks are published in the official EC Attestation Rulebooks Catalog. The LPID specification originates from the EWC consortium (RFC005) and is not yet in the EC catalogue. Healthcare, Education, Travel, and Social Security credential specifications have been validated through Large-Scale Pilots (POTENTIAL, DC4EU, EWC/APTITUDE) but have no published Rulebooks. RPs should monitor the EC catalogue repository for new Rulebook publications and implement a pluggable verification architecture (§6.18) to onboard new attestation types as their Rulebooks publish. For detailed sector-specific profiles, see §6.17.

A Rulebook follows a standardised Chapter structure (template v1.4). The table below maps each Rulebook aspect to its machine-readable counterpart in the TS11 Catalogue of Schemes SchemaMeta model (§6.16):
Mandatory vs. optional attributes within the attestation
schemaURIs[].uri → JSON schema
RP knows which attributes it can request
DocType and namespace identifiers (for mdoc)
RP must use exact identifiers in DeviceRequest
Verifiable Credential Type URI (for SD-JWT VC)
RP uses this in DCQL meta.vct_values
How the issuer chain is validated
RP builds verification logic accordingly (§6.16)
Per-claim selective disclosure policy (ARB_30)
schemaURIs[].uri → claim metadata
RP must respect per-claim disclosability rules
Whether RP must also verify PID alongside this attestation (ARB_27)
RP's obligation engine checks co-verification requirement
Device-bound vs. non-device-bound (ARB_34)
RP verification pipeline differs based on binding
cryptographically_bound_to field for binding to PID (ARB_28)
RP must verify cryptographic binding when specified
Default and allowed disclosure policies
RP knows if embedded restrictions may apply
Required revocation mechanism (Status List)
RP knows which endpoint to check
RP can enforce freshness thresholds
ISO 18045 security evaluation level
RP can apply risk-based acceptance policies
Machine-readable discovery: The SchemaMeta fields above are queryable via the TS11 Catalogue of Schemes REST API — see §6.16 for the discovery mechanism and RP integration flow.
The PID Rulebook defines the full PID attribute set. RPs should request only what they need:
KYC, legal contracts, account opening
Age-gated services (no DOB disclosure needed)
Visual identity verification (proximity)
Healthcare, formal documents
Data minimisation example: An age-verification RP for alcohol sales should request ONLY age_over_18, NOT birth_date, family_name, or any other attributes.

The PID Rulebook defines the full set of attributes that a PID may contain. RPs must be aware of the complete catalogue to construct precise DCQL queries. The table below shows every attribute, its SD-JWT VC claim_path, and mdoc elementIdentifier:

Namespace note: In mdoc format, all PID attributes use the namespace eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1. The elementIdentifier above is the key within that namespace. For age_over_NN, NN can be any integer (e.g., age_over_21, age_over_65).

RP data minimisation: System attributes (issuance_date, expiry_date, issuing_authority, etc.) are contained in the MSO/Issuer JWT metadata — RPs receive them automatically during verification. They should NOT be requested as separate claims in DCQL queries.

Member State PID variation: CIR 2024/2977 defines the mandatory and optional attribute sets, but each Member State decides which optional attributes to include in its national PID (Recital 12). This means the PID a RP receives from one country may differ from another. For example, the Netherlands has indicated it will limit PID to the mandatory attributes only, possibly supplemented by a national administrative number (BSN equivalent) via the personal_administrative_number field. RPs accepting cross-border PIDs must design presentation flows that gracefully handle varying attribute availability — requesting optional attributes as non-mandatory in DCQL queries and using additional EAA attestations to supplement information demands that the PID alone cannot satisfy.

The Legal Person Identification Data (LPID) attestation uses the same dc+sd-jwt format profile as the natural person PID (§6.1). The LPID attribute model is defined in §3.3; this section covers the credential format specifics relevant to RP verification pipelines.
EWC RFC005 §6.10.4 defines the LPID attestation with VCT value EWC_LPID_Attestation. The credential issuer metadata entry:
VCT value: EWC_LPID_Attestation — distinct from eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1. RPs must check the vct claim to distinguish natural person from legal person credentials.
Format: dc+sd-jwt — identical format profile to natural person PID. The same SD-JWT VC verification pipeline applies (§11.3).
Selective disclosure: All claims are independently disclosable via SD-JWT's _sd mechanism.
Key binding: Uses the same cnf claim as natural person SD-JWT VCs — same cnf.jwk device binding model.

VCT standardisation gap: The VCT value EWC_LPID_Attestation is defined by the EWC consortium (RFC005). The ARF does not yet define an official LPID VCT. A future standardised value (potentially eu.europa.ec.eudi.lpid.1, following the naming convention of the natural person PID) may supersede the EWC value. RPs should implement VCT matching using a configurable allowlist rather than hardcoded string comparison.
RFC005 §6.10.3 provides the JSON Schema for LPID credential subject validation. The credential_subject object requires exactly two fields:

RFC005 does not define an mdoc profile for LPID. The EWC specification focuses exclusively on SD-JWT VC. For EUDI ecosystem proximity use cases, an mdoc encoding would likely use docType value eu.europa.ec.eudi.lpid.1 (analogous to eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1), but this is not standardised.

Specification gap: The absence of an mdoc LPID profile means proximity-based LPID presentation is not currently standardised. RPs expecting proximity-based legal person verification should plan for SD-JWT VC over remote flows or await mdoc LPID standardisation.
The ARF defines a three-layer architecture for attestation governance:
passages.headingText: 6.16 Rulebook Discovery via Catalogue of Schemes (TS11)

passages.text: Governance document — attributes, encoding, usage, trust anchors, revocation, compliance
passages.headingText: 6.16 Rulebook Discovery via Catalogue of Schemes (TS11)

passages.text: Machine-readable (JSON schema)
passages.headingText: 6.16 Rulebook Discovery via Catalogue of Schemes (TS11)

passages.text: Software-consumable version of the Rulebook — data model, namespaces, attribute types
passages.headingText: 6.16 Rulebook Discovery via Catalogue of Schemes (TS11)

passages.text: Discovery service where Wallet Units and RPs find and validate attestation schemas
passages.headingText: 6.16 Rulebook Discovery via Catalogue of Schemes (TS11)

passages.text: Rulebooks are for humans (product managers, compliance teams), while Schemes are for software (the RP's verification pipeline ingests them at runtime). The Catalogue is the discovery mechanism that bridges the two. TS11 (v1.0.1, January 2026) defines the complete Catalogue infrastructure.
passages.headingText: 6.16 Rulebook Discovery via Catalogue of Schemes (TS11)

passages.text: The SchemaMeta class is the core data model an RP consumes from the Catalogue of Schemes API:
Unique schema identifier — used as cache key
Version tracking — cache invalidation trigger
Link to the human-readable Rulebook; RP can retrieve for compliance documentation
Critical — resolves to trust anchors (§6.16.2)
Level of security: iso_18045_high / moderate / enhanced-basic / basic
claim, key, biometric, or none — determines KB-JWT verification requirement
dc+sd-jwt, mso_mdoc, jwt_vc_json, jwt_vc_json-ld, ldp_vc
Persistent URIs for format-specific JSON schemas — used for structural validation
The TrustAuthority object within SchemaMeta.trustedAuthorities[] determines which trust verification path the RP must follow:
Base64URL-encoded Authority Key Identifier
Direct X.509 trust — RP matches AKI to cached issuer certificate
Standard TL — QEAAs, PuB-EAAs. Same path as PID verification (§5.5)
LoTE — non-qualified EAAs with voluntary trust registry
OID-FED — non-qualified EAAs only. Trust chain resolution per §5.5.5

Cross-reference: The three trust verification paths correspond to the branching logic described in §5.5.9 (Non-Qualified EAA Issuer Trust). The frameworkType value is the machine-readable equivalent of the trust model diagram in §7.2.3.
The Catalogue of Schemes exposes an OpenAPI 3.1–compatible REST API:
Parameterised query — filter by id, supportedformat, attestationlos, bindingtype, trustedAuthoritiesFrameworkType, schemauri, rulebookuri
Retrieve specific SchemaMeta by UUID
All GET endpoints are publicly accessible (no authentication needed for read access). Responses are JWS-signed and paginated.
When an RP receives an attestation type it has not previously verified, the following discovery flow applies:
Extract identifier — from the VP: vct claim (SD-JWT VC) or docType (mdoc)
Query Catalogue — GET /schemas?supportedformat=dc+sd-jwt with the VCT or format as filter
Resolve trust authority — read trustedAuthorities[].frameworkType to determine the verification path (X.509 AKI, Trusted List, LoTE, or OID-FED)
Validate against schema — fetch the JSON schema from schemaURIs[].uri; validate the VP payload for structural correctness
Apply Rulebook obligations — retrieve the Rulebook from rulebookURI; check Chapters 4–6 for PID co-verification, binding, and revocation requirements
The Catalogue API has rate limits. RPs should implement:
TTL-based caching: Cache SchemaMeta locally with a configurable TTL (e.g., 24 hours)
Version-based invalidation: When SchemaMeta.version changes, invalidate the cached entry and re-fetch
Schema pre-fetching: For known attestation types (PID, mDL), pre-fetch and cache schemas at startup
Graceful degradation: If the API is unreachable, fall back to the most recently cached SchemaMeta
Source: TS11 v1.0.1 (January 2026), Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/1569.

The following profiles are projected based on Large-Scale Pilot (LSP) outputs, the Rulebook template v1.4 structure, and Discussion Paper O. No sector-specific Rulebooks are published in the EC catalogue as of March 2026. RPs should treat these as planning aids — the actual Rulebook, when published, is the authoritative source. Cross-reference: §28 (LSP table).
Healthcare is a priority use case validated by the POTENTIAL Large-Scale Pilot (cross-border ePrescription flows).
Electronic prescription for cross-border pharmacy dispensation
SD-JWT VC + mdoc (offline pharmacy)
Health Professional Card (HPC)
Proves healthcare professional qualification
Emergency medical data (allergies, conditions, medications)
PID co-verification (ARB_27) — mandatory
Healthcare RPs must verify patient identity alongside health credentials
cryptographically_bound_to PID (ARB_28)
Health credentials likely required to be device-bound and bound to PID
Proximity/offline support (ARB_02)
Pharmacies, ambulances, and emergency rooms may lack connectivity → mdoc format likely mandatory
Health data is special category — RP needs explicit lawful basis
Data minimisation enforcement
Strict selective disclosure — e.g., age verification only, not full PID

Trust model: Trusted Lists (QEAA/PuB-EAA model). Healthcare credential providers would be national health authorities or QTSPs. MyHealth@EU may serve as an additional trust intermediary. Timeline: POTENTIAL LSP completed pilot testing (2024–2025). No published Rulebook. Expected: post CIR 2025/1569 implementation (late 2026).
Education has the most advanced existing infrastructure (EDCL, EBSI, Europass) and is the sector most likely to produce early Rulebooks.
University degree certificate
Short learning achievement (course, certificate)
Standardised EU-wide learning credential (EDCL)
PID co-verification — recommended
Likely SHOULD (not SHALL) — to bind diploma to holder
RP (employer, university) should verify the issuing institution is accredited
Diplomas may have long validity but should be checkable (institution closure, degree revocation)
European Qualifications Framework (EQF) level should be verifiable

Trust model: Education is the one sector where EBSI/DID may be relevant. DC4EU tested EBSI-backed credentials with did:ebsi identifiers (§7.3.2). Possible bifurcated trust: LoTE (ETSI TS 119 602) for EBSI-backed trust — alongside standard Trusted Lists for QEAA credentials. RPs may need to implement both paths. Timeline: DC4EU concluded July 2025. EBSI Verifiable Attestation schema supported since Feb 2024. No published Rulebook. Expected: among the first sector-specific Rulebooks.
Travel credentials operate on a parallel regulatory track — the DTC has its own regulation (COM/2024/670, COM/2024/671) and follows ICAO/ISO standards rather than the Attestation Rulebook template.
Digital Travel Credential (DTC)
mdoc (ICAO 9303 / ISO 23220-2)
Age/identity for hotel registration
Age/identity + driving qualification
PID co-verification — mandatory
Border control entities must verify identity
Proximity/offline support (ARB_02)
Border gates, hotel lobbies may use NFC/BLE → mdoc likely mandatory for DTC
DTC must align with ICAO 9303 Machine Readable Travel Document standards
DTC likely includes facial image — live face matching possible

Trust model: Government Trusted Lists (Member State issuing authority). DTC follows ISO 23220-2 standards track. Timeline: DTC regulatory proposals published (2024). EWC pilot completed. No published Rulebook. DTC more likely to follow ISO standards track than the Attestation Rulebook template.
Social security credentials are critical for cross-border worker mobility, driven by the DC4EU Large-Scale Pilot.
Confirms social security coverage for cross-border workers
European Health Insurance Card replacement
Entitlement to specific social benefits
PID co-verification — mandatory
Social security credentials must be bound to verified identity
Issuing Member State verification
RP must verify which Member State is responsible for coverage
PDA1 must be recognised across all Member States
Administrative processes are predominantly online

Trust model: PuB-EAA model — national social security institutions issue via QTSP-signed certificates, verified against Trusted Lists. EESSI (Electronic Exchange of Social Security Information) integration for backend validation. Timeline: DC4EU piloted PDA1 digitalisation (2024–2025). No published Rulebook. Expected: among the later sector-specific Rulebooks.
Most current RP implementations hardcode their verification logic for PID. When sector-specific Rulebooks arrive, this approach fails to scale:
Maintenance burden — each new Rulebook requires code changes to the verification pipeline
Trust model fragmentation — healthcare uses Trusted Lists, education may use EBSI/LoTE, travel uses government TLs
Format heterogeneity — some attestations are mdoc-only (mDL, DTC), some SD-JWT VC-only, some both
The solution is a Rulebook-driven verification architecture that dynamically adapts based on the SchemaMeta retrieved from the Catalogue of Schemes (§6.16).
Cache SchemaMeta locally vs. always query
Cache with TTL (e.g., 24h), invalidate on version change. The Catalogue API has rate limits (§6.16.5).
Hardcode vs. pluggable trust resolvers
Pluggable — implement TrustResolver interface with X509AKIResolver, TrustedListResolver, LoTEResolver, OpenIDFederationResolver implementations
Separate pipelines vs. unified
Separate: mdoc (§11.4) and SD-JWT VC (§11.3) parsing are fundamentally different. Use a FormatHandler abstraction.
Always verify PID vs. per-Rulebook
Per-Rulebook (check Rulebook Ch. 4). Healthcare sectors mandate it; education recommends it; some sectors don't require it.
Static rules vs. Rulebook-derived
Hybrid: static rules for regulation-mandated obligations (revocation check), Rulebook-derived for sector-specific (PID co-verification, binding type).
When a new sector-specific Rulebook is published, an RP should follow this 5-step process:
Register — query the Catalogue of Schemes (§6.16.3) for the new VCT/DocType to obtain the SchemaMeta
Configure trust — extract trustedAuthorities[] from SchemaMeta; add the corresponding trust resolver to the pipeline (e.g., add a LoTEResolver for education credentials using EBSI/LoTE)
Configure schema — download the JSON schema from schemaURIs[].uri; add format-specific validation rules (SD-JWT VC claims, mdoc namespace/elementIdentifiers)
Configure obligations — read Rulebook Chapters 4–6; set flags for PID co-verification (ARB_27), cryptographically_bound_to (ARB_28), device binding type (ARB_34), and revocation mechanism
Test — validate end-to-end with the LSP/pilot reference implementation (if available) or the EU Reference Wallet (§27)

RP planning impact: Implement the pluggable trust resolution module and format routing abstraction now, even while only PID and mDL Rulebooks are published. When sector-specific Rulebooks arrive, onboarding a new attestation type becomes a configuration change rather than a code change.

§6.18.4 addresses the RP's perspective when consuming a new attestation type. This section addresses the complementary question: how should an organisation design an EAA project from scratch — whether as an issuer, a sector body publishing a Rulebook, or a dual-role RP that both issues and consumes attestations?
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: The critical first-order decision is not about credential formats or technical architecture — it is about which regulatory regime the attestation falls under. Only after the regulatory regime is determined do source verification, issuer governance, wallet issuance, registration, publication, technical modelling, and status/revocation follow.
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Three regulatory regimes: Within the EUDI ecosystem, four categories of attestations exist (PID, QEAA, PuB-EAA, and non-qualified EAA), but for EAA project design the distinction collapses to three regimes:
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Risk management (CIR 2025/2160); personal data separation (Art. 45b(3)); no qualified legal claim
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Qualified Trust Service Provider (QTSP)
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Full QTSP obligations; identity verification under Art. 24 eIDAS; conformity assessment; CIR 2025/1569 revocation framework
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Public sector body responsible for an authentic source (or on its behalf)
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Notification to Commission (CIR 2025/1569 Art. 5); Commission-published provider list (Art. 6); conformity assessment equivalent to QTSP
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Design rule: The same attestation content (e.g., a diploma, a professional licence) may fall into a different regulatory regime depending on who issues it. A university directly issuing diplomas into wallets operates under the PuB-EAA regime; a commercial credential aggregator issuing the same diploma attestation on the university's behalf would likely need a QTSP designation under the QEAA regime.
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Design considerations every EAA project must address:
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Regulatory regime: Non-qualified EAA, QEAA, or PuB-EAA — without this determination, downstream decisions cannot be made correctly
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Subject, attributes, and source: Which person or entity is the subject, which attributes are attested, from which authentic source each attribute is obtained, and how the issuer verifies correctness before issuance (for QEAAs, this is part of the issuance regime per CIR 2025/1569 Art. 9)
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: EUDI Wallet issuance scope: Whether the attestation must be issued into an EUDI Wallet — if yes, CIR 2024/2977 applies (format, namespace, binding); if the attestation targets a proprietary wallet, the EUDI legal framework does not automatically apply
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Attestation Rulebook and scheme registration: A human-readable specification plus a machine-readable definition (§6.16), with attestation type and namespace registered in the Catalogue of Schemes (§6.16.3)
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Trust infrastructure: How issuing parties, relying parties, trust anchors, and registrations are published and discovered — including RP registration (§4), access certificates (§5.2), and LoTE publication (§5.5)
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Validity and revocation model: Short-lived, ASL, ARL, or hybrid — chosen early in the design, not retrofitted (see Appendix B.1 for the binding legal framework)
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: QEAA identity verification hardening: For QEAAs, the main identity verification obligation derives from eIDAS Art. 24: before issuing a qualified certificate or QEAA, the QTSP must verify the identity and relevant attributes of the person concerned. CIR 2025/1566 makes this concrete by designating ETSI TS 119 461 V2.1.1 (2025-02) as the reference standard, specifically Annex C, clause C.3, with EU-specific adaptations. CIR 2025/1566 entered into force but becomes applicable on 19 August 2027. Specific hardenings include:
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Identity proofing connected to authoritative evidence must be peer reviewed or certified at assurance level high under eIDAS, or comply with ETSI TS 119 461 C3.1–C3.6
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Independent conformity assessment bodies must be accredited; certification must reference eIDAS assurance levels for notified eID means or certified EUDI Wallets
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Fully automated identity proofing requires defined target FAR/FRR values based on risk analysis; values may not be weaker than for comparable hybrid cases
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Physical identity document validation must be tested by an accredited laboratory or national authority by 19 August 2027 and every two years thereafter
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: RP impact: For RPs that also operate as QEAAs issuers (dual-role organisations), these identity verification hardening requirements directly affect onboarding flows. Where the user already holds a PID in the EUDI Wallet, the QTSP can perform identification at LoA high by requesting and verifying PID attributes — a practical design pattern documented in ARF v2.8.0, though not an independent legal exemption from Art. 24.
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: Recommended sequencing: Since downstream requirements cascade from upstream decisions, the design considerations above should be addressed in roughly this order: (1) determine the regulatory regime, (2) decide EUDI Wallet scope and applicable CIRs, (3) define the attribute model and source verification chain, (4) design the Rulebook and register the attestation scheme, (5) select the validity/revocation model, and — for QEAAs specifically — design the issuance flow against Art. 24 + CIR 2025/1566 from the outset rather than treating identity verification as an afterthought.
passages.headingText: 6.18.5 EAA Project Design Framework: Regulatory Regime First

passages.text: A common question for RP integration architects is: does the EUDI Wallet ecosystem use Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs), and does my RP need to support DID resolution? This is particularly relevant because several non-EU digital identity systems — including the Swiss swiyu wallet and the EBSI infrastructure — use DIDs, and some commercial wallet SDKs advertise DID support as a feature.
This chapter provides a definitive answer by examining:
The ARF's official position on X.509 vs. DIDs, including the authoritative clarification from the ARF team (§7.2)
The DID methods that exist in the European identity landscape and their relevance to the EUDI ecosystem (§7.3)
The trust model used by every category of wallet an RP may encounter: platform wallets like Apple Wallet and Google Wallet (§7.4), national EUDI wallets (§7.5), and commercial/SSI wallets (§7.6)
The W3C Digital Credentials API browser mediation layer that connects RPs to wallets (§7.7)
A practical RP decision matrix answering "do you need DID support?" for each wallet type (§7.8)

A Decentralized Identifier (DID) is a globally unique URI (e.g., did:web:example.com, did:ebsi:z123...) that resolves to a DID Document containing public keys, service endpoints, and authentication methods. Unlike X.509 certificates — which derive trust from a hierarchical Certificate Authority chain — DIDs derive trust from the DID method's resolution mechanism (DNS, blockchain, distributed hash table, etc.). The W3C DID Core v1.0 specification achieved Recommendation status in July 2022; v1.1 is a Candidate Recommendation (March 2026).

Relationship to other chapters: This chapter builds on §5 (Trust Infrastructure — certificates, trusted lists, LoTEs) and §6 (Credential Formats — SD-JWT VC, mdoc, Rulebooks). It must be read before §8 (OpenID4VP), which discusses client_id_scheme = x509_hash — the mandatory RP authentication mechanism whose rationale is explained here. For RPs considering non-qualified EAA acceptance or interoperability with Member States that use OpenID Federation for RP trust (notably Italy), see §5.5.5–§5.5.9 for the OID-FED protocol, trust chain resolution, and the dual trust model precedent.
The ARF Trust Model (§6.1) establishes the foundational rule:

"For PIDs, qualified EAAs, PuB-EAAs, access certificates, and registration certificates, interoperability is essential (§4.2.3). Interoperability is achieved by using a PKI following X.509 certificate standards ([RFC5280], [RFC3647]). Non-qualified EAAs may adopt alternative trust models and verification mechanisms." — ARF Main Document, §6.1
This rule is reinforced by the high-level requirement RPA_02 (Annex 2, Topic 6): Wallet Units and Relying Party Instances must support X.509 certificates for authentication.

The question of DID inclusion was formally raised in GitHub Issue #288 — "Request for inclusion of Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs) in the ARF" — filed on 2024-07-09 by representatives of the Decentralized Identity Foundation (DIF), the Digital Credentials Consortium (DCC), and the Trust over IP Foundation. The issue received 94 reactions (49 👍, 11 🎉, 20 ❤️, 14 🚀) and 26 substantive comments before being closed on 2025-05-15.
The official ARF team response (December 2024, from a contributor with the CONTRIBUTOR role) stated:

"Whether this is possible depends on the legal attestation type. For PIDs, qualified EAAs, and PuB-EAAs, a primary objective is interoperability (see ARF §4.1.3). This means we need a single trust model that is supported by all actors and components in the ecosystem. Therefore, these types of attestations will be implemented using X509 certificates and 'classical' X509-based Certificate Authorities according to [RFC5280] and [RFC3647]. The same is true for non-qualified EAAs complying with ISO/IEC 18013-5, because the use of X.509 certificates is specified in that standard. However, for non-qualified EAAs complying with [SD-JWT VC], other trust frameworks may be used, including ones that use DIDs. This will be clarified in §6.1 of the ARF."
The following flowchart shows the decision path an RP follows to determine whether DID resolution is needed for a given attestation:

RP planning impact: The overwhelmingly common case is the left branch — PIDs, QEAAs, PuB-EAAs, and ISO 18013-5 credentials all route to "X.509 only." The amber branch (DID possible) is reached only for non-qualified EAAs using SD-JWT VC with a custom Rulebook or W3C VCDM 2.0. As of March 2026, no production Rulebook mandates DID-based trust — but this may change as domain-specific ecosystems (e.g., educational credentials, health credentials) mature.
PID (Person Identification Data)
X.509 PKI — Trusted Lists / LoTEs
X.509 PKI — QTSP Trusted Lists
X.509 PKI — qualified certificate on issuer
Non-qualified EAA (ISO/IEC 18013-5 format)
Non-qualified EAA (SD-JWT VC format)
Alternative trust frameworks allowed
Non-qualified EAA (W3C VCDM 2.0 format)
Optional format; Rulebook defines trust
X.509 PKI per the ISO standard
X.509 for qualified; alternative trust for non-qualified
❌ Optional (non-qualified only)
The rationale, as documented in ARF §5.2.3, §7.1, and the GitHub Issue #288 discussion:
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: Interoperability mandate: eIDAS 2.0 and ARF §5.1.3 require a single trust model for PIDs, QEAAs, and PuB-EAAs that all 30 participating countries (27 EU + 3 EEA/EFTA) can implement uniformly. A single trust model eliminates the combinatorial explosion of method-specific resolution, verification, and key management that would arise from supporting multiple DID methods.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: Interoperability mandate: eIDAS 2.0 and ARF §5.1.3 require a single trust model for PIDs, QEAAs, and PuB-EAAs that all 30 participating countries (27 EU + 3 EEA/EFTA) can implement uniformly. A single trust model eliminates the combinatorial explosion of method-specific resolution, verification, and key management that would arise from supporting multiple DID methods.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: Existing PKI infrastructure: EU Member States already operate qualified trust service provider (QTSP) PKI infrastructure under eIDAS 1.0. X.509 builds on this existing investment — the trust anchors (Trusted Lists, LoTEs) described in §5.5 are inherently X.509-based.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: Existing PKI infrastructure: EU Member States already operate qualified trust service provider (QTSP) PKI infrastructure under eIDAS 1.0. X.509 builds on this existing investment — the trust anchors (Trusted Lists, LoTEs) described in §5.5 are inherently X.509-based.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: ETSI standards alignment: ETSI TS 119 475 (access certificates) and ETSI TS 119 411-8 define X.509 profiles specifically for the EUDI ecosystem. These standards are referenced by the CIRs and cannot be replaced without regulatory action.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: ETSI standards alignment: ETSI TS 119 475 (access certificates) and ETSI TS 119 411-8 define X.509 profiles specifically for the EUDI ecosystem. These standards are referenced by the CIRs and cannot be replaced without regulatory action.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: ISO/IEC 18013-5 compatibility: The mobile driving licence standard mandates X.509 reader authentication certificates (IACA — Issuing Authority CA). Using DIDs would break interoperability with deployed mDL infrastructure in 30+ countries.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: ISO/IEC 18013-5 compatibility: The mobile driving licence standard mandates X.509 reader authentication certificates (IACA — Issuing Authority CA). Using DIDs would break interoperability with deployed mDL infrastructure in 30+ countries.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: DID method proliferation risk: The W3C DID registry lists 100+ registered DID methods, each with different trust assumptions, resolution infrastructure, key management, and security guarantees. Mandating DID support would require selecting specific methods — which itself fragments the ecosystem and creates governance complexity that X.509 PKI avoids.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: DID method proliferation risk: The W3C DID registry lists 100+ registered DID methods, each with different trust assumptions, resolution infrastructure, key management, and security guarantees. Mandating DID support would require selecting specific methods — which itself fragments the ecosystem and creates governance complexity that X.509 PKI avoids.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: The core philosophical position is: identity in the eIDAS model structurally stems from sovereign states — the EUDI trust model is anchored in government-operated Trusted Lists and LoTEs (§5.5), which inherently use X.509 PKI. DIDs, by contrast, enable self-sovereign or organisationally-sovereign identity — a fundamentally different trust paradigm.
passages.headingText: 7.2.6 Why the Mandatory Core Uses X.509

passages.text: While the EUDI mandatory core does not use DIDs, several European identity ecosystems do. RPs should be aware of these methods because: (a) non-qualified EAA Rulebooks may reference them, (b) EBSI pilot credentials use them, and (c) the Swiss swiyu ecosystem uses did:webvh for its entire trust infrastructure.
passages.headingText: 7.3 DID Methods in the European Landscape

passages.text: Self-contained — public key encoded in DID
Self-contained — JWK embedded in DID
DNS + verifiable history log
EBSI permissioned blockchain
Distributed Hash Table (Mainline DHT)
The European Blockchain Services Infrastructure (EBSI) — operated by the European Commission — has two DID profiles:
passages.headingText: 7.3.2 EBSI: The EU's Own DID Infrastructure

passages.text: Legal Entities: DID documents stored on EBSI's permissioned ledger (Hyperledger Besu). Supports key rotation. ~40 European public bodies participate.
passages.headingText: 7.3.2 EBSI: The EU's Own DID Infrastructure

passages.text: Natural Persons: DID documents generated and stored only in the wallet (not on ledger) — effectively a did:key profile to preserve privacy.
passages.headingText: 7.3.2 EBSI: The EU's Own DID Infrastructure

passages.text: The DC4EU Large-Scale Pilot (Digital Credentials for Europe — educational credentials, social security) is actively evaluating tools to integrate EBSI capabilities — specifically did:ebsi and did:key — into the EUDI Wallet Reference Implementation. This evaluation will determine whether EBSI DIDs can coexist with the X.509 trust model in a single wallet.
passages.headingText: 7.3.2 EBSI: The EU's Own DID Infrastructure

passages.text: Switzerland occupies a unique position: an EFTA member but not an EEA member, it develops its own e-ID system under Swiss federal law (not eIDAS 2.0). The swiyu wallet uses did:webvh (DID Web + Verifiable History) — an enhancement of did:web that adds a verifiable audit trail (did.jsonl log file) and self-certifying identifiers (SCIDs) without blockchain dependency.
Credential format: SD-JWT VC — the same format used in the EUDI ecosystem
Trust infrastructure: swiyu Base Registry (public registry of DIDs, public keys, revocation lists) + swiyu Trust Registry (trust statements certifying legitimate issuers/verifiers)
Timeline: Beta testing started March 2025; full launch from summer 2026
Interoperability: swiyu uses SD-JWT VC and OpenID4VC, matching EUDI at the protocol level — but differs at the trust anchoring level (DID-based Base Registry vs. X.509-based Trusted Lists)

Switzerland is negotiating bilateral eIDAS mutual recognition separately from the Bilaterals III package (signed March 2026). If this mutual recognition is achieved, RPs interoperating with Swiss entities may need to handle did:webvh-based trust anchors alongside the X.509-based LoTEs described in §5.5.

RP planning impact: Swiss cross-border interoperability is not yet a reality. Monitor the eIDAS mutual recognition negotiations (mandate preparation initiated January 2025) — if concluded, it may require RPs to support did:webvh resolution for Swiss-issued credentials. Until then, no action is required.

Platform wallets represent the wallets with the largest installed base — hundreds of millions of users already have them on their devices. Their trust model directly impacts RP integration requirements because they may be the first wallet type a user presents.
passages.headingText: 7.4 Platform Wallets: Apple Wallet and Google Wallet

passages.text: Credential format: Apple Wallet exclusively supports the mdoc format (ISO/IEC 18013-5). There is no SD-JWT VC support and no DID support of any kind.
Current capabilities (as of iOS 26, Fall 2025):
Mobile Driver's Licences (mDL) in multiple U.S. states; Japan My Number Card
"Verify with Wallet on the Web" — uses the W3C Digital Credentials API (§7.7) with the org-iso-mdoc protocol (ISO/IEC 18013-7 Annex C)
Passport storage as digital ID derived from mdoc format
NFC Secure Element opened to third-party developers (iOS 18.1, August 2024)

EUDI integration status: Apple has not announced providing an EUDI Wallet itself. However, Apple's Secure Platform Element could serve as a WSCD (Wallet Secure Cryptographic Device) for third-party EUDI wallet apps, and the NFC opening enables third-party EUDI apps to leverage Apple hardware for proximity flows. Safari 26 (September 2025) shipped W3C Digital Credentials API support — enabling web-based credential presentation from Apple Wallet and third-party wallets.
DID relevance: ❌ Zero. Apple Wallet operates entirely within the ISO/IEC 18013-5 stack using X.509 IACA (Issuing Authority CA) certificates.
Credential format: Google Wallet supports mdoc for digital IDs. The Android Credential Manager supports both mdoc and W3C Verifiable Credentials formats, though Google Wallet itself focuses on mdoc.
Current capabilities (as of Android 16, 2025):
Digital IDs (mDL, state IDs) in multiple U.S. states
Zero Knowledge Proof (ZKP) technology for age verification (announced Google I/O 2025; planned for open-source release)
Native OpenID4VP support built into Android (April 2025) — the EUDI-mandated presentation protocol
Android Credential Manager provides the DigitalCredential API for credential presentation
Chrome 141 (September 2025) shipped stable W3C Digital Credentials API support

EUDI integration status: Google has built native OpenID4VP directly into Android — this is the exact protocol the EUDI ecosystem mandates for remote attestation presentation. The Android platform acts as a selector/mediator: any EUDI-compliant wallet app on Android can present credentials via the system credential selector.

DID relevance: ❌ No DID support in Google Wallet's digital ID implementation. Google Wallet uses mdoc with X.509 issuer certificates. However, the Android platform provides OpenID4VP infrastructure that is protocol-agnostic — a third-party wallet app on Android could theoretically use DIDs if the credential Rulebook permits it (§7.2.3, amber branch).
The following diagram shows the wallet ecosystem grouped by category and trust model:
passages.headingText: 7.4.3 Wallet Landscape and Trust Model Overview

passages.text: RP planning impact: An RP wanting to accept credentials from Apple Wallet or Google Wallet does not need DID resolution infrastructure. Both platforms operate entirely in the ISO/IEC 18013-5 (mdoc) stack with X.509 issuer authentication. The RP authenticates using the same X.509-based x509_hash client ID scheme described in §8.3.

The security of every EUDI Wallet depends on how and where its cryptographic keys are stored and used. The ARF (§5.5.2) defines four Wallet Secure Cryptographic Device (WSCD) architecture types. While the RP never directly inspects the WSCD type — trust is established indirectly via Wallet Unit Attestations and Trusted Lists (§5, §11.9) — understanding these architectures is important for ecosystem awareness, availability planning, and user-support scenarios.
HSM on a secure server operated by the Wallet Provider

Requires connectivity for every cryptographic operation (signing, presentation). Proximity flows (§13) may need online fallback. Key recovery and multi-device support are simpler because keys are not device-bound.
Smart card, hardware token, FIDO authenticator
User must have the physical device present. High assurance but adds friction. Common for national eID card integration.
Inside user device (separate chip)
SIM/eSIM (eUICC), embedded Secure Element (eSE)
Hardware-bound keys; works offline. Security depends on the SE certification level. Tied to the specific device — no cross-device portability.
Inside user device (OS-managed)
TEE / Secure Enclave (Apple) / StrongBox (Android)
Most common consumer deployment. Keys managed via OS APIs. Security level depends on device model and OS version. Works offline.

"Cloud wallet": Industry marketing terminology (used by multiple infrastructure vendors and telcos) for a wallet that uses a Remote WSCD architecture. The term is potentially confusing because it does not mean the wallet application runs in the cloud — only the WSCD (key storage and signing) is cloud-hosted. The wallet app itself still runs on the user's device.

Hybrid architectures are expected in production: several Wallet Providers are combining a Remote WSCD (cloud HSM for high-assurance signing) with a Local Native WSCD (device TEE/StrongBox for access control and session authentication). The user authenticates locally on the device, which then authorises the remote HSM to perform the actual signing operation.

RP planning impact: The WSCD architecture is transparent to the RP. All four types produce the same OID4VP protocol output — the RP's verification backend does not need to branch based on WSCD type. However, RPs should be aware that users with Remote WSCD wallets require network connectivity even for proximity flows, and users with Local External WSCDs (smart cards) need physical token presence. These operational differences may affect UX design and error-handling guidance (§11.6).
All national EUDI wallet implementations currently in development follow the ARF trust model (X.509 PKI). None use DIDs for core identity flows.
Open-source; architecture reference, not production
First MS to launch; integrated in IO app
Frontrunner; building on existing eID
MoU with 75+ organisations (Nov 2025); Lissi won Challenge
Pilot (first data test Jan 2025)
Target 2027; open-source (EUPL); Rust + Flutter
Linked to DNIe; DC4EU LSP coordination
Production (national); EUDI target end 2025
21.7M users; sole non-EU member in POTENTIAL LSP

RP planning impact: No national wallet requires DID support. RPs can rely entirely on the X.509-based trust chain described in §5 for verifying credentials from any national EUDI wallet. This includes Ukraine's Diia, which — despite being a non-EU wallet — aligns with the ARF trust model.

Unlike platform and national wallets, the commercial SSI wallet ecosystem often supports DIDs alongside ARF compliance. These wallets maintain dual-mode operation: X.509 for EUDI mandatory flows, and DID resolution for EBSI, SSI, or custom Rulebook flows.
Infrastructure SDK; used across all Phase 1 LSPs
Won German EUDI-Wallet Challenge; Connector for RP integration
did:ebsi, did:web, did:ethr, did:dht
Open-source; bridges EBSI and SSI ecosystems
Berlin-based; e-government focus; less strict ARF alignment

Key insight: These wallets demonstrate that DID support and ARF compliance are not mutually exclusive. A wallet can resolve DIDs for EBSI-issued educational credentials while simultaneously using X.509 for government-issued PIDs. For the RP, this is transparent — the RP always verifies using the trust model dictated by the attestation type (§7.2.3), not by the wallet's internal capabilities.

RP planning impact: Even when the user's wallet is a DID-capable commercial wallet, the RP's verification path for PIDs and QEAAs remains X.509. The RP may encounter DIDs only if it accepts non-qualified EAAs from providers that use EBSI-based or custom trust frameworks. In that case, the Attestation Rulebook (§6.11) will specify the required DID method and resolution mechanism.

The W3C Digital Credentials API (DC-API) is the browser-level JavaScript API that mediates between RP websites and wallet apps during remote credential presentation. Understanding this layer is critical because it is the actual interface an RP's front-end code calls — and it is entirely trust-model-agnostic.

The Digital Credentials API does not exist in isolation — it is one of four credential type extensions built on top of the W3C Credential Management API (Credential Management Level 1), the base framework specification that defines the navigator.credentials interface and the CredentialsContainer methods (get(), create(), store()). Each extension registers a new credential type and option key with this framework:
navigator.credentials Method
Passkeys / FIDO2 authenticators
Wallet → RP credential presentation

All four extensions share the same navigator.credentials entry point but are mutually independent — they do not interact, cannot be combined in a single get() call, and serve fundamentally different identity layers. The option key determines which extension handles the request:

RP relevance: For EUDI Wallet integration, the RP uses only the digital option key. WebAuthn (publicKey) becomes relevant when the RP implements pseudonym-based authentication (§16) — where the initial EUDI presentation is bound to a WebAuthn passkey for subsequent logins. FedCM (identity) and PasswordCredential (password) are unrelated to the EUDI ecosystem. The four extensions coexist on the same API surface but address completely separate authentication and identity use cases.

Unified credential selector: The W3C Federated Identity Working Group is exploring a unified browser credential selector that could surface passkeys, federated accounts, and verifiable credentials in a common picker — but this is a UX-level convergence, not an API merger, and has no committed timeline.

As described in §7.7.1, the DC-API extends navigator.credentials via the digital option key. The browser acts as a trusted mediator: the RP website does not communicate directly with the wallet. Instead:
The RP calls navigator.credentials.get() with protocol-specific parameters
The browser validates the calling origin and delegates to the OS credential manager
The OS shows a credential selector UI listing eligible credentials from all installed wallets
The user selects a credential; the wallet app creates and signs a Verifiable Presentation
The response flows back through the OS → browser → RP chain
✅ Shipped (default Oct 2025)
OpenID4VP, ISO 18013-7 Annex C
Same-device + cross-device via QR
OpenID4VP, ISO 18013-7 Annex C
Mozilla cites privacy/interop concerns

Safari protocol limitation: Safari 26's DC-API implementation supports only the org-iso-mdoc protocol (ISO 18013-7 Annex C). It does not support the openid4vp protocol used for SD-JWT VC presentation. This means same-device SD-JWT VC flows will not work on Safari — the wallet must use mdoc format, or the RP must fall back to cross-device flows (§9). This limitation is also noted in §27.1 (W3C DC API Browser Support Matrix).
The following sequence diagram shows the full browser-mediated credential presentation flow, illustrating how the trust model (X.509 vs. DID) is resolved:
1. Relying Party calls navigator.credentials.get()

The Relying Party initiates the presentation flow by constructing a standard OpenID4VP request packaged as a signed JWT Secured Authorization Request (JAR). To ensure the browser mediates this request securely without revealing installed wallets, the Relying Party invokes the W3C Digital Credentials API (DC-API).
Inside the SIGNED_JAR, the Relying Party defines its exact presentation requirements:
client_id: Uses the x509_hash://sha-256;BASE64 scheme to securely embed a hash of its WRPAC. This serves as a critical defense mechanism against identity spoofing.
dcql_query: A Digital Credential Query Language payload specifying the required attributes (e.g., given_name, age_over_18).
response_mode: Set to direct_post.jwt to ensure the response is wrapped in JARM (JWS/JWE).
nonce: A crypto-random value used for anti-replay verification during the device binding phase.
Artifact Produced: OpenID4VP JAR (JWT Secured Authorization Request) and cryptographic nonce (stored in Relying Party session state).
Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a DC_API_PRESENTATION_REQUESTED event.

Why use the DC-API? Directly utilizing deep-links or custom URL schemes allows covert tracking by arbitrary applications. The DC-API ensures the request is handled natively by the OS, explicitly preventing the Relying Party from profiling the User's installed wallets.
2. Browser forwards credential request to OS Credential Manager
The Browser intercepts the API call and immediately enforces strict, client-side security heuristics:
Origin Validation: Ensures the calling script matches the verified top-level domain, preventing Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) subversion.
Permissions Policy: Checks if the digital-credentials-get permission is granted (e.g., preventing cross-site iframe abuse).
Protocol Registry: Confirms openid4vp is a registered and supported protocol.
Failure Path: If the origin is untrusted or permissions are missing, the Browser rejects the promise with a SecurityError and halts the flow immediately.
Upon successful validation, the Browser passes the raw JAR payload to the underlying operating system's Credential Manager module (such as the Android IdentityCredential API or iOS Wallet framework).
3. OS Credential Manager invokes Wallet selector

The OS natively enumerates all installed wallet applications capable of handling the requested protocol (e.g., openid4vp). However, to preserve strict privacy boundaries, the OS does not natively inspect individual credentials to filter them.
Instead, the filtering logic is delegated to WebAssembly (Wasm) Matchers:
When a Wallet Instance installs, it registers a sandboxed Wasm matcher binary alongside its credentials.
The OS feeds the incoming Relying Party request into these isolated Wasm sandboxes.
The Wallet Instance's Wasm logic securely evaluates the payload against its own credentials and returns a matching list to the OS.
The OS uses this output to dynamically populate the native selector UI (bottom sheet) before the wallet application is ever launched.
Failure Path: If no matching credentials are found across any wallet, the OS returns an empty result to the Browser, which rejects the Relying Party's request.

Privacy Guarantee: Neither the Browser nor the Relying Party is informed about which wallets the User has installed. Simultaneously, the Operating System is kept entirely blind to the sensitive cryptographic payload and the User's attributes.
4. Wallet Instance verifies Relying Party identity and requests User consent
The selected Wallet Instance receives the OpenID4VP JAR from the OS. Before displaying anything to the User, the Wallet Instance performs strict identity verification on the Relying Party:
JAR Signature Evaluation: The Wallet Instance cryptographically verifies the JAR signature against the Relying Party's public key (WRPAC).
x509_hash Alignment: The Wallet Instance ensures the client_id hash matches the actual SHA-256 hash of the presented WRPAC.

Trust Anchor Resolution: Crucially, the Wallet Instance resolves the WRPAC against the national Trusted List or LoTE. If the certificate is revoked or not issued by an authorized QTSP, the flow halts immediately.

Failure Path: If the signature is invalid, the certificate is revoked, or the client_id hash mismatches, the Wallet Instance aborts the presentation and warns the User of a potentially malicious request.
Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs a WRPAC_VALIDATION_COMPLETED event.

Once verified, the Wallet Instance displays a consent screen detailing exactly which attributes are being requested (e.g., "Example Bank is requesting your Date of Birth"). The User must explicitly authorize this selective disclosure.
5. Wallet Instance returns encrypted Verifiable Presentation
After receiving User consent, the Wallet Instance constructs the Verifiable Presentation (VP) in the required format:

For SD-JWT VC: It applies the selective disclosure policies, picking only the user-approved ~ separated strings, and signs a new Key Binding JWT (KB-JWT) using the secure element's device key intimately linked to the credential.
For mdoc: It generates a CBOR DeviceResponse containing the DeviceMAC or DeviceSignature over the session transcript.

Because the Relying Party requested direct_post.jwt, the entire presentation payload (vp_token and presentation_submission) is encrypted into a JWE utilizing the ephemeral public key the Relying Party provided in its original response_encryption_jwk. This encrypted JWE is securely passed from the Wallet Instance, back through the OS, and finally to the Browser.
Artifact Produced: Encrypted Verifiable Presentation (JWE).
Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Instance logs a USER_CONSENT_GRANTED event.
6. Relying Party verifies JARM response

The Browser receives the encrypted JWE response from the OS and resolves the Javascript navigator.credentials.get() Promise. The Relying Party's front-end forwards this opaque string to its backend services for verification:
Decryption: The Relying Party uses its ephemeral private key to decrypt the JARM payload to extract the vp_token.

Trust Verification: The Relying Party extracts the Issuer's signature from the credential and validates it against the Issuer's public key found on the national Trusted List/LoTE. No DID resolution is performed. The ecosystem relies entirely on X.509 PKI bounds.

Device Binding & Revocation: The Relying Party mathematically verifies the device binding proof (KB-JWT or DeviceAuth) targeting the presentation nonce to ensure the credential wasn't cloned or replayed. Finally, it checks the Issuer's Status Lists (IETF Token Status List) to confirm the credential hasn't been revoked.
Failure Path: If the issuer signature is invalid, device binding fails, or the credential is revoked, the Relying Party rejects the authentication attempt.
Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a DC_API_PRESENTATION_VERIFIED event.
Once all cryptographic checks pass, the validated claims are extracted into the Relying Party's underlying business logic.
Artifact Produced: Validated Subject Attributes and Ephemeral Authentication Session.

Trust model resolution: Note that in Phase 4, the RP fetches the issuer trust anchor from the Trusted List / LoTE (X.509-based, §5.5) — not from a DID resolution endpoint. This is true regardless of which wallet type presented the credential. The DC-API itself is trust-model-agnostic: it transports protocol messages without interpreting trust semantics. The trust model is determined by the attestation type (§7.2.3) and resolved during RP verification.

FedCM distinction. The Federated Credential Management API (FedCM) is a sibling extension of navigator.credentials that mediates federated authentication — not wallet credential presentation. See §7.7.1 for the full Credential Management API framework and how the DC API, WebAuthn, and FedCM relate.
This section provides the definitive answer to the practical question: if you are building RP integration, do you need to implement DID resolution?

passages.text: Conclusion: For the EUDI mandatory flows (PID, QEAA, PuB-EAA), an RP does not need DID support — regardless of which wallet the user presents. DID support becomes relevant only in two scenarios:
passages.headingText: 7.8 RP Decision Matrix: Do You Need DID Support?

passages.text: Accepting non-qualified EAAs from providers whose Attestation Rulebook (§6.11) specifies a DID-based trust framework. This is a Rulebook-specific, domain-specific decision — not a universal EUDI requirement.
passages.headingText: 7.8 RP Decision Matrix: Do You Need DID Support?

passages.text: Cross-border interoperability with non-EU ecosystems that use DID-based trust (e.g., swiyu). This requires bilateral mutual recognition agreements that do not yet exist.
passages.headingText: 7.8 RP Decision Matrix: Do You Need DID Support?

passages.text: RP planning impact: Do not invest in DID resolution infrastructure for EUDI launch. Focus on the X.509 verification pipeline described in §8–§11. Monitor non-qualified EAA Rulebook publications for your specific domain — if one mandates DID-based trust, the Rulebook will specify the exact DID method, resolution endpoint, and verification procedure. The client_id_scheme used for RP authentication is x509_hash (§8.3) — there is no DID-based alternative in the HAIP profile.
passages.headingText: 7.8 RP Decision Matrix: Do You Need DID Support?
