passages.text: TEE-backed: The biometric check and key release happen entirely within the TEE (e.g., Android StrongBox, Apple Secure Enclave). The Wallet app receives only a success/failure signal.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: SE-backed: The key material is stored in a discrete hardware secure element. The SE requires a PIN or biometric confirmation via the TEE before releasing the signing capability.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The WSCA authorises a single COSE_Sign1 operation (step 15) and immediately revokes the key access. This ensures that even if the Wallet app is compromised, the device key cannot be used without User authentication.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: User Authentication Token / Biometric Match
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 15. Wallet Unit builds DeviceResponse with DeviceAuth signature
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet constructs the DeviceResponse CBOR structure containing: (a) the issuer-signed attributes (issuerSigned.nameSpaces) with their per-element random salts, and (b) the deviceAuth.deviceSignature — a COSE_Sign1 over the DeviceAuthentication CBOR structure.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The DeviceAuthentication structure binds the response to this specific session:
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet signs this structure using the credential's device key (the key bound to the MSO's deviceKeyInfo) via the WSCA. The Reader will verify this signature (step 20) to confirm that the presenting device possesses the private key that the PID Provider originally bound to the credential during issuance.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 16. Wallet Unit transmits encrypted DeviceResponse over BLE
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet fully encrypts the DeviceResponse CBOR structure using the symmetric session key and transmits it back to the Reader over BLE.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Encrypted DeviceResponse CBOR Payload
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs an ENCRYPTED_DEVICE_RESPONSE_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 17. mdoc Reader decrypts DeviceResponse
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Reader decrypts the DeviceResponse using SKDevice — the symmetric key derived in step 6 from the ECDH key agreement. The decryption uses AES-256-GCM with the SessionTranscript as additional authenticated data (AAD). Upon successful decryption, the Reader obtains the plaintext CBOR containing the documents array with the issuer-signed attributes and device authentication data.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: BLE transfer characteristics: The DeviceResponse may exceed the BLE MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit, typically 512 bytes). ISO 18013-5 §9.3.3.1 defines a chunking protocol where the response is split across multiple BLE characteristic writes. The Reader must reassemble the chunks before decryption.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If decryption fails, the mdoc Reader MUST reject the presentation and display a cryptographic error to the Agent.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Decrypted DeviceResponse CBOR Payload
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The mdoc Reader logs a DEVICE_RESPONSE_DECRYPTED event.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 18. mdoc Reader verifies IssuerAuth MSO signature
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Reader verifies the issuerAuth COSE_Sign1 signature within the DeviceResponse. This is the mdoc equivalent of SD-JWT issuer signature verification — it proves the credential was legitimately issued by a notified PID Provider:
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Extract the MSO (MobileSecurityObject) from the COSE_Sign1 payload
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Extract the PID Provider's signing certificate from the x5chain unprotected header
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Verify the COSE_Sign1 ES256 signature using the PID Provider's public key
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Validate the certificate chain against the PID Provider LoTE trust anchor (§5.5.3)
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Check MSO validityInfo: signed timestamp, validFrom ≤ now ≤ validUntil
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: In online-connected supervised terminals, the Reader can perform the LoTE lookup in real-time. For offline terminals (e.g., border checkpoints with intermittent connectivity), the Reader must use a cached copy of the PID Provider LoTE, refreshed during its last online maintenance window.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the IssuerAuth signature verification fails, the mdoc Reader MUST immediately display a critical error on the Agent's screen (e.g., ❌ "Credential issuer signature invalid — document cannot be trusted"). The Agent MUST NOT accept the credential and should escalate to physical document verification.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The mdoc Reader logs an ISSUER_AUTH_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 19. mdoc Reader verifies data element digests
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: For each IssuerSignedItem in the issuerSigned.nameSpaces, the Reader verifies that the attribute value was not tampered with after issuance. The verification process (ISO 18013-5 §10.3.1):
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Re-encode the IssuerSignedItem as CBOR (tagged with CBOR tag 24)
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Compute SHA-256 over the re-encoded CBOR bytes
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Look up the corresponding digestID in the MSO's valueDigests map for the matching nameSpace
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Compare the computed digest with the MSO digest — they must match exactly
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: This per-element digest verification enables selective disclosure in the mdoc format: the Wallet only transmits the IssuerSignedItem entries for the attributes the User consented to share. Attributes not disclosed are represented only by their digest in the MSO — the Reader cannot reconstruct undisclosed values from the digest alone.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If any data element digest does not match, the specific attribute has been cryptographically tampered with in transit. The mdoc Reader MUST aggressively flag the invalid attribute and display a severe integrity warning to the Agent.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The mdoc Reader logs a DATA_ELEMENT_DIGESTS_VERIFIED event.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 20. mdoc Reader verifies DeviceAuth signature over SessionTranscript
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Reader verifies the deviceSignature COSE_Sign1 to prove the presenting device possesses the private key bound to this credential at issuance. The verification:
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Reconstruct the DeviceAuthentication CBOR array: ["DeviceAuthentication", SessionTranscript, docType, DeviceNameSpaceBytes]
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Extract the deviceKey public key from the MSO's deviceKeyInfo field
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Verify the COSE_Sign1 ES256 signature over the DeviceAuthentication structure using the deviceKey
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: This is the mdoc equivalent of KB-JWT verification in SD-JWT VC — it proves: (a) the device presenting the credential now is the same device that received it from the PID Provider, (b) the presentation is bound to this specific session via the SessionTranscript, and (c) the credential has not been cloned or forwarded to another device.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the DeviceAuth verification fails, this constitutes a critical security failure, mathematically proving a potential credential cloning, forwarding, or relay attack. The mdoc Reader MUST reject the presentation and trigger an administrative security alert.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The mdoc Reader logs a DEVICE_AUTH_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 21. mdoc Reader displays portrait and verified attributes to RP Agent
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Once all cryptographic verifications pass (steps 18–20), the Reader terminal renders the verified attributes on the Agent's screen. The display includes:
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Portrait photograph — displayed prominently in high resolution for visual comparison (step 22)
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Personal attributes — family_name, given_name, birth_date, formatted according to the locale
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Verification status — green indicators (✅) for each passing verification dimension (issuer signature, digest integrity, device binding)
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Credential metadata — PID Provider name, issuance date, validity period
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The terminal display should be positioned so that only the Agent can see it — showing the User's portrait and personal data on a public-facing screen would violate privacy principles. Some terminal implementations use a secondary screen visible only from the Agent's position.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 22. RP Agent visually compares portrait against User
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Agent performs the human-in-the-loop identity binding that distinguishes supervised from unsupervised flows. The Agent visually compares the displayed high-resolution portrait photograph against the physical appearance of the User standing before them. This visual check verifies what cryptography alone cannot — that the person operating the device is the same person whose PID was issued by the PID Provider.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The visual comparison addresses threats that device-binding alone cannot mitigate:
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Coerced presentation — the User may be forced to unlock their device under duress
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Shoulder-surfing PIN theft — an attacker who observed the User's PIN could operate the Wallet
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Device theft with biometric bypass — theoretical bypass of biometric authentication (e.g., sleeping user, prosthetic fingerprint)
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Accessibility considerations: For users with facial differences, the Agent should exercise professional judgement and may request additional verification (e.g., asking the User to confirm their date of birth verbally). The portrait comparison should not be the sole identity binding mechanism for accessibility reasons.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The mdoc Reader terminal logs a VISUAL_COMPARISON_INITIATED event, recording the start of the human-in-the-loop validation window.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 23. RP Agent confirms identity match on terminal
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Agent taps "Confirm" on the terminal to record that the visual comparison was successful. This confirmation has dual significance:
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Verification completion — the terminal records the verified identity and applies the RP's business rules (e.g., grant access, onboard customer, pass border control)
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit trail — the terminal logs: timestamp, WRPAC identifier, credential docType, Agent identifier (if applicable), verification result (pass), and which attributes were disclosed. The Agent's confirmation is itself evidence of the human verification step.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The terminal should NOT store the portrait photograph or attribute values beyond the immediate verification session unless the RP's data retention policy explicitly requires it (e.g., KYC onboarding per §22 requires retained identity records). For transient verifications (e.g., age checks at a venue), the attributes should be discarded immediately after the Agent's confirmation.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: End of proximity session: After confirmation, the BLE session is terminated, both ephemeral key pairs are destroyed, and the symmetric session keys are purged. The next verification requires a fresh NFC handshake and new ephemeral keys.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The mdoc Reader terminal logs an IDENTITY_MATCH_CONFIRMED event.
passages.headingText: 13.4 Supervised Flow Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: In the unsupervised proximity flow, there is no human agent — the RP terminal operates autonomously. This flow is used at:
E-gates (automated border control)
Self-service kiosks (age verification for alcohol/tobacco vending)
Parking barriers (credential-gated access)
IoT devices (smart locks, car rental)
Device-level user authentication (biometric/PIN)
Typically requested for visual match
Lower (device-only verification)
Convenience (age check, access)
In the unsupervised flow, the RP trusts the device binding and user authentication mechanisms of the Wallet Unit rather than performing visual verification.
User taps device on terminal
Ephemeral key + BLE/Wi-Fi info
Short range prevents interception
Ephemeral key + BLE/Wi-Fi info
Visual proximity requirement
Reader pushes engagement via BLE
After exchanging ephemeral public keys, both parties derive session keys using ECDH + HKDF:
All subsequent messages are encrypted with AES-256-GCM using the derived session keys.
1. User approaches Automated Terminal

The User approaches the Relying Party's automated terminal — an unattended device such as an e-gate at an airport, an age-verification turnstile at a venue, a vending machine for age-restricted goods, or a self-service kiosk. Unlike the supervised flow (§13.4) where a human RP Agent mediates the transaction, the unsupervised terminal operates autonomously: it must complete the entire verification pipeline, make the access decision, and provide feedback without human oversight. This means the terminal's software must handle all error cases gracefully, since there is no agent to fall back to (see §13.11 for online fallback strategies).
2. Automated Terminal displays "Tap your device" prompt

The terminal detects the User's presence (via proximity sensor, motion detection, or button press) and displays a visual prompt: "Tap your device to verify". This prompt must comply with EN 301 549 accessibility requirements (§13.12): it should use large, high-contrast text, be accompanied by an audio cue for visually impaired Users, and include a pictogram showing where to tap. The NFC reader area should be clearly marked with the standard NFC contactless symbol (ISO/IEC 18092). The terminal also begins advertising its BLE service UUID to allow Wallet Units that detect the advertisement to pre-initiate the connection.
Audit Telemetry: The Automated Terminal logs an UNSUPERVISED_VERIFICATION_INITIATED event.

The User opens their EUDI Wallet application and navigates to the credential they expect to present (e.g., PID for age verification, mDL for driving privilege check). On some platforms, the Wallet may activate automatically when the device detects an NFC field from the terminal — this "tap-first, open-second" UX depends on the Wallet Provider's implementation and the OS's NFC dispatch behaviour (Android HCE vs. iOS Core NFC). The Wallet enters the mdoc "device" role (ISO 18013-5 §9.3.3.1.2), ready to receive a DeviceEngagement handshake.

UX consideration: For unsupervised flows, the User has no RP Agent to guide them. The Wallet's UI should clearly indicate which credential is about to be presented and to which type of terminal (using the terminal's WRPAC identity if available at this stage).
4. Wallet Unit transmits DeviceEngagement via NFC tap

The User taps their device on the terminal's NFC reader. The Wallet transmits the DeviceEngagement CBOR structure (ISO 18013-5 §10.1.1.4) via NFC, containing its ephemeral public key and BLE connection parameters:

This is identical to the supervised flow (§13.4 step 4). The NFC tap serves a dual purpose: (1) it transfers the DeviceEngagement payload (ephemeral key + BLE connection info), and (2) it establishes physical proximity — the NFC range of ~4cm provides implicit proof that the User is physically present at the terminal. After the NFC exchange, communication switches to BLE for the data transfer (NFC bandwidth is insufficient for full credential exchange).
Artifact Produced: DeviceEngagement CBOR Payload
Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a DEVICE_ENGAGEMENT_TRANSMITTED event.
5. Automated Terminal generates ephemeral EC key pair

The terminal generates a fresh EC P-256 ephemeral key pair for this specific session (ISO 18013-5 §10.1.1.5). This key pair is used only for the ECDH key agreement in step 6 and is discarded after the session ends. The terminal's secure element or HSM should generate the key — software-only key generation on embedded terminals is acceptable for low-security use cases (age gates) but not for high-security deployments (e-gates, KYC kiosks).

Key difference from supervised flow: In the supervised flow, the mdoc Reader is typically a tablet or smartphone with a full-featured crypto library. In the unsupervised flow, the terminal may be a constrained embedded device (ARM Cortex-M class) — implementers must verify that their hardware supports P-256 ECDH with sufficient entropy for key generation.
Artifact Produced: Ephemeral EC Key Pair (Terminal)
Audit Telemetry: The Automated Terminal logs an EPHEMERAL_KEY_GENERATED event.
6. Automated Terminal derives symmetric session keys via ECDH

The terminal performs ECDH key agreement (ISO 18013-5 §10.1.1.5) by combining its ephemeral private key with the Wallet's ephemeral public key (received in step 4) to produce a shared secret Z. It then derives two symmetric AES-256 session keys via HKDF-SHA-256 (RFC 5869):
SKDevice — key for encrypting data from Wallet → Terminal (used by the Wallet to encrypt the DeviceResponse)
SKReader — key for encrypting data from Terminal → Wallet (used by the Terminal to encrypt the DeviceRequest)

The HKDF info parameter includes the SessionTranscript (ISO 18013-5 §10.1.5.1) — a CBOR structure binding the session keys to the specific DeviceEngagement and handover method. This prevents session hijacking: even if an attacker captures the BLE traffic, they cannot derive the session keys without the ephemeral private keys.
Artifact Produced: Symmetric Session Keys (SKDevice, SKReader)
Audit Telemetry: The Automated Terminal logs a SESSION_KEYS_DERIVED event.
7. Automated Terminal sends encrypted DeviceRequest over BLE

The terminal establishes the BLE connection using the UUID from the DeviceEngagement (step 4) and sends an AES-GCM encrypted DeviceRequest (ISO 18013-5 §9.3.2.1.2). For an unsupervised age-verification terminal, the request typically asks for a minimal attribute set:

Key differences from the supervised flow (§13.4 step 7): (1) the request is typically for a single boolean attribute (age_over_18: true/false) rather than a full identity (name, portrait, DOB), minimising data exposure per the data minimisation principle; (2) IntentToRetain is false — the terminal has no need or right to store the attribute beyond the immediate access decision; (3) the readerAuth contains the terminal-specific WRPAC, not an RP Agent's personal credential.
Artifact Produced: Encrypted DeviceRequest CBOR Payload
Audit Telemetry: The Automated Terminal logs an ENCRYPTED_DEVICE_REQUEST_DISPATCHED event.
8. Wallet Unit verifies WRPAC certificate chain via LoTE

The Wallet Unit extracts the WRPAC certificate chain from the readerAuth (step 7) and validates it against the national LoTE trust anchors (§5.5.3). The chain typically consists of the terminal's leaf WRPAC → Access CA intermediate → LoTE root. The Wallet verifies each certificate's signature, checks validity periods, and confirms the chain terminates at a LoTE-listed trust anchor.

Offline verification: In unsupervised scenarios, the Wallet Unit may not have internet access at the point of interaction (e.g., underground parking garage). The Wallet relies on its cached LoTE — a locally stored copy of the List of Trusted Entities refreshed periodically when connectivity was available. The cache TTL determines the maximum period during which a revoked Access CA certificate could still be trusted. See §13.11 for cache management strategies.

Failure Path: If chain verification fails, the Wallet Unit MUST display a warning to the User: "This terminal's identity could not be verified." The User may still choose to proceed (at their own explicit risk), but the Wallet MUST visually distinguish this from a verified interaction.
Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a WRPAC_CHAIN_VALIDATED event.
9. Wallet Unit displays consent prompt to User

The Wallet displays a consent screen showing the terminal's verified identity (extracted from the WRPAC — e.g., "Supermarket Berlin — Tobacco Vending Machine"), the specific data requested (age_over_18), and the IntentToRetain status (false — data will not be stored). For unsupervised flows, the consent UX must be optimised for speed — the User is standing at a terminal, possibly in a queue, and expects a sub-5-second interaction. The Wallet should:
Show a single-tap approval button (not a multi-screen flow)
Display the terminal's identity prominently so the User can confirm they're interacting with the expected terminal
Use a colour-coded indicator: 🟢 WRPAC verified vs. 🟡 WRPAC unverified (if chain validation failed in step 8)

Accessibility: The consent screen should support VoiceOver/TalkBack for visually impaired Users, and the approval gesture should be configurable (biometric, PIN, or device unlock depending on User preference — see §13.12).
10. User approves via biometric or PIN

The User approves the data release by authenticating locally via biometric (fingerprint, FaceID) or device PIN. This user verification step proves "possession + inherence" (or "possession + knowledge") — the Wallet Unit only releases the credential after confirming the authorised User is physically present. The authentication happens via the WSCA/WSCD (unlike the WebAuthn pseudonym flow in §16.6 which uses OS-level authentication), because the credential being presented is an eIDAS-governed attestation (PID) that requires the full eIDAS trust chain.

Failure Path: If authentication fails, the Wallet MUST NOT release any data. The terminal detects the timeout (no DeviceResponse within the configured window, typically 30–60 seconds), resets to step 2, and logs a USER_AUTHENTICATION_TIMEOUT event.
Artifact Produced: User Authentication Token / Biometric Match
11. Wallet Unit transmits encrypted DeviceResponse over BLE

The Wallet constructs the DeviceResponse CBOR structure (ISO 18013-5 §9.3.2.1.2.2), containing only the approved attribute(s) and the DeviceAuth proof of possession. For the age-verification use case, the response contains a single data element:

The Wallet encrypts this payload with SKDevice (the session key derived in step 6) using AES-256-GCM and transmits it over the BLE channel. The deviceSignature (COSE_Sign1) signs the SessionTranscript with the device's private key — this binds the response to this specific physical proximity session (see step 13).
Artifact Produced: Encrypted DeviceResponse CBOR Payload
Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs an ENCRYPTED_DEVICE_RESPONSE_DISPATCHED event.
12. Automated Terminal verifies IssuerAuth MSO signature

The terminal decrypts the BLE payload using SKDevice, parses the DeviceResponse, and verifies the issuerAuth COSE_Sign1 signature in the Mobile Security Object (MSO). This confirms that the PID Provider (e.g., Bundesdruckerei) actually issued this credential. The terminal:
Extracts the issuer's X.509 certificate from the issuerAuth unprotected header (33 / x5chain)
Validates the certificate chain against its cached LoTE trust anchors (same as the Wallet did for the WRPAC in step 8)
Verifies the COSE_Sign1 signature over the MSO payload using the issuer's public key
Confirms the MSO's validityInfo (signed, validFrom, validUntil) is within the current time window

Offline trust anchor risk: The terminal's cached LoTE may not include newly onboarded PID Providers. Terminals with expired LoTE caches should fall back to degraded mode (§13.11) where they accept only credentials from issuers already in their cache, with a visible warning.
Failure Path: If the IssuerAuth signature verification fails, the Automated Terminal MUST immediately reject the presentation and abort the transaction.
Audit Telemetry: The Automated Terminal logs an ISSUER_AUTH_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event.
13. Automated Terminal verifies DeviceAuth signature

The terminal verifies the deviceSignature COSE_Sign1 (ISO 18013-5 §10.1.3.6) to prove that the credential was presented by the Wallet Unit that holds the corresponding private key — preventing credential cloning and relay attacks. The verification process:
Reconstruct the SessionTranscript CBOR array: [DeviceEngagementBytes, EReaderKeyBytes, Handover] — this binds the signature to this specific NFC engagement + BLE session
Construct the DeviceAuthentication CBOR: ["DeviceAuthentication", SessionTranscript, docType, DeviceNameSpaceBytes]
Verify COSE_Sign1(deviceKey, DeviceAuthentication) using the deviceKey public key extracted from the MSO's deviceKeyInfo

If the signature verifies, it proves: (a) the Wallet possesses the private key bound to this credential (device binding), and (b) the response was generated specifically for this session (session binding via SessionTranscript). A replayed response from a different session would fail because the SessionTranscript — which includes the terminal's ephemeral key — would differ.

Failure Path: If the DeviceAuth verification fails, it mathematically proves a potential clone or relay attack. The Automated Terminal MUST reject the presentation and trigger an internal security alert.
Audit Telemetry: The Automated Terminal logs a DEVICE_AUTH_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event.
14. Automated Terminal evaluates access policy (age_over_18)

The terminal's embedded policy engine evaluates the cryptographically verified attribute against its local access rule. For the typical age-verification use case, the policy is a simple boolean check:

More complex terminals (e-gates, customs kiosks) may evaluate compound policies — e.g., checking nationality against a permitted list, verifying driving privilege categories for vehicle rental, or comparing portrait against a live camera feed. The policy should be configurable and auditable — loaded from a signed configuration file rather than hardcoded, so that policy changes don't require firmware updates. See §26.1 for the three-tier policy architecture (static → parameterized → dynamic).

Data minimisation enforcement: After policy evaluation, the terminal MUST discard all credential data. Since IntentToRetain was false (step 7), the terminal has no legal basis to persist the attribute values. Only the access decision (grant/deny) and an anonymised transaction log (timestamp, terminal ID, decision) may be retained.
Audit Telemetry: The Automated Terminal logs an ACCESS_POLICY_EVALUATED event.
15. Automated Terminal grants or denies access
Based on the policy evaluation result, the terminal executes the physical access decision:

GRANT: The terminal activates the physical mechanism — unlocks a turnstile, opens a gate, enables a vending machine's dispensing mechanism, or marks a parking barrier for lifting. The terminal may also issue a time-limited session token (e.g., a QR code on a receipt) if the access control system requires it.

DENY: The terminal keeps the physical barrier locked and does not reveal the specific reason for denial (to avoid information leakage). The display shows a generic "Access denied" message. The terminal should NOT display "Age verification failed" or similar specific messages, as this would disclose the User's age status to bystanders — a privacy violation.

Failure Path: If verification failed (steps 12–13), the terminal treats it as a DENY with a distinct internal error code (VERIFICATION_FAILED) for audit, but presents the same generic "Access denied" message to the User. The terminal logs the failure type (signature invalid, chain untrusted, DeviceAuth mismatch) for operational diagnostics without logging any credential attribute values.
Audit Telemetry: The Automated Terminal logs an ACCESS_DECISION_EXECUTED event.
16. Automated Terminal provides visual feedback to User
The terminal provides immediate, multi-modal feedback to the User (per EN 301 549 accessibility requirements — §13.12):
Haptic (if terminal supports)
Short vibration (if handheld reader)

The entire unsupervised flow — from NFC tap (step 4) to feedback (step 16) — should complete within 3–5 seconds for age-verification use cases, matching the UX expectations of contactless payment terminals. Longer verification times (e.g., for compound policy evaluation with online revocation checks) should be accompanied by a progress indicator to prevent the User from re-tapping and initiating a duplicate session.

Cross-reference: For terminals with internet connectivity and the option to perform online revocation checks, see §13.11 (Online Fallback for Proximity Terminals) for caching strategies and latency trade-offs.
When a proximity terminal has internet connectivity, it faces a design choice:
High-security terminals (e-gates, KYC kiosks)
Latency impact (~200–500ms per revocation check); depends on connectivity
Cache-first, online-fallback
Standard terminals (age verification, access control)
Fast response; revocation window equals cache TTL
No-connectivity environments (underground parking, aircraft)
Fastest; highest revocation window risk

Recommendation: For terminals with internet, use the cache-first strategy with a short TTL (1–4 hours). Perform online revocation checks only when the cached Status List Token has expired. This balances user experience (sub-100ms verification) with security (bounded revocation propagation delay).

Remote alternative: For remote (online) age verification with maximum unlinkability, consider the EU Commission's ZKP-based Age Verification Solution (§19). It provides cryptographic unlinkability between presentations — each proof is unique and cannot be correlated, even with issuer collusion.
Pre-load full LoTE at provisioning time; differential sync when connectivity available
30 days (recommended); 90 days (absolute maximum) — beyond this, unknown new Providers cannot be verified
Terminal management system pushes LoTE updates during nightly sync windows
Stored locally on the terminal's secure element or HSM
Until certificate expiry (typically 1–2 years)
Re-provisioning required; automated renewal via terminal management if API available
Cache locally; refresh TTL per strategy above
Depends on risk tolerance: 4 hours (high-security) to 7 days (low-risk)
HTTP fetch from Status List endpoint when connectivity available
1 year (aligned with IACA certificate validity)
Updated during terminal firmware updates

Operational guideline: Terminals should track their last successful trust artifact sync and display a warning indicator (e.g., amber status LED) when any cached artifact exceeds 50% of its maximum offline period. Terminals that have been offline beyond the maximum period should switch to a degraded mode that accepts only presentations verifiable against cached trust anchors, with a visible notification that full verification is unavailable.

Regulatory basis: The caching strategies above are consistent with ARF requirements VCR_14 and VCR_15. VCR_14 mandates that RPs perform a risk analysis to determine whether to accept or refuse a credential when no reliable revocation information is available (e.g., offline with expired cache). VCR_15 recommends central Status List fetching with RP-internal distribution and RP-determined refresh frequency — expressly discouraging per-request Status List endpoint queries.
EUDI Wallet proximity flows must accommodate users with disabilities to meet EU accessibility requirements (EAA — European Accessibility Act, Directive 2019/882):
NFC tap as primary engagement method; haptic feedback on successful tap
Difficulty with precise NFC positioning
Larger NFC antenna targets on terminals; extended timeout windows
Wallet Providers should offer simplified consent UIs; RP terminal displays should use clear, large-font instructions
Visual indicators (LED colours, screen text) must accompany all audio cues

RP terminal guidance: Ensure that all proximity terminals comply with EN 301 549 (ICT accessibility requirements) and provide multi-modal feedback (visual + haptic + audio) for all interaction states.

TS9 defines how two Wallet Units can exchange credentials directly without an RP backend server. In this flow, one Wallet User acts as the Verifier (requesting attributes) and the other acts as the Presenter.
passages.headingText: 14.1 Overview and Relevance to Relying Parties

passages.text: At first glance, a Wallet-to-Wallet flow seems out of place in an Enterprise Relying Party integration guide. However, W2W is fundamentally the "RP-on-a-phone" deployment model and is legally and technically critical to the RP ecosystem for three overriding reasons:
passages.headingText: 14.1 Overview and Relevance to Relying Parties

passages.text: Ad-Hoc Relying Parties (Micro-RPs): Legally, eIDAS 2.0 (Art. 3) defines a Relying Party broadly as a natural or legal person that relies upon an electronic identification or trust service. This includes a massive long-tail of non-enterprise verifiers: a freelance notary verifying a client, a landlord checking a tenant's identity, a bouncer checking age at a club door, or a gig-economy delivery driver verifying age for restricted goods. Imposing full enterprise registration infrastructure (registering with a National Member State Registrar, obtaining X.509 WRPACs from Access Certificate Authorities) is completely unviable for these use cases. W2W enables any Wallet Unit to toggle into "Verifier Mdoc Reader Mode," transforming the citizen into an ad-hoc Relying Party immediately capable of verification.
passages.headingText: 14.1 Overview and Relevance to Relying Parties

passages.text: Shared Protocol Stack: Technically, W2W uses the exact same proximity standard (ISO/IEC 18013-5 mdoc over BLE/NFC/Wi-Fi Aware) as traditional enterprise point-of-sale terminals. Mobile developers building offline RP-facing verification apps (e.g., transit ticket inspector apps or embedded mobile POS systems) are effectively building W2W Verifiers or reusing identical offline CBOR parsing logic.
passages.headingText: 14.1 Overview and Relevance to Relying Parties

passages.text: The Trust Model Exception: W2W creates a structural exception in the trust ecosystem. Because the W2W Verifier is an unregistered natural person, they lack an Access Certificate (WRPAC). Consequently, the Holder's Wallet cannot cryptographically authenticate the Verifier via a certificate chain or query a Registrar API for their "intended use" or privacy policy. Documenting this provides Security Architects with a complete threat model: offline W2W verification without WRPACs is inherently a lower-assurance operation than authenticated enterprise proximity flows, requiring different user consent UX to compensate for the anonymity.
passages.headingText: 14.1 Overview and Relevance to Relying Parties

passages.text: ISO/IEC 18013-5 (proximity only)
passages.headingText: 14.1 Overview and Relevance to Relying Parties

passages.text: mdoc only (SD-JWT VC NOT supported for W2W)
passages.headingText: 14.1 Overview and Relevance to Relying Parties

passages.text: The Verifier's Wallet Unit acts as the mdoc reader
passages.headingText: 14.1 Overview and Relevance to Relying Parties

passages.text: Open issue — how the Verifier authenticates is under discussion
passages.headingText: 14.1 Overview and Relevance to Relying Parties

passages.text: W2W interactions use the same ISO 18013-5 protocol as proximity RP flows
The Verifier's Wallet Unit generates a PresentationOffer specifying requested elements
The Presenter's Wallet Unit processes this as if it were a standard proximity request
No WRPAC — the Verifier is a User, not a registered RP. This means the standard RP authentication via certificate chain validation does not apply.

Open Issue (TS9): The mechanism for authenticating the Verifier in W2W is still under active discussion. Possible approaches include: (1) the Verifier presents their own PID first, (2) mutual disclosure via a negotiation protocol, or (3) no Verifier authentication with explicit user consent.
1. Verifier User activates W2W mode
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier User manually toggles their EUDI Wallet into Wallet-to-Wallet (W2W) mode via a dedicated menu option. This mode transforms the Wallet into a mobile mdoc Reader (ISO 18013-5 §9.3.3) — the same role that a stationary RP terminal plays in supervised proximity flows (§13.4), but running on a citizen's smartphone instead of dedicated hardware. The W2W feature is defined in TS9 and is the only mechanism that allows a natural person (not a registered RP) to verify another person's credentials.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: W2W is mdoc-only: TS9 §1.2 explicitly restricts W2W to the mdoc format — SD-JWT VC is not supported. This means the Verifier Wallet must implement the mdoc Reader protocol (CBOR decoding, COSE_Sign1 verification, ECDH key agreement) in addition to the standard Holder functions.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Verifier Wallet logs a W2W_VERIFIER_MODE_ACTIVATED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 2. Verifier Wallet displays a mandatory trust warning
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Before allowing verification, the Verifier Wallet displays a mandatory security warning: "Wallet-to-Wallet verification should only be used with natural persons you trust. The person presenting their credentials will not be able to verify your identity." This warning is a regulatory countermeasure to prevent W2W from being abused as a data harvesting channel — since the Verifier has no WRPAC (STS9_30), the Holder cannot cryptographically authenticate the Verifier's identity.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The warning must be displayed on every W2W session — it cannot be dismissed permanently or hidden behind a "don't show again" toggle. This ensures informed consent from both parties in every interaction.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 3. Verifier User selects the Verifier role
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier User explicitly selects the "Verifier" role, which initialises the BLE peripheral listener and (optionally) the NFC reader interface. The Wallet begins advertising its BLE service UUID so the Holder's device can discover it after the QR code scan (step 10). The Verifier's device now waits for the Holder to initiate the engagement.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Rate limiting: The Wallet enforces per-device rate limits (STS9_31/32) — max 5 requests per hour, 20 per day, and 50 per week (sliding windows). This prevents a compromised or malicious Wallet from mass-querying credentials. If the rate limit is exceeded, the Wallet refuses to enter Verifier mode.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 4. Holder User activates W2W mode
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: In parallel, the Holder User activates the W2W module in their own Wallet. The Holder's experience is similar to standard proximity presentation (§13.4 step 2), but with the critical awareness that the Verifier is a natural person — not a registered RP with institutional accountability. This distinction is reinforced by the warning in step 5.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Holder Wallet logs a W2W_HOLDER_MODE_ACTIVATED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 5. Holder Wallet displays a mandatory trust warning
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder Wallet displays a complementary warning: "You are about to share your credentials with another person. This person cannot be verified — they have no registered identity. Only share data with people you trust." Unlike RP flows where the WRPAC provides cryptographic identity assurance, W2W interactions rely entirely on the Holder's out-of-band trust in the Verifier (physical presence, personal relationship).
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: No WRPAC = no RP identity: The Holder-side warning emphasises that the Verifier cannot be authenticated. In standard RP flows (§9.2, §13.4), the Wallet validates the RP's WRPAC before showing the consent screen. In W2W, no such validation is possible — the Holder must rely on personal judgement.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 6. Holder User selects the Holder role
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder User selects the "Holder" role, which prompts the Wallet to enumerate all valid credentials from its secure storage (WSCA boundary). Unlike RP flows where the Verifier's DCQL query determines which credentials are relevant, in W2W the Holder proactively chooses what to offer — this is the Holder-driven disclosure model unique to TS9.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 7. Holder Wallet displays available attestations and attributes
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder Wallet queries its secure storage and displays a list of all valid credentials and their individual attributes. The Wallet shows each credential type (PID, mDL, QEAA) with expandable attribute lists. Only mdoc-format credentials are eligible (TS9 §1.2) — SD-JWT VC credentials are filtered out of the W2W interface.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Offline readiness: Since W2W operates in proximity (BLE), the Holder's Wallet must be able to enumerate credentials and construct the PresentationOffer without internet connectivity. All credential metadata must be locally cached.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 8. Holder User selects attributes to offer
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder User manually selects the specific attributes they are willing to share. This reverses the standard RP flow: in RP interactions (§9.2, §13.4), the RP specifies which attributes it needs via a DCQL query or DeviceRequest, and the Holder approves. In W2W, the Holder proactively builds an "offer" defining the maximum disclosure boundary — the Verifier can then select a subset (step 12) but cannot request anything outside the offer.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: This Holder-first design is a privacy safeguard: it prevents Verifiers from even knowing which credentials the Holder possesses beyond what the Holder chooses to reveal.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 9. Holder Wallet builds the PresentationOffer CBOR
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder Wallet encodes the user's selected attributes into a structured PresentationOffer CBOR payload. This acts as a cryptographic manifest of what the Holder is willing to share in this specific session.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: PresentationOffer CBOR Payload
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 10. Holder Wallet generates a DeviceEngagement QR code
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder Wallet creates a DeviceEngagement payload (ISO 18013-5 §10.1.1.4) extended with the TS9-specific PresentationOffer at CBOR key -1. The standard engagement fields (ephemeral ECDH public key, BLE UUID) enable the encrypted transport channel, while the -1 extension advertises the Holder's offered attributes. The complete payload is CBOR-encoded and rendered as a QR code on the Holder's screen.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier scans this QR code (step 11), simultaneously establishing: (a) the BLE connection parameters, (b) the ECDH key exchange, and (c) the set of attributes available for request. STS9_07/08 mandates QR codes for W2W device engagement — NFC tap is optional.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: DeviceEngagement QR Code (with PresentationOffer extension)
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Holder Wallet logs a DEVICE_ENGAGEMENT_QR_RENDERED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 11. Verifier Wallet displays offered attributes
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier Wallet scans the QR code, decodes the CBOR DeviceEngagement, extracts the PresentationOffer from key -1, and displays the offered attributes on the Verifier's screen. The display shows: credential type (e.g., "EU Digital Identity — PID"), and the individual attributes offered (e.g., family_name, given_name, birth_date, age_over_18).
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier User can now see what the Holder is willing to share — but cannot request anything outside this set. This transparency ensures the Verifier understands the boundaries of the interaction before making their request.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Verifier Wallet logs a DEVICE_ENGAGEMENT_QR_SCANNED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 12. Verifier User selects a subset of attributes to request
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier User selects the specific attributes they need from the offered set. The TS9 protocol enforces a strict subset constraint — the Verifier can only request attributes listed in the PresentationOffer. The Wallet UI should present the offered attributes as checkboxes with a "Select All" option.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: For example, if the Holder offered [family_name, given_name, birth_date, age_over_18], the Verifier might select only [age_over_18] for a simple age check — demonstrating the data minimisation principle applied at the Verifier level.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: PresentationOffer is optional (STS9_12): If the Holder did not include a PresentationOffer in the DeviceEngagement, the Verifier can request any attributes. This fallback enables simpler W2W interactions where the Holder trusts the Verifier to request only what is needed.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 13. Verifier Wallet transmits the DeviceRequest over BLE
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier Wallet establishes the BLE connection and transmits the DeviceRequest. Crucially, because the Verifier is an unregistered natural person, there is no readerAuth signature (no WRPAC certificate). Additionally, to comply with privacy rules, all IntentToRetain flags must be forcibly set to false.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Unauthenticated DeviceRequest CBOR Payload
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Verifier Wallet logs an UNAUTHENTICATED_DEVICE_REQUEST_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 14. Holder Wallet validates that the request is a strict subset
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder Wallet validates the incoming DeviceRequest against the PresentationOffer built in step 9. For each docType and nameSpace, the Wallet checks that every requested element identifier exists in the offer's element list. If the Verifier attempts to request an attribute not included in the PresentationOffer, the Wallet immediately terminates the BLE session and displays an error: "The Verifier requested data outside the agreed scope."
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: This enforcement is critical because the Verifier has no WRPAC — there is no institutional accountability for over-requesting. The PresentationOffer mechanism is the primary technical control limiting what the Verifier can access.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the request breaches the subset constraint, the Holder Wallet MUST explicitly reject and abort the session.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Holder Wallet logs a PRESENTATION_OFFER_SUBSET_VALIDATED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 15. Holder Wallet displays the final consent screen
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder Wallet displays a final consent screen showing exactly which attributes the Verifier requested. Unlike RP flows (§9.2 step 13), the consent screen in W2W prominently displays:
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: ⚠️ No verified Verifier identity — "You are sharing with an unregistered person"
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The specific attributes requested (e.g., age_over_18 only)
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: IntentToRetain: false for all attributes — "The Verifier's Wallet will not store your data"
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: This double-consent model (first: selecting what to offer in step 8; second: confirming the actual request here) provides layered privacy protection unique to the W2W flow.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 16. Holder User approves and authenticates
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder User taps "Approve" and authenticates via biometric (fingerprint / face) or PIN to unlock the WSCA and authorise the DeviceAuth signature (step 17). The authentication flow is identical to §13.4 step 14 — the WSCA releases the credential's device key for a single COSE_Sign1 operation over the DeviceAuthentication structure.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: User sovereignty in W2W: The Holder has full control — they chose which attributes to offer (step 8), they can refuse the Verifier's request at any point, and they must explicitly authenticate. The double-consent + authentication triple-gate is more protective than RP flows (which have only a single consent + authentication gate).
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If authentication fails or is cancelled, the Holder Wallet MUST completely abort the transaction and dispatch a generic error payload across the BLE channel.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: User Authentication Token / Biometric Match
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 17. Holder Wallet transmits the DeviceResponse over BLE
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Holder Wallet constructs and encrypts the DeviceResponse CBOR structure (identical to §13.4 step 16's format) and transmits it over the BLE channel. The response contains:
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: issuerSigned — the selected IssuerSignedItem entries with per-element random salts, plus the issuerAuth COSE_Sign1 (MSO signed by the PID Provider)
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: deviceSigned — the DeviceAuth COSE_Sign1 over the DeviceAuthentication structure (binding the response to this session's SessionTranscript)
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Per STS9_29, the IntentToRetain for all attributes is false — the Verifier Wallet is contractually (but not technically) prohibited from persisting the attribute values after the session ends. The technical enforcement relies on Wallet Provider compliance (STS9_36 — no screenshots; STS9_37 — no data forwarding).
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Encrypted DeviceResponse CBOR Payload
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Holder Wallet logs an ENCRYPTED_DEVICE_RESPONSE_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 18. Verifier Wallet verifies IssuerAuth (MSO)
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier Wallet performs the same IssuerAuth verification as an RP terminal (§13.4 step 18): extract the MSO from the issuerAuth COSE_Sign1, verify the ES256 signature using the PID Provider's certificate, and validate the certificate chain against the LoTE trust anchor. The Verifier Wallet must maintain its own LoTE cache (refreshed when online) to perform this verification.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: This step ensures the attribute values are genuine — even though the Verifier is an unregistered natural person, the Verifier Wallet can still cryptographically confirm that the data was issued by a legitimate, Member State–notified PID Provider.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Offline verification: W2W is designed for proximity and should work offline. The Verifier Wallet relies on cached LoTE entries and cached MSO validity periods. If the LoTE cache is stale, the Wallet should display a warning but may still proceed.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the IssuerAuth signature verification fails, the Verifier Wallet MUST immediately alert the Verifier User that the physical credential is mathematically invalid or fraudulent.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Verifier Wallet logs an ISSUER_AUTH_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 19. Verifier Wallet verifies DeviceAuth
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier Wallet verifies the deviceSignature COSE_Sign1 over the DeviceAuthentication CBOR structure (§13.4 step 20). This proves: (a) the person holding the Holder device possesses the private key that the PID Provider originally bound to the credential, and (b) the presentation is bound to this specific session via the SessionTranscript.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: In W2W, DeviceAuth verification is especially important because there is no visual portrait comparison (unlike supervised flows, §13.4 steps 21–22) and no RP-level monitoring. The cryptographic device binding is the primary technical assurance that the credential has not been forwarded or cloned.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the DeviceAuth verification fails, the proof of possession fails. The Verifier Wallet MUST mathematically reject the credential.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Verifier Wallet logs a DEVICE_AUTH_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 20. Verifier Wallet verifies PID validity
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Verifier Wallet checks the MSO's validityInfo to confirm the PID has not expired (validFrom ≤ now ≤ validUntil). If the Verifier Wallet has internet connectivity, it can also query the Token Status List to check for revocation — but this is often unavailable in offline W2W scenarios.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: As discussed in §5.4.2 step 4, PID validity serves as an indirect Wallet Unit health proxy — a valid PID implies the PID Provider has not detected a compromise of the Holder's Wallet Unit. In W2W, this is the Verifier's primary assurance that the Holder's device is trustworthy.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Revocation check limitations: In fully offline W2W scenarios, the Verifier cannot query the Status List in real-time. The Verifier Wallet should cache the last-known Status List Token and check the relevant index, accepting the risk of a stale status within the cache TTL window.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Verifier Wallet logs a PID_VALIDITY_CONFIRMED event.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: 21. Verifier Wallet displays verified attributes
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Upon successful verification (steps 18–20), the Verifier Wallet renders the verified attributes on the Verifier User's screen with verification indicators (✅ "Verified by [PID Provider name]"). The display is ephemeral — once the Verifier closes the screen, navigates away, or the session times out, the attribute values are permanently purged from device memory (STS9_29).
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Provider must implement technical safeguards (STS9_36) to prevent screenshots of the verified attributes screen, and must not provide any mechanism to export, copy, or forward the data (STS9_37). These constraints make W2W a "view-only" verification channel — the Verifier can confirm facts (e.g., "this person is over 18") but cannot retain evidence of the interaction.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: End of session: The BLE connection is terminated, ephemeral keys are destroyed, and the SessionTranscript is purged. No persistent trace of the W2W interaction remains on the Verifier's device.
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Key TS9 constraints verified against source specification:
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: QR mandatory for device engagement
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: QR codes are required; NFC is optional (unlike RP proximity where NFC is primary)
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Verifier has no WRPAC. Authentication relies on out-of-band trust and PID validity
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Verifier SHALL NOT persist received data
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Max 5 requests/hour, 20/day, 50/week (sliding windows)
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Wallet Providers SHOULD prevent screenshots of received presentations
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Verifier Wallet SHALL NOT communicate received data to any other party
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Holder may present without a prior offer; in that case Verifier can request any attributes
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: SD-JWT VC is NOT supported for W2W
passages.headingText: 14.3 W2W Interaction Flow (TS9) (Wallet-to-Wallet Model - No Intermediary)

passages.text: Bank/PSP-specific guidance: For the complete Bank/PSP compliance hub covering SCA Attestation issuance obligations, the "accept vs. issue" analysis, six SCA trigger classifications, and the Dutch PA dispute resolutions, see §24.
passages.headingText: 15. SCA for Electronic Payments: Lifecycle, Flows, and Dynamic Linking

passages.text: Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) is mandated by PSD2 (Art. 97) for electronic payments. TS12 defines how EUDI Wallets can fulfil this requirement through a dedicated SCA attestation that links the Wallet to a Payment Service Provider (PSP).
Is issued by the PSP (or its agent) to the User's Wallet Unit
Contains the User's payment credential reference (e.g., PAN last four digits, scheme)
Enables the Wallet to generate dynamic authentication codes for transactions
Must satisfy PSD2 dynamic linking requirements (binding authentication to transaction amount and payee)
TS12 specifies that SCA Attestations are identified by the category claim in the SD-JWT VC Type Metadata, not by separate VCT identifiers:
The actual VCT values are defined by sector-specific SCA Attestation Rulebooks, not by TS12 itself. TS12 provides three non-normative attestation examples:
Specific card belonging to a User
pan_last_four, scheme, scheme_logo
Specific account belonging to a User
sub only (no instrument details)

While this document focuses on the RP (verification) side, bank RPs in the SCA flow are unique in that they also issue SCA attestations to users' Wallet Units. The issuance uses OID4VCI (OpenID for Verifiable Credential Issuance) and follows this high-level lifecycle:
Enrolment trigger: User adds payment instrument (card/account) to their Wallet via the bank's app
Authentication: Bank authenticates the User through existing channels (existing SCA, online banking login)
Credential Offer: Bank's OID4VCI endpoint sends a Credential Offer to the Wallet Unit (same-device or cross-device)
Wallet requests credential: Wallet Unit calls the bank's OID4VCI Token Endpoint, then the Credential Endpoint

Bank issues SCA attestation: Bank creates the SD-JWT VC with category: "urn:eu:europa:ec:eudi:sua:sca", signs it with the bank's Attestation Provider key, and includes the User's cnf device public key
Wallet stores: Wallet Unit stores the SCA attestation alongside the User's PID
Lifecycle: SCA attestation may be short-lived (requiring periodic re-issuance) or long-lived with revocation via Status List
Revoke old SCA attestation; trigger new issuance
Revoke SCA attestation via Status List
Wallet shows attestation as invalid
Immediate revocation via Status List
SCA flow fails; User must contact bank
Wallet prompts User to refresh

Open area: TS12 cross-references OID4VCI for the issuance protocol but does not fully specify the SCA-specific issuance parameters (e.g., mandatory claims in the Credential Offer, required authentication level for enrolment). This is listed as Open Question #8.

Banks are unique in the EUDI Wallet ecosystem in that they act as both issuers (of SCA attestations via OID4VCI) and verifiers (of SCA attestations via OpenID4VP). This section details the issuance side.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Embedded wallet SDK note: In the dual-wallet model (§9.5.5), the bank can issue SCA attestations via OID4VCI directly into its own embedded wallet SDK — eliminating the need for the external EUDI Wallet in the SCA flow. The OID4VCI protocol and credential format are identical; only the target wallet changes. See §9.5 for the full architectural analysis and §27.7 for vendor evaluation.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: OpenID for Verifiable Credential Issuance (OID4VCI) 1.0 — which achieved Final Specification status in September 2025 — defines the protocol for issuing credentials to Wallet Units. For SCA attestation issuance, the Pre-Authorized Code flow is the expected pattern, since the bank has already authenticated the user through existing banking channels before issuance begins.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 1. User opens banking app and selects "Add card to EUDI Wallet"
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The User navigates to the card management section of their Bank's mobile app and selects an option to provision a payment card into their EUDI Wallet (e.g., "Add Visa •••4242 to EUDI Wallet"). This action initiates the OID4VCI Pre-Authorized Code flow. The Bank's app is the trigger — the User does not start from the EUDI Wallet side. This ensures the Bank controls the enrolment experience and can apply its own eligibility checks (e.g., card status, account standing) before issuing the SCA attestation.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 2. Bank authenticates User via existing SCA
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Bank re-authenticates the User using its existing SCA mechanisms (PSD2 Art. 97) — typically the banking app's biometric lock or PIN. This is a re-authentication, not initial onboarding: the User already has an active banking relationship. The re-authentication proves the User is the legitimate account holder before the Bank issues an SCA attestation. Some banks may require step-up authentication (e.g., SMS OTP in addition to biometric) for high-privilege operations like credential provisioning.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Bank logs an EXISTING_SCA_USER_AUTHENTICATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 3. Bank generates pre-authorized_code and optional tx_code
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Bank generates the cryptographic material for the OID4VCI Pre-Authorized Code flow:
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: pre-authorized_code — a one-time, short-lived code (e.g., SplxlOBeZQQYba49Wd8E3eNLA0f3k2qR) bound to the authenticated session. Valid for a single exchange at the Token Endpoint (step 6). Typically expires in 5–10 minutes.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: tx_code (optional) — a 6-digit numeric PIN sent via SMS or push notification, providing an out-of-band binding factor. This ensures a compromised banking app session cannot silently provision credentials to a malicious Wallet.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Pre-Authorized Code flow is preferred over the standard Authorization Code flow because the Bank has already authenticated the User in step 2 — there is no need for a separate OAuth authorization page.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Artifact Produced: pre-authorized_code (and optional tx_code)
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Bank logs a PRE_AUTHORIZED_CODE_GENERATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 4. Bank transmits Credential Offer to Wallet Unit
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Bank transmits a Credential Offer to the Wallet Unit, typically via a same-device deeplink or by displaying a QR code.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Bank's OID4VCI Issuer Identifier — the Wallet resolves /.well-known/openid-credential-issuer from this
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: credential_configuration_ids
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: References a configuration in the Issuer's metadata that defines the SCA attestation format and claims
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: One-time code issued by the bank; valid for a single Token Endpoint call
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Optional transaction code (PIN/OTP) for additional user binding — sent via SMS or push notification
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Credential Offer (JSON)
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Bank logs a CREDENTIAL_OFFER_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 5. Wallet Unit parses Credential Offer and resolves Issuer metadata
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Wallet parses the Credential Offer JSON and extracts the credential_issuer URL. It then fetches the Bank's OID4VCI Issuer Metadata from https://pay.example-bank.de/.well-known/openid-credential-issuer to discover: (a) the Token Endpoint URL, (b) the Credential Endpoint URL, (c) the credential_configurations_supported map (which defines the SCA attestation's format, VCT, and available claims), and (d) supported proof types (jwt with ES256).
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Failure Path: If the Wallet does not recognise the credential type or cannot satisfy the cryptographic proof requirements, it MUST abort the issuance and explicitly warn the User.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs an ISSUER_METADATA_RESOLVED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 6. Wallet Unit exchanges pre-authorized_code at Token Endpoint
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Wallet calls the Bank's Token Endpoint to exchange the pre-authorized_code (and tx_code if required) for an access token.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Artifact Produced: HTTP POST /token Request
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a TOKEN_ENDPOINT_EXCHANGE_INITIATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 7. Bank returns access_token and c_nonce to Wallet Unit
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Bank validates the codes and responds with an access_token and a cryptographic c_nonce.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The c_nonce is critical — the Wallet must include it in the proof-of-possession JWT sent to the Credential Endpoint. This binds the device key to this specific issuance session.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Failure Path: If the pre-authorized_code or tx_code is invalid, the Bank MUST reject the request, returning an invalid_grant error, and immediately log a security alert for potential fraud.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Token Response (JSON) with access_token and c_nonce
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Bank logs an ACCESS_TOKEN_ISSUED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations
