passages.text: 8. Wallet Unit generates EC P-256 device key pair in WSCA/WSCD
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Wallet generates a fresh EC P-256 key pair inside the WSCA (Wallet Secure Cryptographic Application) or WSCD (Wallet Secure Cryptographic Device). The private key is hardware-bound — it never leaves the secure element (SE, TEE, or StrongBox). This key becomes the credential's device key: the public key is embedded in the credential's cnf.jwk (step 11), and the private key signs KB-JWTs during future presentations (§15.8). The key is specific to this SCA attestation — each credential gets its own key pair.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Device Key Pair (EC P-256)
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a DEVICE_KEY_PAIR_GENERATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 9. Wallet Unit builds proof-of-possession JWT with c_nonce
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Wallet constructs a proof-of-possession JWT (typ: openid4vci-proof+jwt) signed with the newly generated device private key (step 8). The JWT includes: (a) the Bank's c_nonce in the nonce payload claim (binding this proof to the current issuance session), (b) the device public key in the jwk header parameter (so the Bank can extract it), (c) aud set to the Bank's Credential Issuer URL, and (d) iat set to the current time. This proof demonstrates that the Wallet actually possesses the private key corresponding to the public key it is asking the Bank to embed in the credential.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Proof-of-Possession JWT
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a POP_JWT_GENERATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 10. Wallet Unit transmits credential request to Bank Credential Endpoint
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Wallet calls the Bank's Credential Endpoint, presenting the access_token, requesting the specific VCT format, and supplying the proof-of-possession JWT.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The jwk in the proof JWT header is the Wallet's device public key. The Bank will embed this key into the issued credential.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Credential Request (JSON)
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a CREDENTIAL_REQUEST_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 11. Bank creates SD-JWT VC with SCA claims and device key binding
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Bank verifies the proof-of-possession, extracts the jwk from the proof header, and creates the SCA Attestation (SD-JWT VC). The Bank's Attestation Provider signs the credential, embedding the public key into the cnf (confirmation) claim.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The cnf.jwk matches the device public key from the proof JWT. When this SCA attestation is later presented for a payment authorization (§15.8), the Wallet signs a KB-JWT with the corresponding private key — proving possession of the device key and binding the authentication to this specific attestation.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Failure Path: If the proof JWT signature is invalid or the c_nonce is incorrect, the Bank MUST reject the request to prevent malicious key binding.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Artifact Produced: SCA Attestation (SD-JWT VC)
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Bank logs an SD_JWT_VC_CREATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 12. Bank delivers SD-JWT VC response to Wallet Unit
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Bank responds to the Credential Endpoint request with the fully signed SD-JWT VC. The response format:
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The credential field contains the SD-JWT VC string (Issuer-JWT + disclosures, no KB-JWT at issuance time). The Bank may also return a new c_nonce for potential batch issuance or credential refresh.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Artifact Produced: SD-JWT VC Response (JSON)
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Bank logs an SD_JWT_VC_RESPONSE_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 13. Wallet Unit stores SCA attestation securely
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Wallet stores the SCA attestation's SD-JWT VC in its credential store, associated with the WSCA-bound device private key (step 8). The storage links: (a) the Issuer-JWT + disclosures (the credential itself), (b) a reference to the hardware-bound private key (for KB-JWT signing during presentations), (c) the credential metadata (issuer, VCT, expiry), and (d) display information (card scheme logo, last four digits). The credential is now ready for use in SCA flows (§15.8).
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs an SCA_ATTESTATION_STORED event.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: 14. Wallet Unit confirms "Card added to Wallet" to User
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The Wallet displays a success confirmation to the User — e.g., "✅ Visa •••4242 has been added to your EUDI Wallet" with the card scheme logo. The SCA attestation appears in the Wallet's credential list alongside the User's PID and other attestations. From this point, the User can authorise payments using the EUDI Wallet (§15.8) instead of the Bank's dedicated mobile app, enabling cross-PSP SCA portability.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: PSP implementation note: The bank must ensure its OID4VCI Issuer Metadata (at /.well-known/openid-credential-issuer) includes the SCA attestation in its credential_configurations_supported map, with the category claim set to urn:eu:europa:ec:eudi:sua:sca in the VCT Type Metadata. This allows Wallet Units to recognise the attestation as SCA-capable and match it against TS12 DCQL queries from other PSPs.
passages.headingText: 15.4 OID4VCI Issuance Flow for SCA Attestations

passages.text: The preceding sections (§15.3–§15.4) detail how bank/PSP RPs issue SCA attestations via OID4VCI. However, any registered RP may also act as a credential issuer — issuing non-qualified EAAs into users' Wallet Units. This section generalises the SCA-specific pattern into a reusable framework for all RP-as-issuer scenarios.
passages.headingText: 15.5 RP as Credential Issuer: Generalised OID4VCI Pattern

passages.text: An RP that both verifies and issues credentials operates under two distinct trust chains:
OpenID4VP 1.0 (HAIP profiled)
OID4VCI 1.0 (HAIP profiled, §§8–8)
WRPAC (X.509, issued by Access CA)
Attestation Provider signing key (own X.509 chain)
RP registration with Registrar (CIR 2025/848)
EAA Provider registration with Registrar
Access CA → Member State Root
Attestation Provider CA → Member State Root
Authorization endpoint, response endpoint
Credential Offer endpoint, Credential endpoint, Nonce endpoint
Signing JAR requests, verifying VP Token
Signing issued credentials (Issuer-JWT)
Consumer (checks credential revocation)
Publisher (manages own Status List for issued credentials)

The two roles are architecturally independent: an RP can be a verifier-only, an issuer-only, or both. When acting as both, the RP maintains separate key material, separate registration entries, and separate operational pipelines. The WRPAC used for verification requests must not be reused as the credential signing key — these are distinct trust domains.
While §15.4 covers SCA attestation issuance for banks, the OID4VCI protocol applies identically to any credential type. Common RP-as-issuer scenarios include:
Loyalty card with tier, points balance
Pre-Authorized Code (store clerk scans card)
Student attestation with faculty, enrolment year
Authorization Code (student authenticates via IdP)
Employee credential with department, clearance level
Pre-Authorized Code (HR provisions after onboarding)
Season ticket with zones, validity period
Pre-Authorized Code (after purchase)
Health insurance attestation with policy number
Authorization Code (customer portal login)
Professional membership with licence number
Authorization Code (member portal)
All these RPs issue non-qualified EAAs — they do not require QTSP certification. The legal assurance level is lower than QEAA, but the technical issuance protocol (OID4VCI) is identical.
The OID4VCI flow for non-qualified EAAs follows the same steps as §15.4 (SCA issuance), with these differences:
Credential type — the vct (Verifiable Credential Type) is RP-defined (e.g., https://retailer.example/credentials/loyalty-card/v1) rather than the SCA-specific urn:eu:europa:ec:eudi:sua:sca category
Transaction code — optional; depends on the RP's security model (a physical store may use a receipt code; an online portal may skip it)
Key binding — the RP decides whether the credential is device-bound (cnf.jwk present) or bearer. Non-qualified EAAs may be bearer credentials if the use case does not require holder binding

Status List management — the RP must publish and maintain a Status List (Token Status List or JWT Status List) for credential revocation. This is a new operational responsibility not present in the verifier-only role

An RP acting as a credential issuer must publish OID4VCI Credential Issuer Metadata at /.well-known/openid-credential-issuer. This metadata enables Wallet Units to discover what credentials the RP can issue, in which formats, and what proofs are required.
The following is a representative example for a retailer issuing loyalty card attestations:
Key points for RPs implementing this metadata:
The vct value must be a URL the RP controls. The VCT Type Metadata at that URL defines the credential's claims schema, display properties, and status list mechanism
The proof_types_supported section tells the Wallet what kind of proof-of-possession the Credential Endpoint expects. For HAIP compliance, jwt with ES256 is mandatory
The display object is rendered by the Wallet when showing the credential to the User. Invest in good branding — this is what users see in their Wallet
An RP wanting to issue non-qualified EAAs must register as an EAA Provider with the national Registrar. This is a separate registration from the RP registration (CIR 2025/848 Art. 3–6):
Verify credentials from Wallets
WRPAC issued, intended attributes recorded
EAA Provider registration (Art. 12, Annex I)
Issue credentials into Wallets
Provider Information published, attestation types recorded in Common Catalogue (TS11)
The EAA Provider registration requires:
Provider Information — identity, supported attestation types, issuance policies, data sources, geographic scope (Annex I data model, CIR 2024/2981)
Attestation type definition — registered in the Common Catalogue (TS11) with claims schema, format support, and revocation mechanism
Credential Issuer Metadata — published at /.well-known/openid-credential-issuer (§15.5.3) and linked from the Provider Information's providesAttestations field (TS2)
Non-discrimination — per AS-AP-10-044, an EAA Provider must support all Wallet Solutions and must not discriminate between them

Compliance note: Qualified EAAs (QEAAs) can only be issued by Qualified Trust Service Providers (QTSPs) under eIDAS 2.0 Art. 45d. Public-body EAAs (PuB-EAAs) can only be issued by or on behalf of public sector bodies responsible for authentic sources. Non-qualified EAAs have no such restriction — any registered entity can issue them. Most RPs acting as issuers will issue non-qualified EAAs.
Cross-references: §15.4 (OID4VCI Pre-Authorized Code flow — reusable for any credential type), §4.3 (RP Registration flow), §4.4 (Registrar API).
TS12 §3.3.1 defines display hierarchy levels for transaction data fields:
Prominently on main confirmation screen
amount, currency, payee.name
Displayed, but MAY be on supplementary screen
pisp.legal_name, recurrence.frequency
The Wallet Unit renders a custom consent screen with localised labels from the ui_labels catalogue:

In the issuer-requested SCA flow, the PSP that issued the payment instrument (issuer bank) directly requests SCA from the User's Wallet when the User initiates a payment. This is the standard SCA flow for card-present and card-not-present transactions.
1. User initiates payment at Merchant
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The User initiates an electronic payment on the Merchant's website or mobile app (e.g., clicking "Pay now" at checkout for a €49.99 online purchase). The Merchant's payment page collects the User's card information (or a tokenised reference from a previous session) and submits it to the payment processing pipeline. The User may not yet be aware that EUDI Wallet SCA will be triggered — this depends on the PSP's risk assessment (step 3).
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: SCA trigger context: PSD2 Art. 97 mandates SCA for electronic payments above certain thresholds, first-time transactions, or when the PSP's Transaction Risk Analysis (TRA) flags the transaction. Low-value payments (< €30, cumulative < €100) may be exempted per RTS Art. 18.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 2. Merchant forwards authorization request to Issuer PSP
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Merchant's payment backend forwards the authorization request to the User's Issuer PSP (the bank that issued the User's payment card or account) via the card scheme network (Visa, Mastercard) or through an acquirer. The authorization message follows standard EMV 3-D Secure (3DS) or scheme-specific protocols. At this stage, the Merchant's role in the SCA flow is complete — all subsequent SCA steps happen between the PSP and the User's Wallet.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: PSP as RP: In the SCA flow, the Issuer PSP acts as the Relying Party — it is the entity that sends the OpenID4VP request and verifies the response. The Merchant never sees the SCA attestation or the KB-JWT; it only receives the final authorization approval/decline.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 3. Issuer PSP determines SCA is required
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The PSP's risk engine evaluates the transaction against PSD2/PSR regulatory triggers to determine whether SCA is legally required. The evaluation considers:
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Transaction amount — above the RTS Art. 18 exemption threshold (€30 individual / €100 cumulative)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Transaction Risk Analysis (TRA) — the PSP's fraud scoring model: if the transaction's risk score exceeds the reference fraud rate for the PSP's TRA exemption tier (RTS Art. 18(3)), SCA is required
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Merchant category — some categories (e.g., recurring subscriptions) may qualify for exemptions
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: User history — first-time transaction with this Merchant, unusual amount, or new device
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: If SCA is required, the PSP proceeds to build the OpenID4VP request (step 4). If exempt, the PSP approves directly without involving the Wallet.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 4. Issuer PSP builds OpenID4VP presentation request with transaction_data
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The PSP constructs the OpenID4VP authorization request with a DCQL query targeting the User's SCA Attestation, explicitly binding the transaction data to the request.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: OpenID4VP Presentation Request (JSON)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 5. Issuer PSP sends OpenID4VP request (JAR) to Wallet Unit
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The PSP signs the OpenID4VP request as a JAR JWS using its WRPAC private key and delivers it to the User's Wallet Unit. The delivery mechanism depends on the flow type:
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Push notification (primary) — the PSP sends a silent push notification (APNs/FCM) to the Wallet app containing the request_uri. The Wallet fetches the JAR, similar to cross-device flow §10.2 steps 8–9.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: App Link / Universal Link — for same-device flows (User is on their phone), the PSP redirects to the Wallet's registered URL scheme.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: QR code — for cross-device flows (User is on a desktop), the PSP renders a QR code containing the request_uri on the Merchant's checkout page.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The PSP's role in the EUDI ecosystem is identical to any other RP — it holds a WRPAC issued by an Access CA, registers its intended use (SCA attestation presentation), and signs its requests with the WRPAC key. The Wallet treats the PSP's request exactly like any other OpenID4VP request.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: OpenID4VP Authorization Request (JAR)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Issuer PSP logs an OPENID4VP_REQUEST_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 6. Wallet Unit verifies PSP WRPAC certificate chain
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet verifies the PSP's WRPAC certificate chain against the Access CA LoTE trust anchor, identical to §9.2 steps 8–10. The Wallet validates: (a) the JAR JWS signature using the WRPAC's public key, (b) the certificate chain up to the LoTE root, (c) the WRPAC's revocation status via OCSP/CRL, and (d) that the client_id in the JAR matches the WRPAC's SAN.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: For SCA flows, the WRPAC identifies the PSP as a financial institution (e.g., "Example Bank AG"). This verification is essential — it prevents a rogue entity from sending fake SCA requests that could trick Users into authenticating fraudulent transactions.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the WRPAC chain cannot be traced to a trusted LoTE anchor, the Wallet Unit MUST explicitly reject the SCA request and alert the User.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a WRPAC_CHAIN_VALIDATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 7. Wallet Unit validates SCA attestation type via VCT metadata
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet resolves the requested credential's VCT metadata to confirm it qualifies for SCA.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet verifies that category is urn:eu:europa:ec:eudi:sua:sca, confirming this is an SCA attestation.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the category does not match, the Wallet MUST reject the request.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs an SCA_ATTESTATION_TYPE_VALIDATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 8. Wallet Unit validates transaction_data type
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet checks that the type field in the transaction_data array (urn:eudi:sca:payment:1) exists in the credential's VCT metadata transaction_data_types map (step 7). This prevents the PSP from attaching an unsupported transaction type to the request — e.g., a payment PSP cannot send a urn:eudi:sca:account_access:1 transaction type if the SCA attestation's VCT metadata only lists payment types.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Type registry: Transaction data types follow a URN namespace (urn:eudi:sca:*) managed by the EUDI ecosystem. Each type has an associated JSON Schema and UI label set. The Wallet must reject unknown types to prevent rendering incomplete or misleading consent screens.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a TRANSACTION_DATA_TYPE_VALIDATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 9. Wallet Unit validates transaction payload against JSON Schema
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet validates the transaction_data[0].payload JSON object against the authoritative JSON Schema linked in the VCT metadata (schema_uri). The Schema defines: required fields (amount, currency, payee.name), field types (number, string, ISO 4217 currency codes), and constraints (e.g., amount > 0). This validation ensures the PSP provided structurally valid transaction data — not arbitrary or malformed JSON that could confuse the consent screen rendering (step 10).
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: If schema validation fails: The Wallet MUST reject the SCA request and display an error: "The payment request contains invalid data." This prevents the PSP from bypassing the display hierarchy by sending unexpected field structures.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a TRANSACTION_PAYLOAD_SCHEMA_VALIDATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 10. Wallet Unit renders consent screen with transaction details
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet dynamically renders the consent screen layout according to the TS12 display hierarchy.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: amount: 49.99, currency: EUR, payee.name: Online Store GmbH
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)
15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: From ui_labels.affirmative_action_label (de)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 11. User approves payment via biometric or PIN
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The User reviews the transaction details (amount, payee, IBAN) on the consent screen and taps "Zahlung bestätigen" (Confirm Payment) — the localised affirmative action label from the VCT metadata. The Wallet then requires biometric or PIN authentication to unlock the WSCA and authorise the KB-JWT signature.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The SCA flow requires at least two of three independent authentication factors (PSD2 Art. 97(1)):
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Knowledge — PIN or passcode (amr: pin_6_or_more_digits)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Inherence — biometric (fingerprint, face) (amr: fingerprint_device)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Possession — the device itself, proven by the KB-JWT signature (the device key never leaves the WSCA)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The amr array in the KB-JWT (step 12) documents which factors were applied, enabling the PSP to verify PSD2 compliance.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the User denies or authentication times out, the Wallet Unit aborts and returns an access_denied error to the PSP.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: User Authentication Token / Biometric Match
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a USER_PAYMENT_APPROVED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 12. Wallet Unit builds KB-JWT with SCA proof and transaction hash
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet builds the Key Binding JWT, which inherently acts as the requested SCA proof by dynamically linking the transaction details to the cryptographic signature.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Unique identifier, serves as the Authentication Code
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: PSD2 RTS Art. 4 — unique code linked to amount and payee
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Documents the authentication factors applied (knowledge + inherence = 2 of 3)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: PSD2 Art. 97 — at least 2 independent SCA elements
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: SHA-256 of the transaction_data[0].payload (base64url)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: PSD2 Art. 97(2) — dynamic linking: code bound to amount + payee
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Binds KB-JWT to the specific SD-JWT VC presentation
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Prevents replay with different credentials
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Key Binding JWT (KB-JWT) with SCA Proof
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a KB_JWT_SCA_PROOF_GENERATED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 13. Wallet Unit transmits encrypted JWE response to PSP
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Wallet encrypts the complete presentation response (SD-JWT VC with disclosed claims, KB-JWT with SCA proof) into a JWE using the PSP's ephemeral ECDH-ES public key (from the JAR's response_encryption_jwk), identical to §9.2 step 17. The JWE is POSTed to the PSP's response_uri (https://psp.example-bank.de/sca/callback).
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The encrypted payload contains the vp_token (the SD-JWT VC string with KB-JWT appended) and the state parameter for session correlation. The PSP's ephemeral private key is the only key that can decrypt this response — ensuring the SCA proof is not interceptable by the Merchant or any intermediary.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Encrypted JWE Response (vp_token)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs an ENCRYPTED_JWE_RESPONSE_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 14. Issuer PSP verifies SCA attestation signature
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The PSP decrypts the JWE (using its ephemeral private key, then immediately destroys it for forward secrecy) and parses the SD-JWT VC string. The PSP verifies the Issuer-JWT signature to confirm the SCA attestation was legitimately issued. Since the PSP is itself the issuer of the SCA attestation, it can verify the signature using its own public key — no LoTE lookup is needed.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The PSP also verifies the disclosed claims from the SD-JWT disclosures: user_id (the User's account identifier), card_reference (the payment instrument bound to this SCA attestation), and any other claims needed for the authorisation decision.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the SCA attestation signature is invalid, the Issuer PSP MUST reject the authorization and log a critical security alert representing possible fraudulent manipulation.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Issuer PSP logs an SCA_ATTESTATION_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 15. Issuer PSP verifies KB-JWT signature and SCA factors
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The PSP verifies the KB-JWT (the SCA proof) against the cnf.jwk public key bound in the SCA attestation's Issuer-JWT. The verification checks:
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Signature — ES256 signature is valid against the bound device key
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: aud — matches the PSP's client_id (prevents replay to a different PSP)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: nonce — matches the session nonce from step 4 (prevents replay of old KB-JWTs)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: jti — unique identifier, stored by the PSP to detect replay attempts (PSD2 RTS Art. 4 — unique authentication code)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: amr — contains at least 2 of 3 SCA factor categories: knowledge, inherence, possession. The PSP logs which factors were used for regulatory audit compliance
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: iat — within acceptable recency window (typically ≤ 300 seconds)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: If amr has fewer than 2 factors: The PSP MUST reject the SCA proof — PSD2 Art. 97(1) requires at least two independent elements. The PSP should log a compliance alert.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Issuer PSP logs a KB_JWT_SIGNATURE_VERIFIED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 16. Issuer PSP verifies dynamic linking (transaction hash match)
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The PSP performs the PSD2 dynamic linking verification — the critical step that binds the User's authentication to the specific transaction. The PSP recalculates SHA-256(base64url(transaction_data[0].payload)) from its own copy of the original transaction_data payload (step 4) and compares the digest against transaction_data_hashes[0] in the KB-JWT.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: If the hashes match, it proves irrefutably that: (a) the User saw and approved the exact amount (€49.99) and payee ("Online Store GmbH"), (b) the transaction details were not modified between the PSP's request and the User's approval, and (c) the KB-JWT is cryptographically bound to this specific transaction and cannot be reused for a different amount or payee.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: If the hash does not match: The PSP MUST reject the SCA — a hash mismatch indicates the transaction_data was tampered with between the PSP and the Wallet, which could mean a man-in-the-middle attack modified the transaction amount or payee. The PSP should log a security alert.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Issuer PSP logs a DYNAMIC_LINKING_HASH_VERIFIED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 17. Issuer PSP approves financial authorization
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: With all SCA verification steps passed (attestation signature ✅, KB-JWT signature ✅, SCA factors ≥ 2 ✅, dynamic linking hash match ✅), the PSP approves the financial authorisation. The PSP sends an approval response back to the card scheme network (Visa/Mastercard) or directly to the Merchant's acquirer. The PSP logs the complete SCA evidence trail for regulatory audit: jti (authentication code), amr (factors used), transaction_data_hashes (linked transaction), and the verification timestamp.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: PSD2 compliance record: The PSP must retain SCA evidence for at least 18 months per RTS Art. 29. The KB-JWT's jti serves as the unique authentication code required by PSD2 Art. 97(2) for dynamic linking.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Financial Authorization Approval / PSD2 Compliance Record
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Issuer PSP logs a FINANCIAL_AUTHORIZATION_APPROVED event.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: 18. Merchant confirms payment to User
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The Merchant receives the authorisation approval from the PSP (via the card scheme or acquirer) and updates the checkout session. The User sees a payment confirmation screen (e.g., "Payment successful — your order has been placed") in their browser or app. The Merchant never sees the SCA attestation, KB-JWT, or any Wallet-level details — it only receives a standard payment authorisation code.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: The entire SCA flow — from the User clicking "Pay" to seeing the confirmation — typically completes in 10–20 seconds, comparable to current 3-D Secure (3DS) challenge flows. The EUDI Wallet SCA flow replaces the bank's proprietary authentication app (e.g., TAN SMS, banking app redirect) with a standardised, interoperable mechanism.
passages.headingText: 15.8 Issuer-Requested SCA Sequence Diagram (Direct RP Model)

passages.text: In the third-party-requested SCA flow, a party other than the issuer bank (e.g., an AISP or PISP under PSD2/PSR) requests SCA from the User. The flow is structurally identical to §15.7 but with a different requesting party.
The requesting party is an AISP (Account Information Service Provider) or PISP (Payment Initiation Service Provider)
The AISP/PISP must be registered as an RP with a valid WRPAC
The transaction data may contain account access information rather than payment details
The issuer PSP may still be involved for authorization validation

TS12 defines the transaction_data parameter that is included in the OpenID4VP presentation request for SCA flows. This data binds the authentication to the specific transaction (PSD2 dynamic linking):
PSD2 Art. 97(2) requires that authentication codes are dynamically linked to a specific amount and payee. In the EUDI Wallet flow:
The transaction_data containing amount and payee is included in the presentation request
The Wallet Unit displays exact transaction details to the User
The User authenticates specifically for this transaction (not a generic auth)
The WSCA/WSCD signs a hash of the transaction data with the device-bound key
The SCA attestation in the response includes the signed transaction hash
The PSP verifies the signature, confirming the User authenticated for exactly this transaction
This satisfies the three pillars of PSD2 SCA:
Something only the user knows
PIN (WSCA/WSCD authentication)
Device-bound key in WSCA/WSCD
Biometric (WSCA/WSCD authentication)
TS12 defines four standardised payload schemas (non-normative examples — the exact claim names may vary by Type Metadata definition):
transaction_id, payee (name + id), currency, amount
urn:eudi:sca:login_risk_transaction:1
urn:eudi:sca:account_access:1
Account information access (AISP)
E-mandate for payee-initiated tx
transaction_id, conditional: purpose or payment_payload
TS12 §3.6 mandates an amr claim in the Key Binding JWT that documents the authentication factors used. This is critical for PSD2 RTS traceability:
The jti claim serves as the Authentication Code required by PSD2 RTS. The amr proves at least 2 of 3 SCA factors were applied.
The exact JSON Schema from ts12-urn-eudi-sca-payment-1-data-model.json:

ARF Discussion Paper Topic W (v0.97, May 2025) formalises the Wallet's transactional data handling for payment SCA and other use cases requiring User authorisation of a specific action. While §15.10 covers the transaction_data structure from TS12, Topic W establishes High-Level Requirements (TD_01–TD_04) that define the Wallet Unit's obligations and — critically — the RP's ability to control the consent experience.
The Wallet Unit's role in transactional data handling spans three lifecycle phases:
Registration — the RP registers as an authenticator and issues "service credentials" (SCA attestations) to the Wallet Unit
Authentication — the Wallet receives a presentation request with transactional data, displays it to the User, and returns a signed response
De-registration — unlinking the Wallet Unit from the service

Wallet Unit SHALL process and render transactional data included in the presentation request. SHALL display transactional data (or parts of it) to the User in a clear, understandable, and accurate manner when obtaining User confirmation. Content and rendering rules are defined by an Attestation Rulebook (ARF Topic 12).
RP must structure transaction_data so the Wallet can extract displayable fields (amount, payee, date). The RP should define rendering rules in the applicable Attestation Rulebook.

Wallet Unit SHALL deliver transactional data (or parts of it) in the response to the requesting RP, if required by the use case. Format and content SHALL be set in an Attestation Rulebook or in information provided to the Wallet Unit in the presentation request.
RP receives the transactional data back in the signed response — enabling server-side verification of what the User saw and approved.

Wallet Unit SHALL sign the response (including transactional data) with the private key of the attestation, using the mechanisms provided by SD-JWT VC and ISO/IEC 18013-5. Note: Such a response constitutes a proof of transaction, as well as fulfils the requirement of the authentication code required in PSD2.

The signed response IS the PSD2 Dynamic Linking proof. The KB-JWT signature over transaction_data_hashes creates a cryptographic binding between the User's approval and the specific transaction amount/payee.

Wallet Unit SHALL dynamically adapt the dialog displayed to the User (font size, colour, background colour, text position, button labels to "approve" or "reject" a transaction) based on the transactional data contained in the presentation request, per Attestation Rulebook rules.

RP can influence the User's consent UI appearance via structured transactional data fields. This enables payment-specific experiences (e.g., a green "Pay €149.99" button instead of a generic "Approve").
The TD_03 requirement closes the loop on PSD2 Art. 97(2) Dynamic Linking (see also §15.11). The complete chain of proof is:
This satisfies all three PSD2 Dynamic Linking requirements:
Payer awareness: The Wallet displays the exact transaction details (TD_01)
Binding to amount and payee: The transaction_data_hashes in the KB-JWT are computed over the amount and payee fields (TD_03)
Integrity: Any modification to the transaction data after User approval invalidates the KB-JWT signature

Topic W delegates content and rendering rules to Attestation Rulebooks (ARF Topic 12). For payment SCA, the relevant Rulebook will be defined by the payment scheme (e.g., Visa, Mastercard) or the national payment authority. The Rulebook specifies:
Which fields of transaction_data are mandatory for display (e.g., amount and payee are always displayed)
How the consent dialog should be styled (TD_04) — colours, button labels, layout
Whether the transactional data is returned verbatim or as a hash in the response (TD_02)
The scope of information logged in the Wallet's transaction log (linked to TS10)

Key constraint: Topic W explicitly states that the discussion is ONLY intended to establish HLRs — the necessary technical specifications will be developed after agreement. RPs should track the development of payment-specific Attestation Rulebooks for exact field definitions.
The transactional data mechanism is not limited to payments. Topic W identifies a second core use case:

Remote Qualified Electronic Signature (QES): A signing service RP can include the document hash in transaction_data, causing the Wallet to display "Sign document: contract_v2.pdf" to the User before the WSCA signs the KB-JWT. The signed response then serves as the QES activation proof.

Any use case where Article 5f(2) requires strong user authentication — transport, energy, health, postal services, digital infrastructure, education, telecoms — can leverage transaction_data to bind the authentication to a specific transaction context.
Cross-references: §15.10 (transaction data structure), §15.11 (Dynamic Linking), §15.14 (payment payload JSON schema), §21.2 (PSD2/PSR and SCA bridge).

This group explores complex credential requests and privacy-preserving mechanisms. It explains how to construct Digital Credentials Query Language (DCQL) requests, implement pseudonym-based authentication (WebAuthn binding), verify combined presentations involving Legal Person Identification Data (LPID), and construct age verification pipelines.

The EUDI Wallet supports pseudonyms as an alternative to attribute-based identification. This is a fundamental privacy feature: Users can interact with services without revealing their legal identity, yet still authenticate persistently across sessions. For RPs, pseudonym support is not optional — Art. 5b(9) of eIDAS 2.0 mandates that RPs shall not refuse pseudonyms where identification is not required by Union or national law.

The pseudonym functionality is defined in [CIR 2024/2979], Art. 14, which references [W3C WebAuthn] Level 2 (Annex V) as the technical specification. ARF v2.8.0 (incorporating Discussion Paper Topic E) further elaborates four use cases, three pseudonym types, and associated High-Level Requirements (PA_01–PA_22).
The ARF defines three distinct pseudonym types, each offering different assurance guarantees:
User can prove possession of the pseudonym and authenticate as it
RP knows the same User is returning
A third party (e.g., Attestation Provider acting as issuer) attests that a pseudonym is owned by a User
RP can verify the pseudonym was issued by a trusted party
Scope rate-limited pseudonym
User is guaranteed to control only a certain number of pseudonyms within a given scope
RP can verify uniqueness guarantees (e.g., one vote per person)

Key insight for RPs: Verifiable pseudonyms (via WebAuthn) are the baseline. Attested pseudonyms are already possible by any Attestation Provider issuing a (Q)EAA attesting to a pseudonym. Scope rate-limited pseudonyms require new cryptographic protocols not yet standardised — WebAuthn's security model has no concept of issuance ceilings, meaning any user can generate an unlimited number of credentials for the same RP origin. Enforcing an "at most N" constraint requires a fundamentally different scheme (e.g., blind signatures with serial number tracking, or group signatures with revocation accumulators) that neither WebAuthn nor any current EUDI specification provides. The ARF (Topic E) has published guiding HLRs (PA_27–PA_31) but the Commission will not develop a concrete scheme; future implementing acts or standards body coordination is expected.

A pseudonym is a data minimisation mechanism: it enables users to interact with RPs without disclosing their legal identity, fulfilling the eIDAS mandate that wallets must support pseudonym generation at the user's request (Art. 5a(4)) and that RPs must not refuse pseudonyms where identification is not legally required (Art. 5b(9)). Pseudonyms are not full anonymity — the RP still interacts with a real person and may maintain account state — but they prevent the RP from automatically accumulating more personal data than the interaction requires.
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: The following table maps each pseudonym type to the specific assurance it provides and the assurance it does not provide:
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: The user controls the private key bound to this pseudonym
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: WebAuthn challenge-response: wallet signs with private key, RP verifies with registered public key
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: Does not prove who the user is
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: Session-bound authentication
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: The same credential is being used again (same user as last session)
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: Stable public key identifier scoped to the RP (see Use Case A in §16.3 and the progressive assurance pattern in §16.4)
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: Does not prove the user has not delegated the device
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: The pseudonym is unique to this RP; other RPs receive different pseudonyms
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: WebAuthn credential scoping: key material is bound to a specific RP origin (ARF Topic E)
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: Two RPs cannot correlate their pseudonyms to discover they serve the same user — this is a privacy guarantee, not a limitation
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: A pseudonym alone does not prove the user's legal or civil identity
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: Stronger identity assurance requires a separate PID presentation or onboarding step
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: Scope-rate-limit assurance (scope rate-limited pseudonyms only)
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: The user controls at most N pseudonyms within a defined scope
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: Cryptographic protocol (not yet standardised)
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: Does not prove which specific individual holds the pseudonym
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: RP decision point: If an RP's regulatory obligations require knowing the user's legal identity (e.g., AML/KYC for banks under AMLD Art. 13), a pseudonym alone is insufficient — the RP must request PID presentation during onboarding (§16.4). After initial identification, the pseudonym can serve as the persistent authentication mechanism for returning sessions, providing account continuity without re-presenting PID.
passages.headingText: 16.2.1 Pseudonym Assurance Taxonomy: What a Pseudonym Proves

passages.text: A: Pseudonymous authentication
Wallet generates a unique, persistent pseudonym per RP. User registers it, then authenticates with it in subsequent sessions. Multiple pseudonyms per RP are allowed.
Forum account, loyalty programme
B: Attributes + subsequent pseudonym auth
Same as A, but User also presents verifiable attributes (e.g., age_over_18) during registration alongside the pseudonym
Age-gated streaming service account
Verifiable (+ combined presentation)
C: Rate-limited participation
RP can verify that a User controls at most N pseudonyms within a scope. Prevents Sybil attacks (multiple accounts, vote stuffing, troll farms).
Electronic voting, one-per-person surveys
D: Linkable pseudonymous auth
A single pseudonym is recognised across multiple RPs, enabling cross-RP linkability within a sector
e-Commerce order → carrier pickup with same pseudonym
Verifiable (same pseudonym registered at multiple RPs)

Art. 5b(9) creates a default-accept rule for pseudonyms, with identification-required exceptions carved out by EU or national law. The following table maps common RP sectors to their identification obligations:
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Legal Identification Required?
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Must identify for KYC; pseudonym NOT sufficient for account opening
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Banks (returning customer auth)
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Must accept pseudonym for session authentication post-KYC
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: SCA attestation bound to identity; pseudonym alone NOT sufficient
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Identity required in most Member States
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Legal identification required for medical records
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Must accept pseudonym; VLOPs explicitly referenced
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Age verification (retail/content)
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Must accept pseudonym + age_over_18 (Use Case B)
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: Legal identification typically required
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: RP compliance risk: An RP that refuses pseudonyms when identification is not legally required is in direct violation of Art. 5b(9). This is enforceable by the supervisory authority designated under Art. 5b(11).
passages.headingText: 16.4 Legal Framework: When Must RPs Accept Pseudonyms?

passages.text: [CIR 2024/2979] Art. 14 and Annex V mandate [W3C WebAuthn] Level 2 as the technical specification for pseudonym generation. The Wallet Unit acts as the WebAuthn Authenticator; the User's browser is the Client; the RP is the Relying Party Server.

ARF v2.8.0 change: ARF v2.8.0 (incorporating the final Discussion Paper for Topic E) makes WebAuthn implementation optional for Wallet Units (PA_22 change). Wallet Providers MAY implement alternative pseudonym technologies, provided they meet the HLRs. However, WebAuthn remains the only currently standardised approach.
When a User registers a pseudonym at an RP, the browser calls navigator.credentials.create() with the following parameters:
Key parameters for RP implementers:
Scopes the pseudonym to this RP (PA_04: pseudonyms SHALL be unique per RP)
The RP maps this to the pseudonym account internally
Recommended for privacy — "direct" or "enterprise" attestation types risk cross-RP linkability (§16.9)
authenticatorSelection.residentKey
Discoverable credential — enables passwordless login
authenticatorSelection.userVerification
User must authenticate to the Wallet Unit (biometric/PIN)
P-256 ECDSA — mandatory in EUDI ecosystem
The Wallet Unit (Authenticator) responds with a PublicKeyCredential containing:
The RP extracts the public key from attestationObject.authData and stores it alongside the credentialId and user.id.
For subsequent pseudonymous logins, the RP calls navigator.credentials.get():
The Wallet Unit (Authenticator):
Prompts the User to select a pseudonym for this RP (if multiple exist)
Authenticates the User (biometric/PIN — OS-level, not WSCA/WSCD)
Signs the challenge with the private key corresponding to the selected pseudonym
Returns the signed assertion with the credentialId and userHandle
The RP verifies the signature against the stored public key. If valid, the User is authenticated as their pseudonym — no PID, no legal identity, no attributable data.
1. User navigates to RP and chooses "Sign in with EUDI Wallet"
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The User visits the Relying Party's website and opts to register or sign in using their EUDI Wallet rather than creating a traditional password-based account. The RP's login page should present the EUDI Wallet option alongside traditional login methods (per ARF HLR PA_01 — Wallet Units SHALL support pseudonym creation during online presentation flows). The RP detects whether the browser supports navigator.credentials.create() (WebAuthn L3 §6.1) before offering this option; if not supported, the RP falls back to a QR-code-based credential presentation flow (§10) instead.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If denied: The User simply continues with a traditional login. No data is exchanged.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a PSEUDONYM_REGISTRATION_INITIATED event.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 2. RP Server sends PublicKeyCredentialCreationOptions to Browser
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP's backend generates a WebAuthn registration challenge and sends it to the browser via the JavaScript WebAuthn API (WebAuthn L3 §6.1.3). The payload defines the RP identity, an internal user handle, and a cryptographically random challenge:
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The key parameters are documented in §16.5.1. Notably, alg: -7 (ES256 / P-256 ECDSA) is the mandatory algorithm in the EUDI ecosystem, and attestation: "none" is recommended to avoid cross-RP linkability risks (§16.9). The user.id is an opaque RP-assigned identifier — it must NOT contain any PID attributes or real-world identifiers, as this would defeat the pseudonym's privacy guarantee (PA_04).
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: PublicKeyCredentialCreationOptions (JSON Payload)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 3. Browser verifies rp.id matches current origin
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The browser performs the WebAuthn origin validation check (WebAuthn L3 §6.1.4, step 7): it verifies that the rp.id in the PublicKeyCredentialCreationOptions is a registrable domain suffix of the page's effective origin. For example, if the User is visiting https://forum.example.com/login, then rp.id: "forum.example.com" passes but rp.id: "evil.com" is rejected. This is the primary phishing defence — it prevents a malicious site from requesting a credential scoped to a different domain.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If validation fails: The browser throws a SecurityError DOMException and the WebAuthn ceremony is aborted. The RP should display a user-friendly error explaining that the authentication could not be completed.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs a WEBAUTHN_ORIGIN_VALIDATION_FAILED security event if the client reports an aborted configuration.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 4. Browser forwards WebAuthn creation request to Wallet Unit
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The browser invokes the platform authenticator — in this case, the EUDI Wallet Unit — via the WebAuthn Authenticator API (WebAuthn L3 §7.3.2, authenticatorMakeCredential). The browser passes the rpEntity, userEntity, credTypesAndPubKeyAlgs, and the hash of clientDataJSON to the authenticator. The Wallet Unit acts as a platform authenticator (authenticatorAttachment: "platform"), meaning it runs on the same device as the browser rather than over USB/NFC/BLE.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: EUDI-specific note: The Wallet Unit's authenticator role is distinct from its WSCA/WSCD role. Pseudonym key pairs are managed by the OS-level keystore (Android Keystore / iOS Secure Enclave), not by the WSCA/WSCD used for PID and attestation credentials (§16.4). This separation ensures that pseudonym operations do not require interaction with the eIDAS trust infrastructure.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 5. Wallet Unit prompts User to register passkey
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit displays a system-level consent dialogue presenting the RP's identity (rp.name from the creation options) and asking the User to confirm creation of a new passkey. The prompt typically reads: "Create a passkey for forum.example.com?". The Wallet Unit must display the verified rp.id, not just the rp.name, to prevent social engineering attacks where a malicious RP sets a misleading rp.name (e.g., "Your Bank") while the rp.id is a different domain.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If denied: The authenticator returns an error (NotAllowedError), the ceremony is aborted, and no key pair is generated. The User can retry or choose a different login method.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a PASSKEY_CREATION_CONSENT_DENIED event.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 6. User approves and authenticates via biometric or PIN
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The User authorises the credential creation via local user verification — typically a biometric gesture (fingerprint, face) or device PIN/pattern. This satisfies the userVerification: "required" constraint from step 2. The authentication happens at the OS level (Android BiometricPrompt / iOS LAContext), not via the WSCA/WSCD used for eIDAS attestation credentials — pseudonym key operations deliberately avoid the eIDAS trust chain to maintain the separation between pseudonymous and identified interactions (§16.4).
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If authentication fails: The authenticator returns NotAllowedError after the OS's maximum retry count (typically 3–5 attempts before falling back to device PIN). The User can retry from the beginning.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a LOCAL_USER_AUTHENTICATION_FAILED event upon OS maximum retry threshold.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 7. Wallet Unit generates EC P-256 key pair scoped to rp.id
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit generates a fresh ECDSA P-256 key pair (alg: -7 / ES256) inside the device's hardware-backed keystore (Android StrongBox / iOS Secure Enclave). The private key is non-exportable — it never leaves the secure hardware and is bound to the combination of rp.id + user.id (WebAuthn L3 §7.3.2, step 3). This scoping is the mechanism that enforces ARF HLR PA_04: "Pseudonyms SHALL be unique per Relying Party" — the same Wallet Unit generates a different, unlinkable key pair for every RP, making cross-site tracking via credential ID impossible.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Key storage: As a discoverable credential (residentKey: "required"), the credentialId, rp.id, and user.id are stored persistently in the authenticator's credential storage — enabling the passwordless login flow in Phase 2 where the Wallet can look up credentials by rp.id alone.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: EC P-256 Key Pair (Hardware-Bound, Non-Exportable)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 8. Wallet Unit returns PublicKeyCredential to Browser
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit constructs and returns a PublicKeyCredential object (WebAuthn L3 §6.1.3, step 20) containing the newly created credential:
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The attestationObject is CBOR-encoded and contains the authData field (WebAuthn L3 §7.1), which in turn embeds the credentialPublicKey in COSE_Key format (RFC 9052). When attestation: "none" is used (recommended — §16.9), the attStmt is an empty map and fmt is "none", providing no attestation to the RP about the authenticator's provenance.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: PublicKeyCredential (JSON Object with CBOR-encoded elements)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 9. Browser forwards credential to RP Server
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The browser serialises the PublicKeyCredential and transmits it to the RP's backend, typically via a POST to a /webauthn/register endpoint. The browser includes the clientDataJSON (which the RP will parse to verify the challenge, origin, and type fields) and the raw attestationObject. The RP's transport layer should use TLS 1.3 to protect the credential in transit — though the credential itself is not secret (it contains only the public key), integrity protection prevents tampering with the challenge binding.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 10. RP Server verifies attestation and stores public key
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP's backend performs the WebAuthn registration verification procedure (WebAuthn L3 §8.1):
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Parse clientDataJSON and verify type is "webauthn.create", challenge matches the server-issued challenge, and origin matches the expected RP origin
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Decode attestationObject (CBOR) and extract authData
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Verify rp.id hash — the first 32 bytes of authData must equal SHA-256(rp.id)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Check flags — UP (User Present) and UV (User Verified) bits must both be set (since userVerification: "required")
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Extract credentialPublicKey from authData.attestedCredentialData (COSE_Key format)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Verify attestation — for fmt: "none", this is a no-op; for "packed" or "tpm", the RP verifies the attestation signature chain
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Store the credentialId, credentialPublicKey, and signCount in the RP's database, associated with the pseudonym account
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If verification fails (e.g., challenge mismatch, origin mismatch, flag check failure): The RP rejects the registration and returns an error. No credential is stored.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: If verification fails, the RP Server MUST reject the registration and log a WEBAUTHN_ATTESTATION_VERIFICATION_FAILED security alert. On success, the RP Server logs a PSEUDONYM_CREDENTIAL_REGISTERED event.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 11. User returns to RP in a later session
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: In a subsequent session (hours, days, or months later), the User returns to the Relying Party's website and wants to log in pseudonymously. The RP's login page detects that the browser supports WebAuthn and offers a "Sign in with passkey" button. Since the credential was created as a discoverable credential (residentKey: "required" in step 2), the RP does not need to know the User's credentialId in advance — the authenticator will look it up automatically by rp.id.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 12. RP Server sends PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions to Browser
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP's backend generates an authentication challenge and sends it to the browser via navigator.credentials.get() (WebAuthn L3 §6.1.4):
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Note the absence of allowCredentials — since the credential is discoverable (a "resident key"), the Wallet Unit can locate it by rpId alone, enabling a truly passwordless flow. The challenge is a fresh, cryptographically random value (minimum 16 bytes per WebAuthn L3 §14.4.3) that the server stores server-side for verification in step 18. See §16.5.2 for parameter details.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a WEBAUTHN_ASSERTION_OPTIONS_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions (JSON Payload)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 13. Browser forwards assertion request to Wallet Unit
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The browser performs the same origin validation as in step 3 (verifying rpId is a registrable domain suffix of the current origin), then invokes the platform authenticator via authenticatorGetAssertion (WebAuthn L3 §7.3.3). The browser passes the rpId and the SHA-256 hash of clientDataJSON (containing the challenge) to the Wallet Unit.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If no credentials found: If the Wallet Unit has no discoverable credentials for this rpId, it returns NotAllowedError. The RP should fall back to offering a traditional login or a new passkey registration.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: If the authenticator returns a null lookup, the Wallet Unit logs a NO_DISCOVERABLE_CREDENTIAL_FOUND event.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 14. User selects pseudonym and authenticates via biometric or PIN
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit searches its credential store for all discoverable credentials matching the rpId (WebAuthn L3 §7.3.3, step 4). If multiple pseudonyms exist for this RP (e.g., the User registered separate pseudonyms for different purposes), the Wallet presents a selection dialogue. The User picks the desired pseudonym and authenticates via biometric or PIN to satisfy userVerification: "required".
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Single credential shortcut: If only one credential exists for this rpId, the Wallet may skip the selection dialogue and proceed directly to the user verification prompt, depending on the platform's UX policy.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 15. Wallet Unit signs challenge with private key for this rp.id
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit constructs the authenticatorData (WebAuthn L3 §7.1): the rpIdHash (SHA-256 of rpId), flags (UP=1, UV=1), and an incremented signCount. It then computes signature = ECDSA-P256-SHA256(authenticatorData || clientDataHash) using the private key from the hardware keystore (WebAuthn L3 §7.3.3, step 8). The signature is in the DER-encoded ASN.1 format specified by ECDSA (RFC 6979).
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Sign counter: The signCount is incremented on each use and provides a cloned authenticator detection mechanism — if the RP receives a signCount lower than the previously stored value, it indicates the credential may have been cloned (WebAuthn L3 §7.1, Signature Counter Considerations).
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: ECDSA P-256 Signature (DER-encoded ASN.1 over authenticatorData || clientDataHash)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 16. Wallet Unit returns AuthenticatorAssertionResponse to Browser
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Wallet Unit returns the AuthenticatorAssertionResponse object (WebAuthn L3 §6.2.2) to the browser:
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The userHandle field contains the user.id that the RP assigned during registration (step 2) — this allows the RP to identify which account to authenticate against, even if the credentialId is not pre-known.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: AuthenticatorAssertionResponse (JSON Payload with CBOR/DER arrays)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 17. Browser forwards signed assertion to RP Server
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The browser serialises the AuthenticatorAssertionResponse and transmits it to the RP's backend, typically via a POST to a /webauthn/authenticate endpoint. The transmission includes the clientDataJSON, authenticatorData, signature, and userHandle. As with registration (step 9), the transport should use TLS 1.3, though the assertion payload contains no secrets — it is a challenge-response proof of possession.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 18. RP Server verifies signature against stored public key
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP's backend performs the WebAuthn authentication verification procedure (WebAuthn L3 §8.2):
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Look up credential — use userHandle (or credentialId) to retrieve the stored credentialPublicKey and signCount from the database
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)
