passages.text: Parse clientDataJSON — verify type is "webauthn.get", challenge matches the server-issued challenge (from step 12), and origin matches the expected RP origin
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Verify rpIdHash — the first 32 bytes of authenticatorData must equal SHA-256(rpId)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Check flags — UP (User Present) and UV (User Verified) bits must both be set
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Verify signature — compute ECDSA-P256-SHA256-Verify(credentialPublicKey, authenticatorData || SHA-256(clientDataJSON), signature)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Check signCount — if the received signCount is less than or equal to the stored value, the RP should flag a potential cloned authenticator (WebAuthn L3 §8.2, step 21)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Update signCount — store the new signCount value for future comparisons
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If verification fails (challenge mismatch, signature invalid, sign count regression): The RP rejects the authentication attempt and returns an error. For sign count regression specifically, the RP should log a security alert — this may indicate an authenticator cloning attack.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server MUST log a WEBAUTHN_ASSERTION_VERIFICATION_FAILED event upon any signature or challenge mismatch. A CLONED_AUTHENTICATOR_DETECTED critical alert is mandated if the received signCount is lower than the stored value.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 19. RP Server authenticates User as pseudonym (no real identity)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Having verified the signature, the RP creates an authenticated session for the User under their pseudonym. The session is bound to the opaque user.id and credentialId — the RP has no access to the User's PID, legal name, date of birth, or any other attributable identity data. The pseudonym is unlinkable across RPs by design: each RP gets a unique key pair (PA_04), and the credentialId is RP-scoped. Even if two RPs collude, they cannot correlate their pseudonymous users unless the User voluntarily performs a cross-site identity linkage (§16.8).
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Combining with attributes: If the RP needs both a pseudonym and a specific attribute (e.g., age_over_18), it can combine this WebAuthn flow with an OpenID4VP presentation request — see §16.7 (Use Case B: Pseudonym and Attributes). The pseudonym key serves as the persistent account identifier while the attribute attestation provides the one-time age assurance.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a PSEUDONYMOUS_SESSION_ESTABLISHED event upon authenticated session creation.
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Pseudonymous Authenticated Session Token (Cookie / Bearer Token)
passages.headingText: 16.6 Pseudonym Registration and Authentication Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: This is the most common real-world pseudonym scenario. The User creates an account with a pseudonym AND proves age_over_18 at the same time. The RP gets age assurance without learning the User's name, date of birth, or any other identifying information.
passages.headingText: 16.7 Use Case B: Pseudonym and Attributes (Age Verification)

passages.text: 1. User visits age-gated platform
The User visits an age-gated content platform (e.g., streaming service), triggering the onboarding flow that requires both a durable account identifier and age verification.
Audit Telemetry: The Relying Party logs an AGE_GATED_ONBOARDING_INITIATED event.
2. RP Server requests pseudonym + age_over_18
The Relying Party requests both a persistent pseudonym and the age_over_18 attribute via a combined DCQL and WebAuthn flow.
Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a COMBINED_DCQL_WEBAUTHN_REQUEST_DISPATCHED event.
Artifact Produced: Combined Authorization Request (Integrating WebAuthn ceremony within the same TLS session)
3. Wallet Unit presents pseudonym and SD-JWT VC
The Wallet Unit presents both the newly generated pseudonym credential AND an SD-JWT VC with age_over_18 selectively disclosed, ensuring data minimisation.
Audit Telemetry: The Wallet Unit logs a COMBINED_PRESENTATION_DISPATCHED event.
Artifact Produced: SD-JWT VC Presentation combined with PublicKeyCredential
4. RP Server creates account linked to pseudonym

The Relying Party evaluates the presentation, verifies the age attribute, and creates a durable account linked solely to the pseudonym, permanently flagging the account as age-verified without storing any underlying personal data.
Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs an AGE_VERIFIED_ACCOUNT_CREATED event.
Artifact Produced: Bound Account Record (Database specific: Identity=Pseudonym, Age_Verified=True)
5. User authenticates in subsequent sessions
For any subsequent logins, the User authenticates using only the WebAuthn pseudonym credential; no further attributes are needed because the account is already verified.
Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a PSEUDONYMOUS_SESSION_ESTABLISHED event.

Late attribute binding: The ARF explicitly requires that attributes can be bound to a pseudonym after initial registration — not just at registration time (Topic E, Appendix A, Question 1). This means step 2 above can happen in a separate session, days or weeks after the pseudonym was first created. This pattern is the basis for the Progressive Assurance journey documented in §16.13.

The pseudonym registration (WebAuthn create()) and the attribute presentation (OpenID4VP DCQL) are two separate protocol exchanges. WebAuthn generates a key pair; OpenID4VP presents a signed credential. Neither protocol is aware of the other. The ARF explicitly acknowledges this as an open challenge (Topic E, §6.3, Challenge 1): "On its own, [W3C WebAuthn] does not support enable a Relying Party to obtain a strong guarantee ensuring that presentation of attributes as well as the registering of a pseudonym is performed by the same User."
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: This is a critical issue for RPs implementing Use Case B (pseudonym + attributes). Without binding, a malicious User could register a pseudonym on one device and present another User's age_over_18 attestation from a different device, creating a fraudulent age-verified account.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The following binding strategies are available, listed from most practical (deployable today) to most robust (future standardisation):
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: RP maintains a server-side session (e.g., HTTP-only cookie or opaque token). Both the WebAuthn ceremony and the OpenID4VP ceremony happen within the same TLS-authenticated browser session. The session acts as the continuity artifact.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Reasonable — both ceremonies require user presence on the same browser/device within the same session
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: RP embeds the WebAuthn registration challenge inside the OpenID4VP nonce parameter (or vice versa), creating a causal chain. The RP verifies that both responses reference the same challenge material.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Stronger than session-only — proves the two ceremonies were initiated by the same server-side transaction
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 3. Temporal Proximity + User Verification
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Both ceremonies enforce userVerification: "required". If both biometric confirmations happen within seconds on the same device, the RP has high confidence of same-user continuity.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Probabilistic — strong for same-device, weaker for cross-device
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 4. Cryptographic Binding (WSCA proof)
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: ARF Topic K (§3.4, ACP_10–ACP_15) defines a mechanism where the Wallet Unit proves that the private keys of two attestations are managed by the same WSCD. If the pseudonym key and the PID key are both in the WSCA, this proof would cryptographically bind them.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Strongest — hardware-level proof of same-WSCD key management
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: ❌ Not yet standardised — guiding HLRs only
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 5. Attested Pseudonym (alternative path)
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Instead of WebAuthn + OpenID4VP, the Attestation Provider issues a (Q)EAA attesting a pseudonym to the User. The issuance process (OID4VCI) inherently binds the pseudonym to the User's PID via the Attestation Provider's identity verification.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Strong — the Attestation Provider guarantees binding during issuance
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: ⚠️ Available but requires Attestation Provider — no EU-wide pseudonym attestation type defined yet
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Recommended RP approach (today): Combine strategies 1–3: maintain a server-side session token, embed the WebAuthn challenge in the OpenID4VP nonce, and require both ceremonies within a tight temporal window on the same browser context. This provides a defence-in-depth approach:
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 1. RP Server creates session with embedded challenge
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The RP Server generates a cryptographic session that will bind both ceremonies together. Two artifacts are created:
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: session_id: A high-entropy opaque token (e.g., 256-bit random, stored server-side) that identifies this specific onboarding transaction. It is delivered to the browser as an HTTP-only, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: challenge_R: A cryptographic challenge (32 bytes, base64url-encoded) that will be embedded in both the WebAuthn registration and the OpenID4VP nonce — creating a causal chain between the two ceremonies.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The short TTL (5 minutes) ensures temporal proximity — both ceremonies must complete within this window. If either ceremony times out, the session expires and the user must restart.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a BINDING_SESSION_CREATED event.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Session Token (HTTP-only) and challenge_R
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 2. Browser initiates WebAuthn registration via navigator.credentials.create()
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The Browser calls navigator.credentials.create() with the RP Server's challenge. The challenge field contains challenge_R — the same value that will later appear in the OpenID4VP nonce:
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The authenticator (WSCA/platform authenticator) generates a new P-256 key pair, binds it to the RP's origin (example-bank.de), and returns a PublicKeyCredential containing the credentialId and the public key.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: attestation: "none" follows §16.9's recommendation — the RP does not need authenticator identity verification for pseudonym use cases, and requesting attestation introduces linkability risks.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Artifact Produced: PublicKeyCredentialCreationOptions (Client-Side JS execution)
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 3. Browser forwards PublicKeyCredential to RP Server
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The Browser receives the WebAuthn registration response from the authenticator and forwards the PublicKeyCredential object to the RP Server. This response payload contains:
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: credentialId: A unique identifier for the newly created credential (base64url-encoded)
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: publicKey: The P-256 public key that will be used for future authentication
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: signCount: Initially 0 for newly registered credentials
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The Browser stores this credential in the platform WebAuthn manager, scoped to the RP's origin. From this point forward, the User can authenticate to this RP using the same authenticator (biometric/PIN) without needing to present their EUDI Wallet again.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a WEBAUTHN_REGISTRATION_PAYLOAD_RECEIVED event.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Artifact Produced: PublicKeyCredential response payload
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 4. RP Server stores WebAuthn credential in session
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The RP Server receives the PublicKeyCredential, verifies the attestation response (even with attestation: "none", the RP must validate the clientDataJSON.challenge matches challenge_R), and extracts the public key. The credential is stored in the session — not yet persisted to the user database, because the binding is incomplete until the OpenID4VP ceremony confirms the user's verified attributes.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The session transitions to PSEUDONYM_REGISTERED state, indicating that the first ceremony is complete:
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a PSEUDONYM_REGISTRATION_SESSION_BOUND event.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Artifact Produced: In-Flight Session State (PSEUDONYM_REGISTERED)
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 5. RP Server sends OpenID4VP Authorization Request with challenge-embedded nonce
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The RP Server constructs an OpenID4VP Authorization Request, crucially embedding challenge_R as the nonce parameter. This is the binding mechanism — the Wallet's response will contain a KB-JWT whose nonce claim must match the WebAuthn challenge, proving both ceremonies were initiated by the same server-side transaction.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The dcql_query requests only age_over_18 — the minimum attribute needed for an age-gated platform, following data minimisation principles (Art. 5b(2)).
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a CHALLENGE_EMBEDDED_OID4VP_REQUEST_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Artifact Produced: OpenID4VP Authorization Request (JSON Payload) with nonce bound to WebAuthn challenge
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 6. Browser returns vp_token with SD-JWT VC presentation
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The Wallet presents the SD-JWT VC containing the age_over_18 claim. The presentation includes a Key Binding JWT (KB-JWT) whose nonce field echoes the challenge:
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The KB-JWT payload contains "nonce": "dBjftJeZ4CVP-mB92K27uhbUJU1p1r_wW1gFWFOEjXk" — the same challenge used in the WebAuthn registration. This proves that the VP was created in response to the same transaction.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Artifact Produced: vp_token encompassing SD-JWT VC and KB-JWT (Nonce=challenge_R)
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: 7. RP Server verifies nonce match and completes session binding
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: The RP Server performs the critical binding verification:
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Verify the SD-JWT VC — issuer signature, revocation status, holder binding (KB-JWT signature against the cnf key)
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Verify nonce match — the KB-JWT's nonce must exactly match challenge_R from the session
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Verify session_id match — the request originates from the same browser session (cookie validation)
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Verify temporal proximity — both ceremonies completed within the session's TTL window
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Failure Path: If any verification check fails, the RP MUST terminate the binding session. A nonce or session mismatch represents a critical threat and MUST trigger a CROSS_CEREMONY_BINDING_FAILED SIEM alert.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: On success, the RP Server logs a CROSS_CEREMONY_BINDING_VERIFIED event, confirming identity continuity.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Finalized Session State (BOUND) containing linked assurance levels
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: Key insight: The session token is not a mere convenience — it is the binding artifact that ties two otherwise unrelated protocol exchanges to the same User. The RP MUST ensure this session cannot be hijacked (HTTP-only, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookies; or opaque bearer tokens with short TTL). If the session is compromised, the binding guarantee is void.
passages.headingText: 16.7.3 Same-User Binding: How RPs Guarantee Pseudonym–Attribute Continuity

passages.text: In Use Case D, a User registers the same pseudonym at multiple RPs within a sector, enabling those RPs to correlate interactions.
passages.headingText: 16.8 Use Case D: Cross-RP Linkable Pseudonym

passages.text: Example: A User orders goods online and registers pseudonym P at the e-commerce store. When picking up the goods, the User authenticates as P at the carrier. The carrier contacts the store using P as the correlation key.
passages.headingText: 16.8 Use Case D: Cross-RP Linkable Pseudonym

passages.text: Verifiable pseudonym reuse: The User registers the same WebAuthn credential at multiple RPs (possible if the Wallet Unit allows re-use of a pseudonym across RP domains — contrary to the default per-RP scoping)
passages.headingText: 16.8 Use Case D: Cross-RP Linkable Pseudonym

passages.text: Attested pseudonym: The e-commerce RP issues a (Q)EAA attesting P to the User's Wallet. The User presents this attestation to the carrier.
passages.headingText: 16.8 Use Case D: Cross-RP Linkable Pseudonym

passages.text: Privacy warning for RPs: Use Case D introduces deliberate linkability. RPs implementing this pattern should inform Users that cross-RP correlation is occurring and ensure it is limited to the stated purpose. Art. 5b(2) data minimisation obligations still apply.
passages.headingText: 16.8 Use Case D: Cross-RP Linkable Pseudonym

passages.text: The choice of WebAuthn attestation type during registration has significant privacy implications. RPs should understand the trade-offs:
No guarantees about authenticator
No guarantees (signed with credential key)
Authenticator identity verified
❌ Avoid (single attestation key shared across registrations)
Authenticator certified by CA
⚠️ Use only if authenticator assurance is critical
Per-credential certificate from CA
⚠️ Better than Basic, but CA can still track

RP guidance: Request attestation: "none" unless the RP has a specific regulatory need to verify authenticator properties. This aligns with the privacy-by-design principle of the EUDI Wallet ecosystem and avoids the surveillance risks identified in risk register threats TR39, TR84, and TR85.
RPs must maintain a mapping between pseudonym credentials and internal accounts. The recommended data model:
WebAuthn credential identifier (unique per registration)
EC P-256 public key from registration
RP domain that scoped this pseudonym
Last successful authentication
Authenticator signature counter (replay detection)
Whether age was verified during registration (Use Case B)
Any attributes presented alongside the pseudonym
Supported transports (e.g., ["internal", "hybrid"])
Current effective LoA: low (pseudonym-only), substantial (age/attribute verified), high (full PID verified). See §16.13.
Whether identity verification has been performed and bound to this pseudonym (§16.13)
When the step-up verification occurred
Method used: pid_presentation, pid_age_over_18, qeaa_presentation, email_otp
When the identity verification should be re-confirmed (RP-defined policy)
Whether this credential is a synced passkey (backed up, surviving device loss) or device-bound

Data minimisation: The linked_attributes field should store only the result of attribute verification (e.g., age_verified: true), not the raw PID attributes themselves. The RP has no ongoing need for the raw attributes once the verification is complete.

LoA semantics: The assurance_level field is an RP-side classification, not an eIDAS certification. The pseudonym itself has no eIDAS LoA (Topic E, Requirement 8). See §11.11 for LoA inference procedures and the distinction between PID LoA (inferred from trust chain) and pseudonym assurance semantics. See §16.13 for the full progressive assurance lifecycle.
Pseudonym lifecycle events from the RP perspective:
User removes the passkey from their Wallet Unit
RP account becomes inaccessible; User must re-register
RP invalidates the credential locally
User cannot authenticate; other Wallet functionality unaffected
PID Provider revokes PID; Wallet Provider revokes WUA
Pseudonym keys become unusable (Wallet Unit deactivated)
User loses device with Wallet Unit
Pseudonyms are lost unless backed up. PA_19: backup/restore of pseudonymous key material is supported
User moves to new device/Wallet Unit
Pseudonyms must be transferable (Scope rate-limited: Requirement 9 mandates persistence across Wallet Unit changes)

Key RP consideration: Unlike PID credentials (which can be re-issued by the PID Provider), pseudonym credentials are locally generated. If a User loses their device and did not back up, the pseudonym is permanently lost. RPs should implement account recovery flows that allow Users to re-bind a new pseudonym to their existing account using alternative verification methods (e.g., email, PID presentation as a one-time step-up).
The recoverability of a pseudonym after device loss depends on the passkey type:
Cloud keychain (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager)
Hardware keystore (Secure Enclave, StrongBox) only
✅ Yes — restored on new device from cloud backup
❌ No — permanently lost with device
✅ Available on all User's devices via sync
❌ Available only on the registering device
NIST SP 800-63-4 classification
AAL2 or AAL3 (depending on attestation)
BE=1 (Backup Eligible), BS=1 (Backup State) in authenticatorData
May not meet all Wallet Provider requirements if WSCA-level key protection is expected
Provides stronger hardware assurance but creates recovery burden

RP detection: RPs can determine whether a credential is synced or device-bound by inspecting the BE (Backup Eligible) and BS (Backup State) flags in the authenticatorData returned during registration (WebAuthn L3 §7.1). RPs should store backup_eligible in their pseudonym data model (§16.10) to inform recovery UI and policy decisions.

Wallet migration implication: For users with synced passkeys (BE=1), wallet migration is transparent — the passkey is restored from cloud backup (iCloud Keychain or Google Password Manager) and the RP experiences no authentication disruption. The Account Recovery Flow (§16.11.2) only applies when the pseudonym uses a device-bound passkey (BE=0). Cross-platform passkey migration (e.g., iPhone → Android) may additionally depend on FIDO CXP/CXF support, which is on the roadmap but not yet universally deployed. See §5.6.1 for the full migration decision tree.
When a User loses their device and the pseudonym was device-bound (not backed up), the RP must provide an account recovery path. The recommended flow:
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: 1. User visits RP on new device
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: The User arrives at the Relying Party service from a new device but lacks the device-bound passkey required for standard authentication. The RP detects the failure or absence of a credential and explicitly offers a "Recover account" option.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: 2. RP Server offers "Recover Account" option
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: The RP detects that the User's session lacks a valid device-bound passkey. Instead of rejecting the authentication attempt outright, the RP presents an explicit "Recover Account" option in the user interface, guiding the User through a re-verification flow using their EUDI Wallet.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs an ACCOUNT_RECOVERY_INITIATED event.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: 3. RP Server requests identity verification via DCQL
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: To recover the account safely, the RP launches an OpenID4VP DCQL query requesting the same PID attributes that were used for the original KYC step-up (if the account had a verified identity) or a broader set to ensure identity continuity.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a RECOVERY_DCQL_REQUEST_DISPATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Artifact Produced: OpenID4VP DCQL Authorization Request (JSON Payload)
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: 4. Wallet Unit presents PID attributes
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: The Wallet Unit securely presents the requested PID attributes. The RP evaluates the presentation and cryptographically matches the attributes against the existing account's verification record, assuring it is the same User.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Artifact Produced: SD-JWT VC Presentation
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: 5. RP Server validates presentation and matches existing account
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: The RP Server validates the cryptographic presentation (signature, nonce, audience) and then executes an account lookup against its user store, matching the received PID attributes (e.g., personal_identifier, family_name, birth_date) against the verification record from the original account creation. If a match is found, the RP confirms identity continuity.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Failure Path: If identity matching fails (e.g. mismatched personal_identifier), the RP MUST terminate the recovery process and log a RECOVERY_IDENTITY_MISMATCH security alert.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: On success, the RP Server logs a RECOVERY_IDENTITY_MATCHED event.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: 6. RP Server initiates new WebAuthn registration
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Having proven the User's identity, the RP initiates a fresh WebAuthn registration ceremony. The User creates a completely new device-bound passkey on their new apparatus which is securely bound to the existing account.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: 7. Wallet returns new bound passkey
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: The User's new device creates a fresh FIDO2 credential using the platform authenticator (Touch ID, Face ID, Android Biometric). The Wallet returns the new PublicKeyCredential containing the credentialId and public key, bound to the RP's rp.id domain.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: 8. RP Server invalidates old credential and stores new
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: The backend securely revokes the old credential_id preventing it from being misused by whoever found the lost device, while permanently committing the new credential for subsequent logins.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs an OLD_CREDENTIAL_REVOKED followed by a NEW_RECOVERY_CREDENTIAL_BOUND event.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Updated Account Record (New Passkey Bound)
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Privacy trade-off: Account recovery temporarily breaks pseudonymity — the RP sees the User's PID attributes during the recovery session. RPs should minimise the attributes requested (e.g., only age_over_18 if that was the original verification) and discard the raw attributes after matching. The intent_to_retain: false DCQL flag (§17.2.1) should be set for all recovery-related attribute requests.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Pseudonym fragmentation across Wallet Units. A user holding multiple Wallet Units (e.g., phone + tablet, or Wallet Provider X + Wallet Provider Y) will generate separate, unlinked passkeys at the same RP — because WebAuthn credentials are device-bound and each Wallet Unit has its own WSCA/WSCD. When a user authenticates from a second Wallet Unit, the RP sees an unrecognised passkey. The account recovery flow above (§16.11.2) handles this identically to device loss: the user presents PID with personal_identifier, the RP matches the existing account, and the new passkey is registered alongside (or replacing) the old one. RPs that support multi-device users may choose to store multiple passkeys per account rather than invalidating the old credential. Note that scope rate-limited pseudonyms (ARF HLR PA_31) are explicitly designed to be persistent across Wallet Unit changes and are not affected by this fragmentation — they are the only pseudonym type in the ARF with this cross-wallet persistence property.
passages.headingText: 16.11.2 Account Recovery Flow After Device Loss

passages.text: Pseudonym presented to wrong RP
WebAuthn scopes credentials to rp.id; browser verifies origin
Cross-RP linking via attestation
Use attestation: "none" to prevent credential correlation
Challenge-response protocol; signCount tracking
RP impersonation during registration
Browser verifies RP origin via TLS; Wallet trusts browser (PA_20)
TLS between browser and RP; WebAuthn binding to origin
Bypass of user authentication
Wallet requires biometric/PIN before signing assertion
Unjustified pseudonym revocation
RP can only revoke locally; no mechanism for RP to revoke Wallet Unit

This is the most strategically important passkey user journey for Relying Parties. It enables progressive onboarding — the User creates a pseudonymous account with zero identity verification (LoA Low), then upgrades the account via EUDI Wallet identity verification (LoA Substantial/High) only when needed, and subsequently authenticates with just a passkey while the RP treats the session at the elevated assurance level.
passages.headingText: 16.13 Progressive Assurance: Register Low, Verify Identity, Authenticate High

passages.text: ARF basis: Topic E, Appendix A, Question 1 affirms: "It should be possible to register attributes to the pseudonym later than at registration." This explicitly legitimises the late-binding pattern. The progressive assurance journey extends this into a multi-session lifecycle.
passages.headingText: 16.13 Progressive Assurance: Register Low, Verify Identity, Authenticate High

passages.text: LoA semantics: The pseudonym itself has no eIDAS Level of Assurance — the ARF's Topic E Requirement 8 states: "it does not make sense to talk about LoA High for pseudonyms as these does not constitute an electronic means of identification." However, the RP account to which the pseudonym is bound can have an effective assurance level based on the identity verification that was performed and bound to it. The RP must track this distinction in its data model (§15.13).
passages.headingText: 16.13 Progressive Assurance: Register Low, Verify Identity, Authenticate High

passages.text: 1. User visits marketplace and chooses "Sign up with Wallet"

The User navigates to an online marketplace that offers EUDI Wallet integration. The marketplace's registration page presents multiple sign-up options: email/password, social login, and "Sign up with EUDI Wallet". The Wallet option promises frictionless, pseudonymous account creation — no email required, no personal data collected. The marketplace is classified as a sector where legal identification is not required (§16.4), so pseudonym acceptance is mandatory under Art. 5b(9). The RP detects WebAuthn support via PublicKeyCredential.isUserVerifyingPlatformAuthenticatorAvailable() before offering the option.
2. RP Server sends WebAuthn registration options to Browser

The RP initiates a WebAuthn create() ceremony by generating PublicKeyCredentialCreationOptions. The options include the RP's identifier (rp.id), a unique user.id (opaque handle), and a fresh challenge_R (cryptographic random nonce). This follows the standard WebAuthn registration flow documented in §16.5.1, but with a key difference: the account will be created at LoA Low with no identity verification.
Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a LOW_ASSURANCE_REGISTRATION_INITIATED event.
Artifact Produced: PublicKeyCredentialCreationOptions (Client-Side JSON)
3. Browser forwards credential creation request to Wallet Unit

The browser receives the PublicKeyCredentialCreationOptions and forwards the request to the Wallet Unit via the platform's WebAuthn API. On platforms where the Wallet Unit is registered as a platform authenticator, this triggers a direct invocation. The request includes the RP's origin, which the Wallet Unit will verify before proceeding.
4. Wallet Unit prompts User for approval and biometric/PIN

The Wallet Unit displays a consent prompt: "Create a passkey for marketplace.example.com?". The User approves and authenticates via biometric (Face ID, fingerprint) or device PIN. This user verification step ensures that only the legitimate device owner can create credentials bound to this Wallet Unit.
5. Wallet Unit generates key pair scoped to RP's origin

The Wallet Unit generates a new asymmetric key pair (typically P-256 ECDSA). The private key is scoped to the RP's rp.id — it cannot be used to authenticate to any other RP, preventing cross-RP correlation. The key is stored in the Wallet Unit's secure enclave/TEE, wrapped with the device's hardware-backed keystore. No identity attributes are requested or collected.
6. Wallet Unit returns PublicKeyCredential to Browser

The Wallet Unit returns a PublicKeyCredential object containing the credentialId (unique identifier for this credential) and the publicKey (COSE-encoded). The private key never leaves the Wallet Unit. The credential also includes an attestationObject and clientDataJSON that the RP will verify to ensure the credential was genuinely created by this authenticator.
Artifact Produced: PublicKeyCredential (JSON payload)
7. Browser forwards credential to RP Server

The browser POSTs the PublicKeyCredential to the RP's WebAuthn registration endpoint. The payload is JSON-serialized from the browser's navigator.credentials.create() response. The RP's origin is verified by the browser before the credential is forwarded, preventing MITM attacks during the registration flow.
8. RP Server stores credential with LoA Low assurance level
The RP verifies the WebAuthn attestation and stores the credential with the following account metadata:

No identity attributes are requested or presented — the User's account is purely pseudonymous. The RP assigns an opaque user_handle and stores the credential's public key, but has zero information about the User's real identity.
Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a LOW_ASSURANCE_ACCOUNT_CREATED event.
Artifact Produced: LoA Low Account Record (identity_verified: false, stored in RP DB)
9. User returns to marketplace and initiates login (days later)

The User returns to the marketplace after some days. The marketplace's login page offers "Sign in with passkey" as an option. The User clicks this button, triggering the WebAuthn get() ceremony. This is a pseudonymous authentication — no email, no password, just the passkey created during Phase 1.
10. RP Server sends WebAuthn assertion request to Browser

The RP generates PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions containing the RP's rpId and a fresh challenge_A1 (cryptographic random nonce). The RP may include allowCredentials if it knows which credentials the User previously registered, or omit it for a "discoverable credential" (passkey) flow where the User selects from credentials stored on the device.
Artifact Produced: PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions (Client-Side JSON with challenge_A1)
11. Browser forwards assertion request to Wallet Unit

The browser receives the PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions and forwards the request to the Wallet Unit via the platform's WebAuthn API. The Wallet Unit identifies which stored credentials match the RP's rpId and prepares to prompt the User for authentication.
12. Wallet Unit prompts User for biometric/PIN

The Wallet Unit prompts the User to authenticate via biometric (Face ID, fingerprint) or device PIN. This user verification step proves the User's presence and consent to this authentication. The Wallet Unit ensures the User knows which RP they are authenticating to (marketplace.example.com).
13. Wallet Unit returns signed assertion to Browser

The Wallet Unit uses the stored private key to sign the challenge (challenge_A1), creating an assertion. The assertion includes the credentialId, authenticatorData (containing the RP's origin hash and flags), and signature. The private key never leaves the secure enclave; only the signed output is returned.
Artifact Produced: AuthenticatorAssertionResponse (ECDSA P-256 Signature)
14. Browser forwards assertion to RP Server

The browser POSTs the PublicKeyCredential assertion to the RP's WebAuthn authentication endpoint. The payload includes the signed challenge, allowing the RP to verify that the User controls the private key associated with the stored credential.
15. RP Server verifies assertion and grants LoA Low access

The RP verifies the signature using the stored public key, validates the authenticatorData (origin, signCount for clone detection), and checks the challenge_A1 matches the expected value. Upon successful verification, the RP grants access. Because the account has identity_verified: false, the RP treats this session at LoA Low — the User can browse, purchase low-value items, leave reviews, and perform any action that does not require identity verification. For the marketplace, this might mean limiting the User to buying (not selling), or capping transaction values.
Failure Path: If signature validation fails, the RP MUST terminate the login and log a WEBAUTHN_ASSERTION_FAILED alert.
Audit Telemetry: On success, the RP Server logs a LOW_ASSURANCE_SESSION_GRANTED event.
Artifact Produced: LoA Low Session Token (Cookie / Bearer Token tied to pseudonymous account)
16. User requests higher-privilege action (sell goods)

The User decides to sell goods on the marketplace, which requires identity verification. This might be age verification (to ensure the seller is over 18) or full KYC depending on the marketplace's policies and local regulations. The User clicks "Become a seller" or similar, triggering a backend check by the RP.
17. RP Server checks identity_verified flag and triggers step-up

The RP's backend checks the pseudonym account's identity_verified flag. Finding it false, the RP determines that a step-up identity verification is required before the User can access seller functionality. The RP does not reject the User — it initiates a seamless upgrade flow within the current session. The RP prepares an OpenID4VP authorization request to request the necessary identity attributes.
Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs a STEP_UP_VERIFICATION_TRIGGERED event.
18. RP Server sends OpenID4VP Authorization Request to Browser

The RP initiates an OpenID4VP authorization request with a DCQL query requesting age_over_18 from the User's PID. Critically, the RP sets the OpenID4VP nonce parameter to include the original WebAuthn challenge (or a derivative), creating a cross-ceremony binding (§16.7.3, Strategy 2: Challenge Embedding). The request URL might look like:
The JWT at request_uri contains the DCQL query:
Artifact Produced: OpenID4VP Authorization Request (DCQL Query Payload)
19. Browser forwards DCQL request to Wallet Unit

The browser receives the OpenID4VP authorization request and forwards it to the Wallet Unit. On mobile devices, this typically involves opening a deep link (eudi-wallet://) that launches the Wallet Unit app. The Wallet Unit receives the DCQL query and parses it to understand what credentials and claims the RP is requesting.
20. Wallet Unit prompts User for consent to share age_over_18

The Wallet Unit displays a consent prompt: "marketplace.example.com requests: Are you over 18?". This shows the RP's human-readable name, the specific attribute being requested (age_over_18), and the purpose (if provided by the RP). The Wallet Unit highlights that no other personal data (name, address, date of birth) will be shared.
21. User consents and authenticates via biometric

The User reviews the consent prompt and approves the disclosure. The Wallet Unit requires user verification (biometric or PIN) before releasing any credential data. This ensures that even if the device is unlocked, an attacker cannot silently extract the User's identity attributes.
Failure Path: If the User explicitly denies consent or authentication fails, the Wallet Unit aborts the presentation and logs a STEP_UP_CONSENT_DENIED event.
22. Wallet Unit returns vp_token with selectively disclosed SD-JWT VC

The Wallet Unit constructs a vp_token containing an SD-JWT VC with only age_over_18: true selectively disclosed. The SD-JWT uses selective disclosure to reveal just this single claim — no name, no date of birth, no address. The response is returned to the browser:
Artifact Produced: vp_token (SD-JWT VC containing requested claims)
23. Browser forwards vp_token to RP Server

The browser POSTs the vp_token to the RP's OpenID4VP response endpoint (via the response_uri or redirect URI specified in the authorization request). The RP receives the selectively disclosed SD-JWT VC containing only the age_over_18 claim, ready for verification.
24. RP Server verifies SD-JWT VC and cross-ceremony binding
The RP's backend performs the standard SD-JWT VC verification (§18.5, §14.3) and additionally:
Verifies the nonce matches the expected challenge material — confirming cross-ceremony binding between the WebAuthn session and this OpenID4VP presentation
Verifies session continuity — the OpenID4VP response arrived within the same TLS session as the active pseudonym login
Verifies temporal proximity — the attribute presentation happened within seconds of the step-up trigger, preventing replay attacks from older sessions
Failure Path: If any verification check fails, it indicates an invalid step-up attempt. The RP Server MUST log a STEP_UP_BINDING_FAILED security alert and terminate the request.
25. RP Server upgrades account to LoA Substantial
Having verified the attribute, the RP upgrades the pseudonym account:

Data minimisation: The RP stores age_verified: true, not the raw PID attributes. The SD-JWT VC's age_over_18 claim was selectively disclosed — the RP never learned the User's date of birth, only whether they are over 18.
Audit Telemetry: The RP Server logs an ACCOUNT_ASSURANCE_UPGRADED event, mapping the internal account assurance_level to substantial/high.
Artifact Produced: LoA Substantial Account Record (identity_verified: true, stored in RP DB)
26. User returns to marketplace (weeks later)

The User returns to the marketplace weeks after completing the step-up identity verification in Phase 3. The User initiates a login by clicking "Sign in with passkey" — the same frictionless authentication experience they had during Phase 2, but now the account is KYC-verified and the session will be treated at a higher assurance level.
27. RP Server sends WebAuthn assertion request to Browser

The RP generates PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions containing the RP's rpId and a fresh challenge_A2 (a new cryptographic random nonce, distinct from the challenge_A1 used in Phase 2). This is a standard WebAuthn assertion request, identical to Phase 2 — no special handling is required for the KYC-verified status at this step.
Artifact Produced: PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions (Client-Side JSON with challenge_A2)
28. Browser forwards assertion request to Wallet Unit

The browser receives the PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions and forwards the request to the Wallet Unit via the platform's WebAuthn API. The Wallet Unit identifies the stored credential for this RP (the same passkey created in Phase 1) and prepares to prompt the User for authentication.
29. Wallet Unit prompts User for biometric/PIN

The Wallet Unit prompts the User to authenticate via biometric (Face ID, fingerprint) or device PIN. This is the same user verification step as in Phase 2 — the Wallet Unit does not treat this differently because the underlying account is now KYC-verified. The verification is purely about proving possession of the device and consent to authenticate.
30. Wallet Unit returns signed assertion to Browser

The Wallet Unit uses the stored private key to sign the challenge (challenge_A2), creating an assertion. The assertion includes the credentialId, authenticatorData, and signature. This is cryptographically identical to the Phase 2 authentication — the passkey has not changed, only the RP's internal account metadata has been upgraded.
Artifact Produced: AuthenticatorAssertionResponse (ECDSA P-256 Signature)
31. Browser forwards assertion to RP Server

The browser POSTs the PublicKeyCredential assertion to the RP's WebAuthn authentication endpoint. The payload is identical to Phase 2 — a signed challenge proving possession of the private key associated with the stored credential.
32. RP Server verifies assertion and authenticates pseudonym

The RP verifies the signature using the stored public key, validates the authenticatorData, and checks the challenge_A2 matches the expected value. Upon successful verification, the RP identifies the User as the holder of this pseudonymous credential. The authentication itself is identical to Phase 2 — it's the subsequent lookup of account metadata that differs.
33. RP Server checks identity_verified flag and grants LoA Substantial access

After authenticating the pseudonym, the RP checks the account's identity_verified and assurance_level fields. Finding identity_verified: true and assurance_level: "substantial", the RP grants access to seller functionality — even though this session involves only a passkey login and no attribute presentation. The identity verification from Phase 3 "carries forward" through the pseudonym binding. The User can now sell goods, access higher-value transactions, or perform any action requiring LoA Substantial.

Re-verification policy: RPs should define a verification_expiry after which the identity verification must be refreshed. For age verification, this might be years (age doesn't reverse). For KYC-sensitive operations, the RP might re-verify annually or upon suspicious activity. If the verification_expiry has passed, the RP triggers a new step-up flow (returning to Phase 3).
Artifact Produced: LoA Substantial/High Session Token (Cookie / Bearer Token tied to KYC-verified account)
To support progressive assurance, the RP-side pseudonym storage model (§16.10) must include the following additional fields:
passages.headingText: 16.13.2 RP Data Model Changes for Progressive Assurance

passages.text: enum(low, substantial, high)
passages.headingText: 16.13.2 RP Data Model Changes for Progressive Assurance

passages.text: Current effective LoA for this pseudonymous account. Starts at low; upgraded after identity verification.
passages.headingText: 16.13.2 RP Data Model Changes for Progressive Assurance

passages.text: Whether identity verification has been performed and bound to this pseudonym
passages.headingText: 16.13.2 RP Data Model Changes for Progressive Assurance

passages.text: When the step-up verification occurred
passages.headingText: 16.13.2 RP Data Model Changes for Progressive Assurance

passages.text: Method used: pid_presentation, pid_age_over_18, qeaa_presentation, email_otp
passages.headingText: 16.13.2 RP Data Model Changes for Progressive Assurance

passages.text: When the identity verification should be re-confirmed (RP-defined policy)
passages.headingText: 16.13.2 RP Data Model Changes for Progressive Assurance

passages.text: LoA semantics: The pseudonym itself has no eIDAS LoA (Topic E, Requirement 8). The assurance_level field represents the RP's effective trust in the account holder, based on what identity verification was performed. This is an RP-side classification, not an eIDAS certification.
passages.headingText: 16.13.2 RP Data Model Changes for Progressive Assurance

passages.text: PID revoked after KYC binding
RP should flag the account for re-verification at next login. The pseudonym itself remains valid, but the identity assurance may be stale.
RP denies access to the higher-privilege feature but does not revoke the pseudonym. The User retains LoA Low access.
RP can support incremental upgrades: low → substantial (age verification) → high (full PID + address presentation).
RP triggers re-verification. The User presents the same (or updated) attribute. The RP updates identity_verified_at and extends the expiry.

User's device-bound passkey is lost after wallet migration. RP triggers Account Recovery Flow (§16.11.2): request PID, match personal_identifier, register new passkey. After successful PID step-up, restore assurance to at least Substantial. If stored KYC/QEAA verification results are indexed by personal_identifier (not by device key), carry them forward to restore High assurance without re-presentation. For synced passkeys (BE=1), migration is transparent — no recovery needed (§16.11.1). See §5.6.1 for the full decision tree.

The sequence diagrams in §16.6 and §16.13 assume a same-device flow where the browser and the Wallet Unit run on the same device (authenticatorAttachment: "platform"). However, the ARF explicitly requires both same-device and cross-device flows for pseudonyms (Topic E, §5: "The use cases all exist in both cross-device and same device flows"; Appendix A, Question 2: "Both cross-device and same-device flows should support pseudonyms").
In the cross-device scenario, the User browses a website on their laptop (desktop browser) while the EUDI Wallet runs on their phone. The WebAuthn ceremonies use CTAP 2.2 hybrid transport:
CTAP 2.2 hybrid (caBLE): QR code → BLE tunnel → FIDO exchange
User scans QR code on laptop with phone → authenticates on phone
Moderate (two devices, linked by BLE proximity)
Session cookie on same browser
Session cookie on laptop browser; Wallet response tunnelled from phone

RP implementation: To support cross-device flows, RPs should not restrict authenticatorAttachment to "platform". Omitting this parameter or setting it to "cross-platform" allows both flows. The Wallet Unit on the phone acts as a roaming authenticator via CTAP 2.2 — the same protocol used by hardware security keys (YubiKey, etc.), but tunnelled over BLE instead of USB/NFC.

The Digital Credentials Query Language (DCQL) is a JSON-based query language integrated into OpenID4VP 1.0 and mandated by HAIP 1.0. Crucially for implementations, the EUDI Wallet Architecture and Reference Framework (ARF) completely deprecates the legacy presentation_definition format from DIF Presentation Exchange (PEX) in favor of DCQL. RPs migrating from other OpenID4VC ecosystems must discard their PEX queries and rewrite them into DCQL's more expressive, format-agnostic mechanism.
Prior to DCQL, OpenID4VP used DIF Presentation Exchange (PE) v2.0 as its query language. PE was the sole query mechanism through OID4VP Draft 21. The transition timeline:
OID4VP Drafts 1–21 use PE (presentation_definition/presentation_submission) as the only query format
OID4VP Draft 22 introduces DCQL as an alternative alongside PE
PE is fully removed from OID4VP — DCQL becomes the sole query language
OID4VP 1.0 Final published — DCQL only, no PE support
HAIP 1.0 Final published — mandates DCQL; DCQL simultaneously progresses as draft-ietf-oauth-dcql at IETF
RPs migrating from PE-based implementations can use the following concept mapping:
DIF Presentation Exchange (PE)
Each entry describes one credential to request
Identifier used to match request ↔ response
JSONPath → JSON array path (simpler syntax)
JSON Schema filter → flat allowed-values array
DCQL's credential_sets supports required: false for optional credentials
presentation_submission.descriptor_map
vp_token keys = credentials[].id
No separate submission object needed — response is keyed directly by credential ID
Key differences beyond syntax:

Response simplification — PE required a presentation_submission object with descriptor_map entries mapping each credential to its position in the vp_token array. DCQL eliminates this: the vp_token is a JSON object keyed by the credentials[].id values from the query. This is both simpler to implement and less error-prone.
Format-agnostic querying — DCQL's claims[].path syntax works identically for SD-JWT VC and mdoc, whereas PE required format-specific path expressions.
No PE library dependency — PE required the presentation-exchange npm package or equivalent. DCQL queries are plain JSON — no parsing library needed.

RP guidance: If your implementation currently uses presentation_definition, you must rewrite all queries to dcql_query format before connecting to any HAIP-conformant Wallet. No EUDI-ecosystem wallet accepts PE. Non-EUDI OID4VP deployments (e.g., legacy EBSI, some US mDL ecosystems) may still use PE, but this is outside the scope of EUDI RP integration.
Multi-credential requests: Request attributes from multiple credentials in one query
Format specification: Specify preferred credential format per query
Claim-level precision: Request specific claims, including nested claims
Claim value filtering: The values array constrains acceptable claim values — the Wallet only matches credentials where the claim value matches one of the listed values

Alternative credentials: credential_sets with options allow the Wallet to choose between alternative credential combinations (each inner array is an AND set; multiple inner arrays are OR alternatives)
Purpose statements: Human-readable purpose for each credential set — displayed to the User by the Wallet Unit as per ARF §7.6.3.5.5
Retention intent (mdoc only): The intent_to_retain boolean signals to the Wallet User whether the RP intends to store the attribute beyond the immediate transaction — see §17.2.1

For mdoc (mso_mdoc) DCQL queries, each claim object supports an intent_to_retain boolean attribute. This maps directly to ISO 18013-5's IntentToRetain field in ItemsRequest (§9.3.2.1.2.1), which the Wallet Unit displays to the User before consent.
RP will not store this attribute beyond the active session
Wallet may indicate "data not retained"
Supports Art. 5(1)(c) data minimisation
RP intends to store this attribute (e.g., for regulatory retention)
Wallet should warn the User about data retention
Must be justified under a lawful basis (Art. 6)
No retention signal provided
Wallet implementation-dependent
Conservative RPs should explicitly set false

RP best practice: Set intent_to_retain: false for all claims unless there is a specific, documented lawful basis for retention (e.g., AML record-keeping per §22, DORA incident logging per §31). This aligns with the GDPR data minimisation obligations described in §21.3 and with the ARF's emphasis on purpose limitation (ARF §7.6.3.5.5).

Note: intent_to_retain applies only to mso_mdoc format queries. For dc+sd-jwt format, there is no equivalent claim-level retention signal in the DCQL specification — data minimisation for SD-JWT VC is enforced through selective disclosure (requesting only necessary claims) rather than retention signalling.
The credential_sets mechanism enables the RP to express flexible requirements — for example, "present either a PID OR a national ID card, AND in either case also an address attestation":

The Wallet Unit evaluates each options entry as an AND set. If the User's Wallet contains a PID and an address attestation, it satisfies the first option. If the Wallet contains a national ID card and an address attestation but no PID, it satisfies the second option. The Wallet selects the first matching option, or — if multiple match — lets the User choose.

RP implementation note: When using credential_sets with alternatives, the RP must be prepared to receive any of the alternative credential combinations. In DCQL-native mode (HAIP 1.0), the vp_token in the response is a JSON object keyed by credential id — the RP matches each entry against the original dcql_query to determine which option was satisfied. In legacy presentation_submission mode, the descriptor_map identifies which credentials were presented.

DCQL supports constraining acceptable claim values via the values array. This enables the RP to request a credential only if a specific claim has an acceptable value — for example, requesting age verification only from EU Member States:
The Wallet Unit matches the credential only if the nationality claim contains one of the listed values. If no match is found, the credential is not presented and the Wallet informs the User.
A bank performing customer onboarding might request both PID attributes and an address attestation:
passages.headingText: 17.5.1 Example: Bank KYC and Address Verification

passages.text: When an RP interacts with a legal person — e.g., for B2B onboarding, procurement, or contract signing — it uses LPID-specific DCQL queries. The LPID attribute model is defined in §3.3; the credential format in §6.15.
For use cases requiring only company identity (e.g., VAT verification, procurement eligibility check):
For use cases where the RP must identify both the company and the person interacting on its behalf:
passages.headingText: LPID and Natural Person PID: Representative Identification

passages.text: For high-assurance B2B use cases (financial onboarding, contract signing authority) where the RP must verify the company identity, the representative's personal identity, and their authority to act on behalf of the company:
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: Mandate VCT note: The VCT value eu.europa.ec.eudi.mandate.1 is projected — no finalised mandate credential schema exists in the ARF or EWC specifications as of March 2026. ARF Topic 29 (RP_01, RP_02) defines high-level requirements for representation attestations, but only for natural-person-to-natural-person representation. The natural-person-to-legal-person mandate specification is expected to follow the EBW regulation timeline (§3.6). See §18 for the full mandate credential taxonomy, attribute model, and verification flow.
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: Joint Representation (Gesamtvertretung)
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: Some corporate mandates require joint exercise — two or more representatives must act together for the mandate to be valid. This pattern, known as Gesamtvertretung in German corporate law, has no equivalent in natural-person-to-natural-person representation. The mandate credential signals this via two attributes:
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: joint_representation (boolean) — true if this mandate requires joint exercise with another representative
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: joint_partner_ids (array) — identifiers of the other required representatives
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: When the RP receives a mandate with joint_representation: true, it must collect valid mandates from ALL listed joint partners before authorising the operation. This may require:
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: Multi-session orchestration — the RP initiates separate OpenID4VP sessions with each joint representative's Wallet, then binds the results into a single authorisation decision
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: Temporal binding — all joint mandates must be valid at the same point in time (not sequentially revoked-and-reissued)
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: Scope intersection — the effective scope is the intersection of all joint mandates' scope_of_authority
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: No specification exists for multi-Wallet joint representation. ARF and OID4VP do not address multi-user sessions. RPs must implement this as application-level orchestration until a standard emerges.
passages.headingText: LPID and PID and Mandate: Triple-Credential Corporate Onboarding

passages.text: For use cases where the RP already knows the company (e.g., an existing customer portal) and only needs to verify the representative's authority:

Assurance trade-off: A mandate-only presentation lacks the PID and LPID binding checks (§18.5.3). The RP can verify the mandate's represented_entity_id against its own records (e.g., stored EUID from prior LPID presentation), but cannot cryptographically verify the representative's identity in this session. Accept only for lower-assurance operations or when the representative was previously identified.
When an RP receives a combined presentation involving LPID, PID, and potentially a mandate credential, it must perform three-way binding verification:
passages.headingText: 18.5.3 Identity Matching in LPID Combined Presentations

passages.text: Cryptographic binding (presentation-based): All credentials share the same cnf.jwk — proving they come from the same Wallet Unit.
passages.headingText: 18.5.3 Identity Matching in LPID Combined Presentations

passages.text: Representative ↔ PID binding (attribute-based): mandate.representative_id == pid.personal_identifier — proving the natural person presenting is the authorised representative.
passages.headingText: 18.5.3 Identity Matching in LPID Combined Presentations

passages.text: Mandate ↔ LPID binding (attribute-based): mandate.represented_entity_id == lpid.legal_person_id — proving the mandate applies to the presented legal person.
passages.headingText: 18.5.3 Identity Matching in LPID Combined Presentations

passages.text: Key difference from natural-person combined presentations: Natural person combined presentations (§18.5.4) only need to verify same-User binding — one entity, multiple credentials. LPID combined presentations require cross-entity binding — proving that Person A is authorised to act for Company B. This is structurally more complex, requiring attribute-level cross-matching in addition to the standard cryptographic or presentation-based binding described in §18.5.4.
passages.headingText: 18.5.3 Identity Matching in LPID Combined Presentations

passages.text: The three-way binding check is performed after individual credential verification (§11.3 for SD-JWT VC) and before the RP's business logic processes the verified claims. If any binding check fails, the entire combined presentation must be rejected.
passages.headingText: 18.5.3 Identity Matching in LPID Combined Presentations

passages.text: When the RP receives a combined presentation (multiple attestations in one response), it must verify that all attestations belong to the same User. The ARF (§7.6.3.10) defines three binding methods, in ascending order of assurance:
RP assumes attributes in a single presentation response belong to the same User — trusting the Wallet Unit is not compromised
✅ None — no extra attributes needed
Attestations share a common identifier (e.g., PID number, name + DOB) used as a cross-reference
❌ Requires presenting identifying attributes even when not needed for the use case
WSCA/WSCD generates a proof that it manages the private keys of all involved attestations
✅ Minimal — no extra attributes needed; only a cryptographic proof

Current state (ARF v2.8.0): The ARF does not yet specify a particular cryptographic mechanism for cryptographic binding. The HLRs (ACP_10–ACP_15) define requirements for such a scheme, including that it SHALL use Commission-recognised algorithms and MAY use Zero-Knowledge Proofs. Until a concrete scheme is standardised, RPs should rely on presentation-based binding for low-risk use cases and attribute-based binding for high-risk use cases.
The ARF Annex 2, Topic 18 defines the following requirements for combined presentations that are directly relevant to RPs:
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: Wallet Units SHALL support multi-attestation requests/responses per [OpenID4VP] and [ISO 18013-5]
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: RP can rely on Wallet support for DCQL multi-credential queries
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: RPs SHOULD support multi-attestation features per [OpenID4VP] and [ISO 18013-5]
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: RP should implement combined query support
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: If RP receives a cryptographic binding proof, it SHOULD verify it
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: RP should implement proof verification when available
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: RP SHOULD NOT refuse attestations solely because a cryptographic binding proof is absent
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: RP must accept combined presentations without cryptographic proof
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: Cryptographic binding scheme SHALL use Commission-recognised algorithms
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: Future-proofing for RP verification pipeline
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: Scheme SHALL enable proof that multiple keys are managed by the same WSCD
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: Defines the security guarantee RP can trust
passages.headingText: 18.5.5 ARF High-Level Requirements for Combined Presentations

passages.text: Strictest policy prevails: When attestations in a combined presentation carry different embedded disclosure policies, the most restrictive policy applies to the entire presentation. If one attestation's policy denies disclosure to the RP, the User is warned — but can override (§20.3).

Strictest policy prevails: When attestations in a combined presentation carry different embedded disclosure policies, the most restrictive policy applies to the entire presentation. If one attestation's policy denies disclosure to the RP, the User is warned — but can override (§20.3).

Validity period: The combined presentation's validity is determined by the minimum validity of all included attestations. If one attestation expires in 5 minutes and another in 24 hours, the RP should treat the combined presentation as valid for 5 minutes.

Validity period: The combined presentation's validity is determined by the minimum validity of all included attestations. If one attestation expires in 5 minutes and another in 24 hours, the RP should treat the combined presentation as valid for 5 minutes.

Privacy risk of combination: Individual attributes may not be personally identifying on their own, but their combination across attestations may create a unique tracking vector. RPs must be mindful of this when designing DCQL queries — request only what is necessary.

Privacy risk of combination: Individual attributes may not be personally identifying on their own, but their combination across attestations may create a unique tracking vector. RPs must be mindful of this when designing DCQL queries — request only what is necessary.

Single intended use per request: Per ARF §7.6.3.5.5, each presentation request can have only one intended use. If the RP has multiple purposes for requesting attributes, it must send multiple presentation requests, each triggering a separate consent screen.

Single intended use per request: Per ARF §7.6.3.5.5, each presentation request can have only one intended use. If the RP has multiple purposes for requesting attributes, it must send multiple presentation requests, each triggering a separate consent screen.

Sequential presentation UX guidance: When an RP needs multiple presentation requests within a single user session (e.g., identity verification followed by address proof), the following implementation patterns are recommended: ConcernRecommendationPre-warningBefore the first request, inform the user: "This service requires 2 approvals in your Wallet" — reducing abandonment from unexpected second promptsInter-request delayWait at least 1 second between sequential requests to avoid overwhelming the Wallet's consent UI. Some Wallet implementations may queue overlapping requests unpredictablyProgressive disclosurePresent results from the first request before triggering the second — e.g., "Welcome, Anna. We now need to verify your address"Partial failureIf the user approves the first request but declines the second, the RP must decide: (a) proceed with partial data, or (b) discard all data received. Design for both outcomesSession bindingEach request uses a fresh nonce and ephemeral key, but the state parameter should encode sequential ordering (e.g., state: "onboarding-step-2-of-3") for RP-side correlationTimeout between stepsAllow generous timeouts (60–120s) between sequential steps — the user may need time to re-authenticate to their Wallet for each approval

Sequential presentation UX guidance: When an RP needs multiple presentation requests within a single user session (e.g., identity verification followed by address proof), the following implementation patterns are recommended:
Before the first request, inform the user: "This service requires 2 approvals in your Wallet" — reducing abandonment from unexpected second prompts
Wait at least 1 second between sequential requests to avoid overwhelming the Wallet's consent UI. Some Wallet implementations may queue overlapping requests unpredictably
Present results from the first request before triggering the second — e.g., "Welcome, Anna. We now need to verify your address"
If the user approves the first request but declines the second, the RP must decide: (a) proceed with partial data, or (b) discard all data received. Design for both outcomes
Each request uses a fresh nonce and ephemeral key, but the state parameter should encode sequential ordering (e.g., state: "onboarding-step-2-of-3") for RP-side correlation
Allow generous timeouts (60–120s) between sequential steps — the user may need time to re-authenticate to their Wallet for each approval
1. RP Instance decrypts JWE and extracts vp_token
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP receives the encrypted response payload (direct_post.jwt) — a JWE encrypted with the RP's ephemeral public key (from the jwks parameter in the original JAR). The RP decrypts using ECDH-ES key agreement with A256GCM content encryption (HAIP 1.0 §6.4). After decryption, the RP parses the resulting JSON and extracts the vp_token field, the presentation_submission (which maps each credential to the DCQL query it satisfies), and the state parameter (to correlate with the original session). See §9.2 step 19 for the full JWE decryption flow with payload example.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If decryption fails: The RP returns an error and logs the failure. Common causes: ephemeral key mismatch (wrong session), JWE expired, or corrupted ciphertext. The RP should NOT attempt to re-request — the Wallet has already submitted and considers the transaction complete.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: On failure, the RP Instance MUST log a PRESENTATION_DECRYPTION_FAILED SIEM alert.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 2. RP Instance parses multiple attestations from vp_token
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)
