passages.text: The RP examines the vp_token structure. When the DCQL query requested multiple credentials (§18.5.5), the vp_token is a JSON object (not an array) where each key corresponds to a DCQL credential query ID:
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP uses the presentation_submission (or DCQL response mapping) to determine which vp_token entry satisfies which part of the original query. Each entry is an independent credential that must be verified individually (steps 3–8) before cross-credential binding is checked (steps 9–10). The RP must handle both SD-JWT VC format (dc+sd-jwt) and mdoc format (mso_mdoc) within the same response — see §18.5.8 for cross-format matching.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: The decoupled vp_token structure mapping DCQL queries to disparate credential formats.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 3. RP Instance verifies issuer signature against LoTE trust anchor
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: For each credential in the vp_token, the RP verifies the issuer's cryptographic signature. The verification method depends on the credential format:
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: SD-JWT VC: The RP parses the issuer-signed JWT header to extract the x5c certificate chain (or kid referencing a key in the issuer's JWKS), then verifies the JWT signature (ES256 / P-256 ECDSA per HAIP 1.0). The trust anchor is the issuer's root CA certificate obtained from the LoTE (§5.5.3).
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: mdoc: The RP verifies the IssuerAuth COSE_Sign1 signature in the MSO (Mobile Security Object) against the issuer's certificate from the LoTE.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: In both cases, the RP must build a certificate chain from the credential's leaf certificate up to the LoTE-provided trust anchor and verify every link. See §12 for the full cryptographic verification procedure.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If signature verification fails: The RP MUST reject the entire combined presentation — a single invalid credential invalidates the set, since the cross-credential binding (step 9) cannot be trusted if one credential is forged.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: On failure, the RP Instance MUST log a CREDENTIAL_SIGNATURE_INVALID SIEM alert.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 4. RP Instance validates standard claims (exp, iat, vct)
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP checks the temporal and type validity of each credential:
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: exp (Expiration): The credential must not have expired at the time of verification. The RP should apply a small clock skew tolerance (typically ≤ 60 seconds) to account for network latency.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: iat (Issued At): The credential should have been issued before the current time. Credentials with iat in the future may indicate clock manipulation.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: nbf (Not Before): If present, the credential is not yet valid before this timestamp.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: vct (Verifiable Credential Type) for SD-JWT VC / docType for mdoc: The credential type must match one of the types requested in the DCQL query. For PID, this is eu.europa.ec.eudi.pid.1; for mDL, org.iso.18013.5.1.mDL.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Cross-reference: HAIP 1.0 §6.3 defines the mandatory claims for SD-JWT VC. ISO 18013-5 §10.1.2.4 defines the MSO validity info structure for mdoc.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If the credential has expired, was issued in the future, or does not match the requested vct/docType, the RP MUST reject the presentation.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Instance logs a CREDENTIAL_VALIDITY_CHECK_FAILED event upon encountering expired or malformed metadata.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 5. RP Instance verifies selective disclosure hashes
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: For SD-JWT VC credentials, the RP verifies that each selectively disclosed attribute is authentic by recomputing its hash and matching it against the _sd array in the issuer-signed JWT (SD-JWT §6.3):
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: For each ~-delimited Disclosure in the SD-JWT presentation, decode the base64url string to obtain the JSON array [salt, claim_name, claim_value]
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Compute SHA-256(base64url(Disclosure)) to obtain the disclosure hash
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Verify that this hash appears in the _sd array of the issuer-signed JWT payload (at the appropriate nesting level for nested disclosures)
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If any disclosure hash is not found in _sd, reject the credential — it contains a tampered or fabricated attribute
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: For mdoc credentials, the equivalent check verifies data element digests: the RP computes SHA-256 of each disclosed DataElementValue and matches it against the corresponding entry in the MSO's ValueDigests map (ISO 18013-5 §10.1.2.4).
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If any data element digest or SD hash fails verification, it implies structural tampering.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Instance MUST trigger a SELECTIVE_DISCLOSURE_TAMPERING_DETECTED severe alert.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 6. RP Instance verifies device binding (KB-JWT or DeviceAuth)
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP verifies that the credential was presented by the device to which it was originally issued — preventing credential forwarding and replay attacks (§29.2, Cross-RP Collusion):
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: SD-JWT VC (KB-JWT): The RP verifies the Key Binding JWT signature using the cnf.jwk public key embedded in the issuer-signed JWT. It also verifies that the KB-JWT's aud matches the RP's own client_id (typically the x509_hash of the WRPAC), the nonce matches the request nonce, and the sd_hash matches the hash of the SD-JWT presentation (SD-JWT §8.3).
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: mdoc (DeviceAuth): The RP verifies the DeviceSigned.DeviceAuth COSE_Mac0 or COSE_Sign1 signature over the SessionTranscript using the deviceKey from the MSO (ISO 18013-5 §10.1.3.6). The SessionTranscript includes the session-specific handshake data, binding the response to this specific transaction.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If device binding fails: This is a critical security failure — the credential may have been stolen, forwarded, or replayed. The RP MUST reject the presentation and SHOULD log an alert for fraud investigation.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Instance MUST trigger a DEVICE_BINDING_VALIDATION_FAILED SIEM alert.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 7. RP Instance queries Status List for revocation check
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP queries the issuer's Token Status List endpoint for each credential to verify it has not been revoked or suspended since issuance. The RP extracts the status claim from the credential (containing the status_list.uri and status_list.idx), fetches the Status List Token from the URI, and checks the bit at the specified index. See Appendix B (§B.3.1) for the full Status List verification procedure with payload examples.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Batch optimisation: When verifying multiple credentials from the same issuer, the Status List Token may be shared — the RP should cache it after the first fetch to avoid redundant HTTP requests. The cache TTL should respect the exp claim in the Status List Token JWT.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 8. Status List returns credential validity status to RP Instance
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The Status List check returns one of two outcomes per credential: VALID (bit at index = 0) or REVOKED/SUSPENDED (bit at index = 1). Steps 3–8 are executed for each credential in the vp_token — either sequentially or in parallel. If any credential is revoked, the RP must decide whether to reject the entire combined presentation or proceed with the remaining valid credentials, depending on the RP's policy configuration (§26.1).
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Partial revocation policy: For combined presentations where one credential is revoked but others are valid (e.g., mDL revoked but PID valid), the RP's policy engine should define whether partial results are acceptable for the use case. For CDD (§22), all credentials must be valid; for age verification, a valid PID alone may suffice.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Failure Path: If a required credential is functionally suspended or revoked, the RP MUST abandon the dependent business logic.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Instance logs a CREDENTIAL_REVOKED_STATUS_DETECTED event.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 9. RP Instance performs identity matching across all attestations
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: This is the critical cross-credential binding check that distinguishes a combined presentation from two unrelated presentations. The RP must prove that all credentials belong to the same User and were presented from the same Wallet Unit:
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Same-format binding (SD-JWT VC + SD-JWT VC): Verify that all credentials contain the same cnf.jwk public key. Since the KB-JWT for each credential is signed with this key, matching cnf.jwk values proves that the same device key — and therefore the same Wallet Unit — holds all credentials.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Same-format binding (mdoc + mdoc): Verify that all DeviceResponse documents reference the same deviceKey in their MSO. The DeviceAuth signature over the shared SessionTranscript proves possession.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Cross-format binding (SD-JWT VC + mdoc): This is the most complex case — the SD-JWT's cnf.jwk and the mdoc's MSO deviceKey are structurally different keys (JWK vs COSE_Key). The RP must determine whether they represent the same underlying key (see §18.5.8 for the cross-format matching algorithm).
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If identity matching fails (different cnf.jwk values or different deviceKey values): The credentials may originate from different Wallet Units or even different Users. The RP MUST reject the combined presentation — accepting mismatched credentials would allow a "credential cocktail" attack where attributes from different identities are combined.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Instance MUST trigger a CROSS_CREDENTIAL_BINDING_FAILED SIEM alert, an indicator of potential synthetic identity abuse.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 10. RP Instance verifies WSCA proof of single WSCD management
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: If the Wallet provided a Wallet Secure Cryptographic Application (WSCA) binding proof — a cryptographic assertion that all device keys across different credential formats are managed by the same WSCD instance — the RP verifies this proof. This is an additional layer beyond the cnf.jwk/deviceKey matching in step 9: it proves at the hardware level that one physical secure element manages all keys, even if the keys are technically distinct (as in cross-format presentations).
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Current status: The WSCA binding proof mechanism is specified in ARF v2.8 §7.6.3 but the concrete cryptographic protocol is still being standardised. As of March 2026, no Wallet Unit implementation produces this proof in production. RPs should implement step 9's key-matching as the primary binding check and treat step 10 as a future-proofing enhancement. When the WSCA proof standard is finalised, it will likely use a COSE_Sign1 structure binding all device keys under a single WSCA attestation certificate.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 11. RP Instance calculates minimum validity across all credentials
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The RP calculates the overall combined presentation validity window by taking the minimum exp value across all presented credentials. For example, if the PID expires at 2025-12-31T23:59:59Z and the mDL expires at 2025-06-15T00:00:00Z, the combined presentation is valid only until 2025-06-15. This minimum validity determines how long the RP can rely on the combined presentation for session-based decisions (e.g., how long an authenticated session should last before requiring re-verification).
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Session binding: The RP should set the application session's max-age to no longer than this minimum validity, minus a safety margin. For time-sensitive transactions (SCA — §15), the validity window may be further constrained by the transaction_data expiry.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: The RP Instance logs a COMBINED_PRESENTATION_EXPIRY_BOUND event, recording the calculated minimum validity window.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: 12. RP Instance extracts verified attributes into application logic
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Having cryptographically verified each individual credential (steps 3–8) and confirmed they belong to the same User and Wallet Unit (steps 9–10), the RP safely merges the disclosed attributes from all credentials into a unified attribute set for application consumption. For example, a combined PID + mDL presentation yields:
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: The _meta object is an RP-internal construct (not part of the protocol) that records the verification provenance — useful for audit trails (§31.3) and downstream policy decisions. The RP should feed this unified attribute set into its business rules engine (§26.1) for the final application-level decision (CDD, age gate, SCA, etc.).
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Unified verified attribute dataset (JSON) fed to the business rules engine.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: On complete holistic verification success, the RP Instance logs a COMBINED_PRESENTATION_VERIFIED event, explicitly associating the grouped credential IDs.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Key verification step: Step 9 is the critical identity matching check. For SD-JWT VC, the RP verifies that all attestations contain the same cnf.jwk public key. For mdoc, the RP verifies that all DeviceResponse documents reference the same deviceKey in their MSO. If the keys differ, the attestations may originate from different Wallet Units — the RP should reject or flag the combined presentation.
passages.headingText: 18.5.7 Combined Presentation Verification Flow (Agnostic: Applies to Direct RP and Intermediary)

passages.text: Open Question #9 identifies the challenge of combined presentations that mix SD-JWT VC and mdoc credentials in a single response. When this occurs, the RP must bridge two different device binding mechanisms:
cnf.jwk (EC P-256 public key)
In the Issuer-signed JWT payload
deviceKey (COSE_Key, EC P-256)
For same-user verification in a cross-format combined presentation, the RP should:
Extract both keys: cnf.jwk from the SD-JWT VC and deviceKey from the mdoc MSO

Compare the raw public key material: Convert both to a canonical form (e.g., uncompressed EC point) and compare. If the same WSCA/WSCD manages both credentials, the keys may be identical — but this is not guaranteed, as the Wallet Unit may use different device keys for different credential formats.
Fall back to attribute-based matching: If the keys differ, use shared attributes (e.g., personal_identifier, family_name + birth_date) to establish same-user binding.
Await cryptographic binding: When the ARF's cryptographic binding mechanism (ACP_10–ACP_15) is standardised, it will provide a format-agnostic proof that both keys are managed by the same WSCD.

Current limitation: There is no guarantee that a Wallet Unit will use the same device key for SD-JWT VC and mdoc credentials. Implementers requesting mixed-format combined presentations should use attribute-based binding as the primary identity matching method until cryptographic binding is available.
The EUDI ecosystem defines two fundamentally different representation paradigms that share the label "mandate" but differ in legal basis, issuance authority, scope semantics, and revocation model:
Paradigm A — Natural-to-Natural
Paradigm B — Natural-to-Legal-Person
COM(2025) 838 (EBW Regulation), Directive (EU) 2025/25 (28th Regime)
Discussion Paper Topic I (v0.4, May 2025); Topic 29 HLRs (RP_01, RP_02)
Not yet covered — deferred to EBW regulation timeline
Parent for minor, guardian for incapacitated adult, power of attorney for natural person
Company director, employee with signing authority, procurement officer acting for legal entity
Attestation Provider retrieving from Authentic Sources (civil registries, court records)
LPID Provider (business register via BRIS), notary, court, or the legal entity itself via EBW
represented_entity_id → links to a natural person PID personal_identifier
represented_entity_id → links to an LPID legal_person_id (EUID)
Operations the representative may perform (e.g., "medical consent", "financial up to €10,000") — defined per Rulebook
Corporate authority scope (e.g., "contract signing", "procurement", "financial transactions") — linked to company register roles
eu.europa.ec.eudi.representation.1 (per Topic 29 RP_01 Rulebook)
eu.europa.ec.eudi.mandate.1 (projected — see §18.5.2)
Multi-party: represented person, court, guardian authority
Multi-party: legal entity (board resolution), court, national register authority
Governed by national family law — Hague Convention 2000 for adults, Brussels IIb for minors
Governed by national company law — EBW regulation aims to harmonise via digital EU power of attorney

Key gap: Topic I explicitly states it covers only natural-to-natural representation — "not the case of a natural person representing a legal person" (§1.1). Paradigm B has no equivalent Discussion Paper and awaits the EBW regulation.
Based on Topic I requirements (§3.1), RP_01 Rulebook requirements, Annex VI §10, and EBW regulation provisions, a mandate credential MUST contain at minimum:
Common Attributes (Both Paradigms)
Nature of the representation — e.g., "parental_authority", "legal_guardianship", "power_of_attorney", "corporate_mandate"
Unique identifier of the representative — matches personal_identifier in the representative's PID
Name of the representative — should match PID family_name + given_name
"natural_person" (Paradigm A) or "legal_person" (Paradigm B)
Identifier of the represented entity — personal_identifier (A) or legal_person_id/EUID (B)
Name of the represented entity
Structured definition of authorised operations (§11.12.3)
{ "not_before": datetime, "not_after": datetime }
Name/identifier of the entity that issued the mandate credential
