passages.text: The Wallet sends the VP Token to the RP's API gateway endpoint via the direct_post response mode (or direct_post.jwt for encrypted responses). This is the entry point for all credential presentations — the gateway receives the raw HTTP POST containing vp_token, presentation_submission, and optionally state parameters. The gateway is the first RP component to touch the incoming data.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Raw HTTP POST request arriving at the API gateway's response_uri endpoint.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 2. API gateway forwards VP Token and HTTP context to verification engine
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: The gateway captures Layer 3 environmental context before forwarding: source IP (for geo-lookup), TLS fingerprint (JA3/JA4 hash), HTTP headers (User-Agent, Accept-Language), and increments the velocity counter for the personal_identifier (if known from state correlation). The gateway bundles the raw VP Token with this environmental metadata and forwards both to the verification engine as an internal service call.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: VP Token + HTTP context metadata forwarded to the verification engine.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 3. Verification engine emits signal array to composition engine
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: The verification engine processes the VP Token through two verification layers. Layer 1 checks for OID4VP protocol errors — if the submission is an error response rather than a VP Token, Layer 1 signals are generated and Layer 2 is skipped. Layer 2 runs the full credential verification pipeline: issuer signature (§29.2.36), disclosure hashes (§29.2.37), temporal validity (iat/exp), revocation status (§29.2.8), holder binding via KB-JWT (§29.2.2–§29.2.5), trust chain resolution via LoTE, and WUA validation (§29.2.41). Each check produces a pass or fail signal. The complete signal array (Layer 1 + Layer 2 + Layer 3 context signals) is sent to the composition engine.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Signal array containing all Layer 1, Layer 2, and Layer 3 signals with severity classifications.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 4. Composition engine sends ALLOW decision to gateway
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: When the composite risk score is below 20 and no S0 (Critical) signal is present, the composition engine sends an ALLOW decision to the API gateway. This is the happy path — all verification checks passed, environmental context is normal, and no anomalies were detected. The RP can proceed with the business transaction using the verified credential attributes.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: ALLOW decision dispatched to the API gateway.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 5. API gateway returns HTTP 200 with redirect_uri to wallet (ALLOW)
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: The gateway returns HTTP 200 to the Wallet with a redirect_uri that continues the user's session. The redirect URI typically points to the RP's success page or the next step in the business flow (e.g., account creation confirmation, age-gated content access, KYC completion). The Wallet opens the redirect URI in the device browser to complete the user journey.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: HTTP 200 response with redirect_uri for session continuation.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 6. Composition engine sends FLAG decision to gateway
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: When the composite risk score falls between 20 and 49, the composition engine sends a FLAG decision — the presentation is accepted (the user's session proceeds) but marked for post-hoc review. This threshold captures low-confidence anomalies that do not warrant blocking or step-up: minor clock skew, first-time device, unusual but not impossible geo-location.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: FLAG decision dispatched to the API gateway (allow + review marker).
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 7. API gateway returns HTTP 200 with redirect_uri to wallet (FLAG)
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: The gateway returns the same HTTP 200 with redirect_uri as in the ALLOW path — the user experience is identical. The FLAG decision is invisible to the Wallet and the user. The RP processes the credential attributes normally; the review marker is handled asynchronously by the security operations team.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: HTTP 200 response with redirect_uri — user session proceeds normally despite FLAG.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 8. Composition engine sends low-priority VSI event to SIEM
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: The composition engine emits a VSI event to the SIEM/SOAR platform with priority: low. The event contains the full signal array, composite risk score, contributing signals with individual weights, and the FLAG decision rationale. Security analysts review these events in batch — typically during daily or weekly triage. The event follows the §30.8 VSI Event JSON Schema, enabling correlation with previous presentations from the same personal_identifier_hash.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Low-priority VSI event ingested into SIEM for batch review.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 9. Composition engine sends STEP-UP decision to gateway
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: When the composite risk score falls between 50 and 79, the composition engine sends a STEP-UP decision. The presentation is conditionally accepted — the credential attributes are tentatively available, but the RP requires additional authentication before granting full access. Step-up challenges include: re-authentication with WebAuthn (§15), presentation of an additional credential (e.g., a QEAA on top of the PID), or a fresh biometric confirmation. The specific challenge type is determined by the RP's risk policy configuration.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: STEP-UP decision dispatched to the API gateway with challenge type specification.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 10. API gateway returns HTTP 200 with step-up challenge to wallet
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: The gateway returns HTTP 200 with a response body that directs the Wallet to a step-up challenge rather than a simple redirect. The challenge payload specifies what additional authentication is required. The Wallet or device browser presents the challenge to the user — the session remains active but access is gated until the challenge is satisfied.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: HTTP 200 response with step-up challenge payload.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 11. Composition engine sends BLOCK decision to gateway
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: When the composite risk score exceeds 80 or any S0 (Critical) signal is present, the composition engine sends an immediate BLOCK decision. The S0 override is absolute — a single critical signal (e.g., KBJWT_AUD_MISMATCH, SDJWT_SIG_INVALID, WUA_INVALID) triggers BLOCK regardless of how low the other signals score. The credential attributes are discarded; the RP does not proceed with any business logic.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: BLOCK decision dispatched to the API gateway.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 12. API gateway returns HTTP 403 to wallet (BLOCK)
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: The gateway returns HTTP 403 Forbidden to the Wallet. The response body contains a generic error message — the RP does not disclose the specific signal(s) that triggered the block to avoid leaking information to potential attackers. The Wallet displays an error to the user indicating that verification was unsuccessful.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: HTTP 403 response terminating the verification session.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: 13. Composition engine sends critical-priority VSI event to SIEM
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: The composition engine emits a VSI event to the SIEM/SOAR platform with priority: critical. Unlike the low-priority FLAG events, critical events trigger automated incident response playbooks: the source IP range is added to a quarantine watchlist, the personal_identifier_hash is flagged for enhanced monitoring on subsequent presentations, and an alert is dispatched to the on-call security analyst. The event contains the full signal array, composite risk score, the S0 signal(s) that triggered the block, and correlation data from the past 24 hours.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Audit Telemetry: Critical VSI event triggers SOAR playbook execution. Source IP quarantined. On-call analyst alerted. Full signal array preserved for forensic correlation.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Artifact Produced: Critical-priority VSI event ingested into SIEM/SOAR, triggering automated incident response.
passages.headingText: Signal Flow: VP Token to Decision

passages.text: Each VSI signal emits a structured event suitable for SIEM ingestion. The schema extends the §31.3.1 Verification Result Object with per-signal event data, enabling correlation across multiple presentations and drill-down from aggregate dashboards to individual signal detail.

GDPR compliance (§31.3): The event schema logs personal_identifier_hash (a one-way hash), not the raw personal_identifier. The source_ip is included for security incident response (GDPR Art. 6(1)(f) legitimate interest) and must be subject to the RP's IP address retention policy. No attribute values (family_name, birth_date) are included in VSI events — only the credential type, format, and signal metadata.
Always "vsi_signal" — distinguishes VSI events from other SIEM events
The VSI signal identifier from §30.3–§30.5
1, 2, or 3 — the signal's origin layer
STRIDE classification (Spoofing/Tampering/Replay/DoS or null)
Cross-reference to §29 threat catalogue entry (e.g., "§29.2.4")
Client IP from direct_post request
JA3/JA4 hash of the TLS connection
Aggregate risk score at time of decision
"ALLOW", "FLAG", "STEP_UP", or "BLOCK"
correlation.personal_identifier_hash
SHA-256 hash of personal_identifier for cross-session correlation
correlation.previous_signals_24h
Signal IDs from prior sessions in the last 24 hours
session_id linking to the full §31.3.1 verification result object
For SIEM platforms that ingest Common Event Format (CEF), the VSI event maps to:
Splunk — Top 10 signals in the last hour:
Elasticsearch (KQL) — All S0 signals with BLOCK decision:
Elasticsearch — Credential leak pattern (expired + velocity):

passages.text: How to read this table: Each row traces a single signal through the entire security architecture — from the verification pipeline (§30) back to the threat it detects (§29), forward to the alert it triggers (§31.2), and outward to the regulatory obligation it activates. This is the operational value of VSI: converting a scattered set of protocol errors and verification failures into a single, traceable intelligence pipeline.
passages.headingText: 30.9 Cross-Reference Map: Signal to Threat to Alert to Regulation

passages.text: Not all verifier vendors expose the same level of signal granularity. Some return rich per-check failure data; others return opaque pass/fail results. The RP's ability to implement a VSI pipeline depends on the vendor's signal exposure.
This assessment evaluates vendor capabilities across five VSI-relevant dimensions (cross-references §27.6 Unified Vendor Capability Matrix and §27.8 Delivery Mode Selection Guide):
Does the API/webhook return a named failure reason per check (e.g., signature_invalid, revoked, holder_binding_failed), or just a boolean pass / fail?
"When verification fails, does your API response include the specific check that failed and the reason?"
Does the response include a per-check result array (signature ✅, revocation ✅, holder-binding ❌), or a single aggregate boolean?
"Does your verification result include a breakdown of individual policy checks, as described in §31.3.1?"
Can the RP access the raw VP Token (JWT string or CBOR bytes) for independent verification and signal extraction, or only vendor-processed results?
"Can we receive the raw VP Token in addition to your verification result, to run our own verification pipeline?"
Does the vendor distinguish between verification_failed (check failed), verification_error (infrastructure error), verification_timeout, and user_cancelled?
"Does your webhook payload differentiate between verification failure, system error, timeout, and user cancellation?"
Does the vendor produce structured events (JSON with consistent schema) suitable for SIEM ingestion, or are events only available via a vendor-specific dashboard?
"Can we export verification events as structured JSON via webhook or API for ingestion into our SIEM?"
🟢 High (library model — RP controls signal extraction)

Key insight: Library/SDK-embed vendors (walt.id OCI, Spruce DIDKit, Scytáles SDK) inherently provide full VSI readiness because the RP runs the verification pipeline locally and controls all signal extraction. SaaS verifier vendors vary widely — the RP must explicitly evaluate signal granularity during vendor selection. RPs that require a full VSI pipeline (e.g., financial RPs subject to DORA) should strongly prefer library/SDK-embed delivery models (§26.6.5 Model L2) or negotiate per-signal webhook events with their SaaS provider.

NIS2 Art. 21(2)(b) — incident handling: The monitoring framework and alert triggers in this section address the NIS2 requirement for incident handling for RPs in NIS2-covered sectors. EUDI-specific events listed in §31.2 (WRPAC compromise, trust anchor manipulation, mass verification failure) can trigger NIS2 Art. 23 incident reporting — 24h early warning, 72h notification, 1-month final report. See §21.6 for sector scope and reporting timeline details.
Financial RPs integrating with the EUDI Wallet should monitor the following metrics:
Ratio of successful verifications to total presentation requests
Time from receiving encrypted response to verification complete
Time to fetch and evaluate Status List
Percentage of revocation checks served from cache
Days until WRPAC certificate expires
Time since last LoTE update was fetched
Breakdown: access_denied, invalid_request, expired_credential, etc.
Spike in any category → investigate
Cross-border presentation ratio
Percentage of presentations from foreign MS PIDs
Unexpected spike → possible attack vector

Operating an RP in a pan-European, federated, cryptographic ecosystem requires continuous observability across multiple application and infrastructure layers. The specific monitoring tooling (e.g., Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog, Splunk) is entirely at the discretion of the RP's internal engineering organisation. However, it is mandatory that the logical triggers defined in this section are mapped natively into the RP's centralised dashboards, Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems, and operational incident response workflows — including automated escalation to DORA Art. 17 major incident classification pipelines where applicable.

The alert triggers in this section consume signals classified by the Verification Signal Intelligence taxonomy (§30). Each named signal ID referenced in the tables below (e.g., KBJWT_NONCE_REUSE, LOTE_SIG_INVALID) maps to a specific entry in the §30 signal inventory, with a defined severity level (S0–S4) and SIEM event schema (§30.8). The §30.9 cross-reference map provides a complete Signal → Alert → Regulation traceability table.
The alert triggers are organised into six operational pillars.
Triggers in the EUDI ecosystem often carry immediate legal liability consequences. RPs must bridge technical metrics directly into regulatory reporting SLAs.
DORA Major Incident threshold crossed
Total verification bypass detected; or WRPAC private key compromise confirmed
DORA Art. 17 — automatically flag incident for 4-hour regulatory reporting to competent financial authorities
Confirmed exfiltration of decrypted PID datasets from RP storage
GDPR Art. 33 — activate 72-hour supervisory authority notification pipeline; Art. 34 data subject notification if risk is high
Cross-RP collusion indicator
Detection of overlapping SD-JWT salts or cnf.jwk thumbprints matching known external datasets
eIDAS Art. 5b(4) / GDPR Art. 5(1)(b) — investigate immediately; storing or sharing presentation-unique elements violates data minimisation (§11.10)
Monitors the foundational anchors tying the RP to the European federation. Failures in this layer result in systemic verification outages or immediate regulatory non-compliance.
< 30 days (Warning); < 7 days (Critical)

Initiate automated certificate renewal pipeline with the National Access CA. Edge case: the replacement WRPAC may be issued but lack a compliant Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT) — do not deploy a certificate without SCT (§5.2.4)
Unauthorised WRPAC detected in CT log
> 0 unauthorised issuances observed
A rogue or mistaken CA issued a certificate for the RP's domain. Trigger immediate revocation of the rogue certificate via the Trusted List operator (§5.2.4)
> 500ms for signing operations
Evaluate hardware security module health; scale signing capacity. If offline, the RP cannot execute client_assertion authentication or sign JARs
LoTE staleness or fetch failure
> 24 hours since last successful fetch

Force manual cache refresh; investigate outbound network rules preventing national Trusted List synchronisation. Edge case: network partition isolating the RP from the EU Trust Framework infrastructure
Issuer metadata fetch failure
> 3 consecutive failures from the same Issuer iss
Activate temporary cache fallback; raise network-level alert if a specific Member State's .well-known/openid-credential-issuer endpoint is persistently down
Status List / revocation endpoint outage
> 5 minutes HTTP 5xx or timeout

Switch to cached Status List immediately to prevent fail-closed denial of service. Notify on-call engineer. Edge case: an Issuer may rotate the Status List URI without publishing updated metadata — purge local cache and re-fetch Issuer metadata
Trust Anchor key rotation failure
LoTE signature unverifiable against cached root keys

The EU or a Member State rotated their root signing keys, but the RP's local trust store failed to synchronise. Halt all verification until the trust store is updated — accepting credentials against an unverified LoTE is a critical security gap
Deep inspection of the EUDI cryptographic constructs. Alerts in this layer almost universally indicate a direct, active attack against the RP's core security controls.
KB-JWT / SD-JWT signature verification spike
Spike > 5% failure rate above baseline
Sudden spike indicates forged credentials testing the perimeter, or a supply-chain bug in the verification SDK (§29.2.20). Quarantine source IP and escalate to security team
Nonce / state replay detection
> 0 duplicate nonces utilised across independent sessions
Active replay attack (§29.2.1). The exact same attestation payload is being replayed across distinct HTTP sessions. Invalidate all sessions sharing the nonce
Relay attack detected (§29.2.4). The user is presenting a credential whose aud claim does not match the RP's registered client_id. Drop presentation and log all client metadata
Wallet Unit Attestation (WUA) failure
Spike > 1% of authentications

Compromised, rooted, or emulated devices are attempting presentations. Flag user sessions for high-risk manual review. Edge case: a Wallet Provider may delay WUA rotation causing temporary mass failures after a firmware update
Algorithmic downgrade attempt
> 5% of presentations using unexpected algorithms
Attacker attempting to force weak cryptographic algorithms (e.g., RS256 instead of ES256, or non-standard ECDH curves). Deny presentation and log the attempted algorithm
KB-JWT iat > 60 seconds from server receipt time
Indicator of extreme network lag, Wallet clock desynchronisation, or potential replay delay. Log for pattern analysis
Selective Disclosure salt collision
> 0 instances of duplicate salts within a single disclosure array
Malformed SD-JWT structure. Edge case: attacker fuzzing the disclosure array to manipulate hash verification logic. Reject payload and log the raw structure for forensic analysis
Operates at the OpenID4VP / DC API protocol integration surface, detecting infrastructure stress, fuzzing, and volumetric attacks.
PAR endpoint resource exhaustion
HTTP 429 responses > 2% of total PAR requests
Potential Denial-of-Wallet (DDoS) attack flooding the backend with pending authorisation session states. Implement rate limiting per source IP and per client_id
Callback URI origin mismatch
Attempted cross-origin redirect manipulation. The presentation response is returning to a mismatched or unauthorised origin. Log IP aggressively and reject
Malformed payload parsing failures
> 100 parse faults per minute
Automated fuzzing of the JWE/JWS presentation endpoints. Drop traffic at the WAF level based on IP reputation scoring
App-to-app deep link timeout
> 15% timeout rate for cross-device flows
User taps the authentication button on mobile, the Wallet app opens, but the presentation never returns via the redirect_uri. Indicates OS-level deep-link breakage or Wallet-side rendering failure
Token vs. authorisation asymmetry
The PAR endpoint allocates sessions, but the token/response endpoint never receives the corresponding payload. Indicates session dropping, UX abandonment, or intermediary network failures
TLS cipher downgrade attempts
> 5% of TLS handshakes using unexpected cipher suites
Attacker attempting to force weak TLS configurations. Deny handshake and investigate TLS termination proxy configuration
Application-layer heuristics applied to cryptographically valid presentations, detecting systemic abuse of the identity system.
Presentation velocity anomaly
> 5 presentations from the same PID or pseudonym within 10 minutes
Bot activity, shared credential abuse, or aggressive account enumeration. Implement temporary soft-lock or step-up re-authentication challenge
Revoked credential submission spike
> 3 presentations with revoked status within 1 hour
Attacker testing a purchased or leaked database of extracted credentials. Route signals to fraud intelligence team and consider IP-level blocking
Data minimisation / oversharing violation
Wallet returns claims not present in the RP's DCQL query

The Wallet implementation has a privacy bug (e.g., returning address when only age_over_18 was requested), or a compromised Wallet is dumping all available data. Discard unrequested data immediately per GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) and log the incident
Test environment credential leakage
Presentation of credentials issued by staging or test Issuers to the production RP endpoint
Reject immediately. Edge case: a Wallet Provider may deploy test credentials with production-like iss URIs during pilot programmes — maintain an explicit blocklist of known test Issuer identifiers
Continuous access evaluation — mid-session revocation
Status List check reveals revocation for a credential backing an active session

A previously authenticated user's credential has been revoked while their web session remains active. Sever the session token immediately and redirect to re-authentication. This is critical for financial RPs where DORA Art. 9 mandates continuous access controls
Cross-border geolocation skew
Source IP geolocation does not match the Issuer Member State

A German PID presented from a non-European VPN exit node. Contextual signal to elevate the transaction's internal fraud risk score — not necessarily malicious (legitimate travel, VPN usage), but correlated with relay and phishing attacks
Expired credential submission spike
Spike in exp claim validation failures
Monitor for UX implications — the user may not realise their Wallet credential requires manual re-issuance or renewal from the PID Provider
Product-level alerts ensuring the RP is not losing legitimate users due to friction, Wallet-side latency, or schema compatibility errors.
Authorisation abandonment rate
> 15% drop-off after QR code display or redirect initiation
Users generate the authorisation challenge but never submit the presentation payload. Suggests poor Wallet-side UX, deep-link failure, QR rendering issues, or user confusion about the flow
access_denied volumetric spike
> 10% of standard presentation volume

Users are explicitly tapping "Decline" on the Wallet consent screen. Edge case: the RP may be requesting an intimidating array of sensitive attributes in its DCQL query — review the attribute set for data minimisation compliance (§29.2.15)
p95 verification pipeline latency > 1000ms
End-to-end decryption, SD-JWT parsing, or future Zero-Knowledge Proof (ZKP) evaluation is CPU-starved. Scale verification worker processes horizontally
Presentation from unknown vct or docType

A Member State may have deployed a new credential schema, or attackers are spoofing experimental credential types. Log and review; update the RP's supported credential type registry if the new vct is legitimate
Spike in legacy credential formats (e.g., pure mDoc where SD-JWT VC is preferred)
Users with outdated Wallet software are attempting presentations in deprecated formats. Track for graceful deprecation scheduling and user communication
Presentation payload assembly delay
> 500ms for payload reassembly
The Wallet transmits large credential fragments, and the RP backend struggles to reassemble them within acceptable latency. Monitor memory buffers and consider payload size limits
Sudden drop in overall presentation success rate
Success rate drops below 95%

Systemic failure indicator. Check WRPAC validity, JAR construction logic, Status List availability, and verification SDK health in parallel. Correlate with deployment timestamps to identify regression
GDPR Art. 30 and DORA Art. 28 require RPs to maintain records of processing. For EUDI Wallet integrations, the audit trail should capture:
ISO 8601 timestamp of presentation
same-device, cross-device, proximity-supervised, proximity-unsupervised
MS that issued the credential
List of claims in DCQL query
List of claims actually received (may differ if partial)
success, failed_signature, failed_revocation, user_denied, etc.
Serial number of the WRPAC used
Per-credential, per-policy pass/fail breakdown (§31.3.1)
ISO 8601 duration of the verification pipeline execution (e.g., PT0.012S)
Count of verification policies executed

GDPR note: The audit trail should NOT store the attribute values themselves (e.g., not the family name or date of birth). Store only the attribute names and verification results. Raw PID data should be deleted once the business purpose is fulfilled.

Production verification systems should produce a structured verification result object with per-credential, per-policy granularity. This enables precise forensics for DORA incident analysis (Art. 17) and detailed GDPR processing records (Art. 30). The recommended structure is:
Key design principles for the result object:

Per-credential granularity — Combined presentations (§18.5) may contain multiple credentials; each gets its own policy result array. A partial failure (one credential passes, another fails) should be logged with per-credential detail.

Per-credential granularity — Combined presentations (§18.5) may contain multiple credentials; each gets its own policy result array. A partial failure (one credential passes, another fails) should be logged with per-credential detail.

Policy descriptions — Human-readable descriptions enable audit reviewers to understand what was checked without needing to consult implementation documentation. This supports DORA's requirement for "clear and comprehensive" ICT incident records.

Policy descriptions — Human-readable descriptions enable audit reviewers to understand what was checked without needing to consult implementation documentation. This supports DORA's requirement for "clear and comprehensive" ICT incident records.

No attribute values in results — The result object records the credential type and policy outcomes, not the attribute values (family_name, birth_date, etc.). This maintains the GDPR principle from §31.3: log attribute names, not values.

No attribute values in results — The result object records the credential type and policy outcomes, not the attribute values (family_name, birth_date, etc.). This maintains the GDPR principle from §31.3: log attribute names, not values.

Execution timing — ISO 8601 duration enables performance monitoring and SLA tracking (§31.1 key metrics). Abnormally long verification times may indicate revocation list fetch failures or certificate chain issues.

Execution timing — ISO 8601 duration enables performance monitoring and SLA tracking (§31.1 key metrics). Abnormally long verification times may indicate revocation list fetch failures or certificate chain issues.

CIR 2025/847 requires RPs to receive and act on wallet solution security breach notifications within 24 hours (§21.7). The §31.2 monitoring framework should integrate breach notification processing as an additional alert category:
Wallet solution suspension received
CIRAS / MS notification channel
Wallet solution withdrawal received
CIRAS / MS notification channel
Wallet solution re-establishment received
CIRAS / MS notification channel
Wallet solution certification status changed in certified wallet list
Periodic certified list poll

CIRAS integration: From May 2026, breach notifications to the Commission and MS Single Points of Contact flow through CIRAS (Art. 10). RP notification is via the registering MS's notification channel. RPs should proactively establish contact with their MS's Single Point of Contact (Art. 46c(1)) to confirm the notification delivery mechanism (email, API, push notification) and ensure integration with the §31 monitoring pipeline.

Audit trail extension: When a presentation is rejected due to a suspended or withdrawn wallet solution, the §31.3 audit trail should include breach_notification_id and breach_notification_timestamp fields — linking the rejection to the specific breach notification. This provides regulatory compliance evidence demonstrating that the RP acted on the notification within the required timeframe.

This group covers the integration of Qualified Electronic Signatures (QES) with the EUDI Wallet. It details the three primary signing flow patterns (QTSP Web Portal, Wallet-Channelled, RP-Channelled), the CSC API v2.0 protocol, and the RP's obligations when verifying or handling PAdES signatures.

Document signing is one of three core capabilities of the EUDI Wallet — alongside identification (PID presentation) and attestation presentation (QEAA/PuB-EAA). While the previous chapters focus on how RPs verify identity and attributes, this chapter covers the RP's role when the Wallet creates Qualified Electronic Signatures (QES) — legally binding signatures equivalent to handwritten signatures under eIDAS 2.0.
The regulatory foundation for document signing is established across multiple instruments:
Wallet functional requirement: "create qualified electronic signatures and seals"
Wallet SHALL support qualified certificates bound to local/external/remote QSCDs; free-of-charge QES for non-professional use
Free-of-charge requirement affects RP pricing models
SCAs may be provided by wallet providers, QTSPs, or wallet-relying parties
PAdES (ETSI EN 319 142-1) is the mandatory signature format
Format requirement for signed documents
CSC API v2.0 (20 April 2023) is the mandated protocol for integrated SCAs with remote QSCDs
ETSI TS 119 431-1 V1.3.1 for remote QSCD management
26 High-Level Requirements (QES_01–QES_24a)
Detailed functional requirements

The RP participates in signing flows in three distinct configurations (QES_06), each with different protocol stacks, responsibilities, and integration complexity. This chapter walks through each flow, the underlying CSC API v2.0 protocol, the Document Retrieval bridging mechanism, and the RP's specific obligations.

Disambiguation — SCA: In this chapter, "SCA" refers to Signature Creation Application (ETSI TS 119 432), not Strong Customer Authentication (PSD2). These are entirely different concepts despite sharing the same acronym. Where ambiguity exists, this chapter uses "SCA (Signature)" or "SCA (Payment)".
ARF HLR QES_06 mandates that Wallet Providers support at least one of three remote QES creation flows. Each flow assigns a different orchestration role to the RP, the Wallet, and the QTSP.

In this flow, the RP initiates signing by redirecting the User to a QTSP's web portal. The Wallet's role is limited to authenticating the User at the QTSP portal — the signing infrastructure is entirely managed by the QTSP.
1. User requests document signing at Relying Party

The User navigates to the RP's service — a government portal, a bank's contract page, or a notarial platform — and initiates a signing action on a specific document. At this point, the RP has the document in its infrastructure but does not possess signing capability. The RP's role is to identify that the User wants a QES and to route them to the appropriate QTSP.

The RP must have a prior contractual arrangement with the QTSP (or offer the User a choice among multiple QTSPs). This is a business-level relationship, not a protocol-level discovery — no standardized QTSP discovery mechanism exists (see Open Question #23 in §36).
Artifact Produced: Pending unsigned document object (Application layer).
2. Relying Party Server redirects User to QTSP web portal

The RP redirects the User's browser to the QTSP's signing portal, passing the document reference and a callback URL. The exact redirect mechanism is QTSP-specific (not standardized by CSC API or ARF), but typically follows a pattern like:

The RP passes the document hash alongside the URL so the QTSP can verify document integrity upon retrieval. The callback_url is where the QTSP will deliver the signed document or a signing status notification after the process completes.
Key difference from Scenarios B/C: The RP does not interact with the CSC API at any point. The redirect is a simple HTTP 302 — the RP's integration surface is minimal.
Artifact Produced: QTSP HTTP 302 routing redirect (Network layer).
3. User Browser accesses QTSP portal

The User's browser follows the redirect and loads the QTSP's signing portal. The portal may display a welcome screen or require the User to consent to the signing session before proceeding. The QTSP now needs to verify the User's identity before granting access to signing credentials.
4. QTSP Portal requests User authentication via EUDI Wallet

The QTSP's web portal requires User identity verification before granting access to signing credentials. The QTSP initiates an authentication request to the EUDI Wallet. This may use OpenID4VP (§8–§11) for PID presentation, or another QTSP-specific mechanism (SAML, national eID scheme).
If using OpenID4VP, the QTSP acts as a Verifier (it holds its own WRPAC) and constructs a standard presentation request:

The QTSP uses the personal_identifier to look up or create the User's signing credential record. If the User does not yet have a qualified certificate, the QTSP may initiate a certificate issuance flow (out of scope for this chapter).
Artifact Produced: JSON vp_token identity verification request (Protocol layer).
5. EUDI Wallet prompts User for authentication consent

Upon receiving the authentication request from the QTSP, the Wallet displays a consent prompt to the User. The prompt shows the QTSP's identity (verified via its WRPAC) and the specific PID claims being requested (family name, given name, personal identifier). The User must actively approve before the Wallet releases any identity data.
6. User approves authentication with EUDI Wallet

The User reviews the authentication request and confirms their identity by providing biometric (fingerprint, face) or PIN authentication within the Wallet. This step satisfies QES_14's requirement that the user actively agrees to the authentication operation. The Wallet is now authorised to release the PID presentation to the QTSP.
7. EUDI Wallet sends authentication response to QTSP Portal

The Wallet returns the PID presentation (a verifiable credential containing the User's identity claims) to the QTSP. If using OpenID4VP, this is a vp_token response. The QTSP validates the presentation's signature, extracts the personal_identifier, and establishes the User's authenticated session. The User is now logged into the QTSP portal and ready to proceed with signing.
Artifact Produced: Authenticated vp_token validating identity to the QTSP platform, generating a local QTSP web session buffer.
8. QTSP Portal presents document to User (WYSIWYS)

Per QES_10, the Signature Creation Application must present the document representation to the User before creating the signature — the WYSIWYS (What You See Is What You Sign) principle. In Scenario A, this responsibility falls entirely on the QTSP's portal.
The QTSP retrieves the document from the document_url provided by the RP, verifies the hash matches document_hash, and renders it for the User to review.
9. QTSP Portal prompts User for signature creation consent

After the User has reviewed the rendered document, the QTSP displays a signing consent prompt. This prompt must clearly indicate what is being signed (document name, hash, or other identifier) and require explicit User action (e.g., clicking a "Sign" button). Passive consent or pre-checked boxes are not acceptable under eIDAS 2.0.
10. User approves signature creation on QTSP Portal

The User explicitly authorises signature creation by clicking the "Sign" button or similar affirmative action in the QTSP portal. This satisfies QES_14 — "the wallet unit, including the SCA, shall ensure that the user actively agrees to create a qualified electronic signature." In Scenario A, the QTSP portal acts as the SCA UI, collecting the User's explicit consent.
11. QTSP Portal computes document hash and invokes remote QSCD
The QTSP's Signature Creation Application computes the PAdES-specific document hash. For PAdES, this involves:
Preparing the PDF with a signature placeholder (a reserved byte range for the CMS signature structure)
Computing the SHA-256 hash over the document's byte ranges (everything except the placeholder)
Sending the hash to its remote QSCD via the CSC API POST /signatures/signHash

The QSCD creates the PKCS#1 digital signature and returns it. The QTSP then embeds the signature value into the PAdES placeholder, producing the final signed PDF document (conformance level ADES_B_B minimum, with optional qualified timestamp for ADES_B_T).
Artifact Produced: Cryptographically sealed ADES_B_T PAdES (PDF Advanced Electronic Signature) document.
Note: The CSC API interaction here is entirely internal to the QTSP — neither the RP nor the Wallet sees these API calls. From the RP's perspective, the entire QTSP infrastructure is a black box.
12. QTSP Portal delivers signed document to Relying Party Server
The QTSP delivers the signed PDF back to the RP via the previously specified callback_url. The delivery mechanism varies by QTSP implementation — common patterns include:

Verification obligation: Even though the QTSP created the signature, the RP should independently validate the PAdES signature upon receipt. This guards against tampering during transport and confirms the QTSP used a qualified certificate.
Artifact Produced: Signed callback notification (Network layer).
13. Relying Party Server confirms signing completion to User

The RP downloads the signed document from the signed_document_url, verifies the PAdES signature (using ETSI EN 319 102-1 validation rules), and stores it. The User receives confirmation that the document has been signed — typically via a success page in the RP's web interface or an email notification. The signing transaction is now complete.
Failure Path: If the document hash calculated locally by the RP misaligns with the signed bytes returned from the callback, the RP aborts the business process.
Audit Telemetry: QES_VERIFICATION_SUCCESS or QES_INVALID_SIGNATURE_DROP mapped explicitly in the RP's core accounting ledger.
Artifact Produced: Verified legal document ledger entry (Business layer).
QTSP internally (not exposed to RP or Wallet)
PAdES mandatory (Annex IV §1)

In this flow, the Wallet is the primary orchestrator. The RP provides the document, and the Wallet uses its built-in RQES SDK to invoke the CSC API against a remote QTSP, sign the document, and return the signed result to the RP.
1. Relying Party Server initiates Document Retrieval

The RP generates a QR code or deep link containing a request_uri pointing to a JAR-signed request object (see §32.4 for the full Document Retrieval protocol). The User scans this with their Wallet. The URI uses the mdoc-openid4vp scheme:

The RP must pre-compute the document hash and include it in the request object before generating the QR code. This hash will be verified by the Wallet after document download to prevent substitution attacks.
Artifact Produced: mdoc-openid4vp URI containing short-lived JAR retrieval handle.
2. EUDI Wallet resolves the JAR-signed request object

The Wallet fetches the request object from the request_uri. The request is a JAR-signed JWT (RFC 9101) containing document locations, hashes, and access methods. The RP authenticates via one of three client ID schemes: x509_san_uri, x509_san_dns, or pre_registered (§32.4.4).

The Wallet validates the JAR signature against the RP's X.509 certificate (extracted from the x5c header or resolved via client_id_scheme). If signature verification fails, the Wallet rejects the request.
Failure Path: Validation of client_id against the embedded X.509 certificate halts if a mismatch is detected, preventing rogue clients from hijacking request objects.
3. EUDI Wallet downloads documents from Relying Party Server
The Wallet downloads each document from its uri using the specified access_method. The access method determines how the Wallet authenticates to the document host:
No authentication required — document is publicly accessible
HTTP Basic Authentication with username/password
Bearer token from a separate OAuth2 authorization flow

The Wallet stores the downloaded document bytes in memory for hash verification in the next step. If the download fails (network error, 404, auth failure), the Wallet aborts the flow and notifies the User.
4. EUDI Wallet verifies document hashes
After downloading, the Wallet computes the SHA-256 hash of each document and compares it against the hash value from the JAR-signed request object:

This verification is the primary defence against document substitution. The RP commits to the exact document content by including its hash in the signed request object before the User scans the QR code. Even if an attacker intercepts the download and substitutes a different document, the hash mismatch will be detected before the User signs.

Failure Path: If any hash mismatches, the Wallet aborts the flow and displays a security warning to the User. The error message explicitly avoids revealing the expected hash to avoid aiding attackers in crafting substitution documents.
5. EUDI Wallet selects Remote QTSP for signing from configured QTSP list

The Wallet determines which Remote QTSP to use for the signing operation. According to QES_18, the Wallet must have at least one pre-configured default QTSP. If multiple QTSPs are configured, the Wallet may either:
Auto-select the default QTSP configured by the User's preference
Prompt the User to select from the list of available QTSPs
For this flow, the Wallet selects the Wallet-Centric QTSP at walletcentric.signer.eudiw.dev, which will be used for all subsequent CSC API calls.
Artifact Produced: Selected QTSP endpoint https://walletcentric.signer.eudiw.dev/csc/v2 stored in Wallet signing session (Device layer).
6. EUDI Wallet requests RSSP capabilities via GET /csc/v2/info from Remote QTSP

The Wallet calls GET /csc/v2/info on the QTSP's base URI to discover its capabilities. The Wallet may use a pre-configured default QTSP (per QES_18, which mandates at least one default) or allow the User to select from multiple configured QTSPs.
Artifact Produced: HTTP GET CSC discovery request to walletcentric.signer.eudiw.dev (Network layer).
7. Remote QTSP returns metadata and OAuth2 authorization server URL

The authType: ["oauth2code"] confirms that this RSSP uses OAuth2 Authorization Code flow (the mandated mode in the EUDI ecosystem). The oauth2 URL points to the OAuth2 Authorization Server's discovery document, which the Wallet uses for the next two authorization steps.
8. EUDI Wallet sends OAuth2 authorization request to QTSP (Tier 1)

The Wallet initiates the OAuth2 Authorization Code flow (optionally with PAR per §7.5) to request access to the QTSP's signing services. This is Tier 1 of the dual-layer authorization model (§32.1.2).
The scope=service indicates this is a service-level authorization, not a credential-level one. The redirect_uri uses the rqes:// scheme registered by the EUDI reference wallet for OAuth callbacks.
9. Remote QTSP Authorization Server issues service access token to EUDI Wallet

Upon successful authorization (including user authentication if required by the QTSP), the Authorization Server returns a service access token. This token grants the Wallet permission to list credentials and query metadata, but not to sign — signing requires a separate Tier 2 credential-level authorization (step 11).
The service token is cached by the Wallet for the duration of the signing session to avoid repeated authorization round-trips.
Artifact Produced: Temporary OAuth2 Bearer access token scoped exclusively to metadata operations.
10. EUDI Wallet requests credential list via POST /credentials/list
Using the service access token obtained in step 8, the Wallet calls the CSC v2 POST /credentials/list endpoint to retrieve the User's available signing credentials at this QTSP:

The userID parameter identifies the user within the QTSP's credential management system. The request is authenticated using the service access token from the OAuth 2.0 flow completed in the previous step.
Artifact Produced: POST /credentials/list request with userID binding (Protocol layer).
11. Remote QTSP returns available signing credentials
The RSSP responds with the list of credentials available to the authenticated user, including their status, algorithm support, and certificate information:

The SCAL: "2" confirms that this credential supports SCAL2 hash-bound authorization — the mandated level per QES_23. The cert.certificates array contains the X.509 certificate chain that will be embedded in the PAdES signature.
12. EUDI Wallet prompts User to select signing credential from Remote QTSP list

The Wallet presents the available credentials to the User for selection. If multiple credentials exist, the User chooses which one to use for signing this document. If only one credential exists, the Wallet may auto-select it.
The selected credentialID will be used in subsequent CSC API calls to identify which key and certificate to use for the PAdES signature operation.
Artifact Produced: Selected credentialID=credential-qes-001 binding in Wallet signing session (Device layer).
13. User confirms signing credential selection in EUDI Wallet

The User reviews the credential details displayed by the Wallet — including the certificate subject name, issuer, validity period, and SCAL level — and explicitly confirms the selection. This confirmation is typically performed via biometric (fingerprint, face recognition) or device PIN, providing the first factor of the two-factor SCAL2 authentication.

This step establishes the User's intent to sign with this specific credential. The confirmation is logged by the Wallet for audit purposes and establishes non-repudiation evidence linking the User to the upcoming signature operation.
Failure Path: If the User's biometric or PIN authentication fails, the Wallet rejects the credential selection. The QTSP is not notified at this stage — the abort is Wallet-local.
Artifact Produced: Local Wallet signing intent record binding credentialID, document hash, and timestamp (Device layer).
14. EUDI Wallet prepares PDF for PAdES signing

The RQES SDK's calculateDocumentHashes function uses PodofoManager (a native C++ PDF library accessed via JNI on Android) to prepare the PDF for signing and compute the hash. This is not a simple file hash — it is a PAdES-specific hash:

Inject signature placeholder: PodofoManager adds a PKCS#7/CMS signature placeholder field to the PDF's incremental update section, reserving a byte range for the actual signature value. The exact size of the placeholder is calculated based on the certificate chain length and the signature algorithm to ensure sufficient space for the final CMS SignedData structure.
Artifact Produced: Unsigned raw PDF with byte-range CMS placeholder appended.
15. EUDI Wallet computes PAdES byte-range hash for Remote QTSP signing
With the placeholder injected, PodofoManager computes the SHA-256 hash over the PDF's byte ranges:
Compute byte-range hash: The SHA-256 hash is computed over the PDF's byte ranges (the entire document minus the placeholder bytes)
Record byte-range coordinates: The exact byte offsets are recorded for later signature embedding
The hash value is what will be sent to the RSSP for signing — the RSSP never sees the actual document content.
Artifact Produced: SHA-256 byte-range digest kF2Qk8mBNqvXMTb3UvZ4WbYJ9R0cvKHdXsU7oP5wF1M= over PAdES byte-range (Cryptographic layer).
16. EUDI Wallet initiates credential-level OAuth2 authorization with hash binding (Tier 2, SCAL2)

The Wallet initiates a second OAuth2 authorization, this time for the specific credential and bound to the specific document hashes. This is Tier 2 of the dual-layer model and the core SCAL2 security mechanism.
The document hashes are embedded in the authorization request via Rich Authorization Requests (RAR, RFC 9396):
Artifact Produced: SCAL2 RAR authorization request embedding document digest kF2Qk8mBNqvXMTb3UvZ4WbYJ9R0cvKHdXsU7oP5wF1M= bound to credential-qes-001 (Protocol layer).
17. User approves credential use in EUDI Wallet; Remote QTSP Authorization Server issues hash-bound credential access token

The QTSP's authorization server presents the User with a consent screen showing the document(s) to be signed. The User approves with biometric/PIN. The resulting credential access token is cryptographically bound to these specific hashes — if the Wallet later tries to sign different documents, the RSSP will reject the signHash call.
Artifact Produced: Hash-bound Tier 2 OAuth2 credential access token scoped to credential-qes-001 and document digest (Protocol layer).
Failure Path: If the User denies, the Wallet receives an access_denied error from the QTSP AS and aborts the complete signing session.
Audit Telemetry: SCAL2_CREDENTIAL_TOKEN_ISSUED logged by the QTSP's authorization server, binding the credentialID and document digest list.
18. EUDI Wallet signs document hash via Remote QTSP POST /signatures/signHash
With the hash-bound credential access token, the Wallet calls POST /signatures/signHash. For SCAL2, the hashes do not need to be re-sent in the request body (they are already bound in the token):

The signatures array contains the raw PKCS#1 digital signature value created by the remote QSCD. The operationMode: "S" indicates synchronous operation (the signature is computed immediately). The RSSP internally verifies that the signHash request corresponds to the hashes authorized in step 8 — this is the SCAL2 binding enforcement.
19. EUDI Wallet assembles signed PAdES document from QTSP signature value
The RQES SDK's createSignedDocuments function uses PodofoManager to embed the PKCS#1 signature value into the PAdES placeholder that was prepared in step 7:
Retrieves the prepared PDF (with placeholder) from step 7
Constructs a CMS SignedData structure containing: the PKCS#1 signature value from the RSSP, the signing certificate chain, and optionally a qualified timestamp (if ADES_B_T or higher was requested)
Writes the CMS structure into the reserved byte range
Produces the final signed PDF
The resulting document is a valid PAdES-signed PDF that can be verified by any PAdES-compliant validator (e.g., Adobe Acrobat, the EU DSS library).
Artifact Produced: PAdES-B-B (or higher) compliant signed PDF structure stored locally in Wallet.
20. EUDI Wallet dispatches signed document to Relying Party Server
Using the Document Retrieval protocol's dispatch mechanism (§32.4.5), the Wallet sends the signed document back to the RP at the response_uri specified in the original request object:

The RP receives the signed document, validates the PAdES signature, and stores the result. The documentWithSignature field contains the complete signed PDF — the RP does not need to perform any additional assembly.
Alternatively, if the RP specified signatureObject in the request, the Wallet returns only the detached CMS signature bytes, and the RP performs the final PDF assembly on its server.
Artifact Produced: Signed PAdES document dispatched to Relying Party response_uri via direct_post (Network layer).
21. Relying Party Server confirms QES signing completion to User
Upon receiving the signed document, the RP performs final validation and notifies the user of successful completion:

Signature validation: The RP verifies the PAdES signature using a standard validator (e.g., EU DSS library) to ensure: The signer certificate is valid and not revoked The signature covers the correct document hash The certificate chain traces to a trusted QTSP root
Signature validation: The RP verifies the PAdES signature using a standard validator (e.g., EU DSS library) to ensure:
The signer certificate is valid and not revoked
The signature covers the correct document hash
The certificate chain traces to a trusted QTSP root
Document persistence: The RP stores the signed document in its records system, linking it to the original transaction or contract.
Document persistence: The RP stores the signed document in its records system, linking it to the original transaction or contract.

User confirmation: The RP displays a confirmation page or sends a notification to the user confirming: The document was successfully signed The qualified electronic signature is legally valid A copy or receipt is available for download
User confirmation: The RP displays a confirmation page or sends a notification to the user confirming:
The document was successfully signed
The qualified electronic signature is legally valid
A copy or receipt is available for download
Example confirmation response rendered to the user:
At this point, the Wallet-Channelled RQES flow is complete. The user has successfully signed the document using a qualified electronic signature without leaving the RP's web application.
Audit Telemetry: RP_QES_ISSUED_WALLET_CENTRIC generated upon validation of the callback artifact.
Medium — Document Retrieval protocol + callback
No — RP only provides documents and receives results
eudi-lib-jvm-rqes-csc-kt (Android), eudi-lib-ios-rqes-csc-swift (iOS)
In this flow, the RP is the signing orchestrator. The RP selects the QTSP, manages the CSC API interaction server-side, and uses the Wallet only for User authentication and credential authorization.
1. User selects document for signing at Relying Party Server

The User uploads or selects a document for signing on the RP's platform. Unlike Scenarios A and B, the document stays on the RP's server throughout the entire flow — it is never transmitted to the Wallet or the QTSP (only the document hash is sent). This is the key architectural difference: the RP retains full control of the document lifecycle.
2. Relying Party Server selects QTSP for signing

The RP chooses which QTSP to use for signing. Per QES_04 note: "In a Relying Party-centric flow, the remote QTSP will likely be selected by the Relying Party, which implies the QSCD is managed by the remote QTSP."

The RP may pre-configure a single QTSP (most common for enterprise integrations) or maintain a list of qualified QTSPs offering interoperable CSC API v2.0 endpoints. No standardized QTSP discovery protocol exists (Open Question #23, §36) — the selection is based on contractual relationships and Trusted List verification (§32.7).
3. Relying Party Server discovers RSSP capabilities via GET /csc/v2/info
The RP calls GET /csc/v2/info on the QTSP's base URI to discover its capabilities. Unlike Scenario B where the Wallet makes this call, here the RP's server-side CSC client initiates the discovery:
The response includes the OAuth2 authorization server URL, supported signature algorithms, and available CSC methods.
4. Remote QTSP returns metadata to Relying Party Server
The QTSP's RSSP responds with its service metadata, including the OAuth2 authorization server URL, supported signature algorithms, and available CSC methods:
This metadata enables the RP to construct subsequent OAuth2 authorization requests correctly.
5. Relying Party Server requests service access token via Client Credentials

The RP uses the Client Credentials OAuth2 flow for server-to-server service authorization. This is the key difference from Scenario B — the RP authenticates as a confidential client with pre-registered credentials, not via interactive browser-based OAuth.

The grant_type=client_credentials indicates that no user interaction is needed for this step — the RP authenticates using its registered client ID and secret (or certificate-based authentication). This service access token authorizes the RP to list credentials and query metadata.
6. Remote QTSP returns service access token to Relying Party Server
The QTSP's authorization server responds with a service access token:

This service access token is valid for credential listing and metadata operations. It does not authorize signing — a separate credential access token is required for that, obtained later after user authorization.
7. Relying Party Server requests credential list from Remote QTSP
Using the service access token, the RP calls POST /credentials/list to retrieve the User's available signing credentials from the QTSP:

The QTSP responds with a list of credential IDs associated with the User, along with basic metadata (signing algorithm, certificate status). The RP selects the appropriate QES credential for the signing operation.
8. Remote QTSP returns credential IDs to Relying Party Server
The QTSP responds with the list of available signing credentials for the User:

Each credential ID represents a different QES certificate the User has registered with the QTSP. The RP selects the appropriate credential based on the signing context (e.g., corporate vs. personal certificate, validity period).
9. Relying Party Server computes document hash for signing

The RP prepares the PDF for PAdES signing by adding the signature placeholder and computing the SHA-256 hash over the document byte ranges. Since the RP is a server, it uses standard cryptographic libraries (e.g., BouncyCastle, OpenSSL) rather than a mobile PDF library:
This document hash will be bound to the credential authorization in later steps, enforcing SCAL2's hash-binding requirement.
10. Relying Party Server requests User authorization via EUDI Wallet (OpenID4VP with transaction_data)

The RP sends an OpenID4VP authorization request to the User's Wallet, including the document hash in the transaction_data extension (§15.15.5). This serves two purposes: (a) WYSIWYS — the Wallet displays the signing context to the User, and (b) proof of authorization — the signed KB-JWT response is cryptographic evidence that the User consented to sign this specific document.

The Wallet displays the label and qtsp_name to the User: "Sign 'Service Agreement - Example Corp' using RP-Centric QTSP?". The User approves with biometric/PIN. The Wallet returns a KB-JWT that includes a hash of the transaction_data in its payload — this cryptographically binds the User's consent to the specific document hashes.

Cross-reference: §15.15.5 covers the full transaction_data specification for QES use cases, including how the hash commitment chain ensures the Wallet cannot be tricked into signing a different document.
11. EUDI Wallet displays signing request to User (WYSIWYS)

Upon receiving the OpenID4VP request with transaction_data, the Wallet parses the document digest information and presents a What You See Is What You Sign (WYSIWYS) interface to the User. The display includes:
Document label: The human-readable name from transaction_data[0].document_digests[0].label
QTSP name: The qualified trust service provider performing the signing
Signature format: PAdES (PDF Advanced Electronic Signature)
Credential to be used: The specific QES credential referenced by credential_id

The Wallet ensures the displayed information matches the cryptographic hashes in the transaction_data. This prevents UI spoofing attacks where a malicious RP might display one document while requesting signature on another. The hash commitment in the KB-JWT response cryptographically binds the User's consent to the exact document hashes received.
12. User approves signing with biometric/PIN authentication
The User reviews the WYSIWYS display and decides whether to authorize the signing operation. If they approve, the Wallet prompts for local authentication:
Biometric: Face ID, Touch ID, or device fingerprint scanner
PIN/Password: The Wallet's unlock credential

This local authentication serves as the User consent signature — it proves the User was present and actively approved the signing operation. The Wallet cannot proceed without this step, ensuring the QES is always the result of explicit User intent.
Once authenticated, the Wallet generates a Key-Bound JWT (KB-JWT) containing:
The User's verifiable credential claims (from the requested PID or signing credential)
A cryptographic hash of the transaction_data (binding the consent to specific documents)
A signature from the Wallet's key pair (proving the response came from this Wallet instance)

The KB-JWT is signed with the Wallet's binding key (cnf.jwk), which the RP can verify against the Wallet's public key obtained during the initial wallet authentication or from the verifiable presentation.
13. EUDI Wallet returns authorization response to Relying Party Server
The Wallet sends the signed authorization response to the RP via the response_uri specified in the OpenID4VP request. The response is a JWT containing the VP token with the KB-JWT:
The RP validates the response:
Signature verification: Checks the KB-JWT signature against the Wallet's public key
Transaction data binding: Verifies that the transaction_data_hashes in the KB-JWT match the SHA-256 hashes of the transaction_data originally sent
Credential claims: Extracts the User's identity claims (family_name, given_name) from the VP token
Expiry check: Ensures the response hasn't expired

This authorization response serves as the User's cryptographic consent for the RP to proceed with credential authorization at the QTSP. The RP now has the proof it needs to demonstrate that the User approved signing these specific document hashes with this specific QTSP.

Security note: The direct_post.jwt response mode ensures the response is encrypted and integrity-protected during transmission. The RP's TLS certificate (referenced by client_id_scheme: x509_san_dns) is used for encryption.
14. Relying Party Server requests credential authorization from Remote QTSP (SCAL2 hash-bound)

Using the Wallet's authorization proof (the KB-JWT from step 8), the RP requests credential-level authorization from the QTSP. The document hashes are bound to the authorization request via RAR (Rich Authorization Requests):

This is the second tier of CSC's dual-layer OAuth2 model — credential authorization (scope: credential). Unlike the service access token obtained earlier, this token will be cryptographically bound to specific document hashes, enforcing SCAL2's hash-binding requirement per QES_23.
15. Remote QTSP issues hash-bound credential access token to Relying Party Server
The QTSP validates the authorization request and issues a credential access token bound to the specified document hashes:

The resulting credential access token has a short expiry (typically 300 seconds) and is bound to the specific document hashes. The RSSP will verify this binding when signHash is called — any mismatch causes the signing operation to fail.
16. Relying Party Server sends signHash request to Remote QTSP
The RP calls POST /signatures/signHash with the credential access token to obtain the digital signature from the QTSP's remote QSCD:

The operationMode: "S" indicates synchronous signing — the QTSP will return the signature value immediately rather than requiring polling. The credential access token's hash binding ensures the signature can only be applied to the hashes authorized in step 10.
17. Remote QTSP returns digital signature value to Relying Party Server
The RSSP routes the request to the remote QSCD and returns the raw ECDSA signature value:

The signature is a Base64-encoded ASN.1 DER structure containing the ECDSA (r, s) pair. This is the raw cryptographic signature over the document hash — not yet embedded in a CMS/PKCS#7 container or PDF structure.
Artifact Produced: Raw ASN.1 DER ECDSA signature sequence isolated from the PDF container.
18. Relying Party Server assembles PAdES document server-side
The RP embeds the signature value into the prepared PDF, creating a PAdES-B-LT (Long-Term) document. Unlike Scenario B (where the Wallet uses PodofoManager), the RP uses server-side PDF libraries:

The assembly process wraps the raw signature in a CMS Advanced Electronic Signature (CADES) container, embeds it into the PDF's AcroForm signature field, and adds a qualified timestamp (ADES_B_T level). The RP also logs the signing event per §32.8.

ETSI TS 119 101 obligation: Because the RP provided the SCA (orchestrated the signing flow, computed the hash, and assembled the document), QES_24a applies. The RP must comply with ETSI TS 119 101's policy and security requirements for the SCA implementation.

Failure Path: If the remote QSCD detects a mismatch between the token's hash bindings (authorization_details) and the target signHash string length or integrity, the HSM actively aborts the transaction via 400 Bad Request.
19. Relying Party Server delivers signed document to User
The signed PAdES document is stored on the RP's infrastructure and made available to the User for download. The RP typically:
Stores the signed document in the User's document archive with metadata (signing timestamp, certificate chain, QTSP identifier)
Notifies the User via email or in-app notification that the signed document is ready
Provides a download link or API endpoint for the User to retrieve the document
Validates the final PAdES signature locally to ensure assembly was performed correctly
The User now has a qualified electronic signature on the document, legally equivalent to a handwritten signature under eIDAS Article 26.
Audit Telemetry: RP_QES_ISSUED_SERVER_CENTRIC generated upon validation of the newly assembled PDF blob.
High — server-side CSC client + QTSP relationship + transaction_data
Yes — full server-side CSC v2.0 client required
Must comply with ETSI TS 119 101 (QES_24a)
RP-managed; no standardized discovery protocol
Cross-references: §15.15.5 (transaction_data for QES), §8 (OpenID4VP for wallet authentication in Phase 3).
The CSC API v2.0 defines 14 methods across 4 functional groups. All endpoints are relative to a base URI ending in /csc/v2:
RSSP metadata: name, region, auth types, methods, algorithms
Explicit user authentication (non-OAuth path)
List credential IDs for the authenticated user
Credential metadata: key, certificate chain, SCAL level
Authorize credential use (explicit auth mode)
Deliver OTP for challenge-response
credentials/extendTransaction
Extend transaction authorization window
Create PKCS#1 signatures from hash values
Sign entire documents server-side
Poll for async signing results
RFC 3161 timestamp generation

The EUDI reference implementation (eudi-lib-jvm-rqes-csc-kt) implements the core happy path: info → credentials/list → credentials/info → signatures/signHash, using OAuth2 for both service and credential authorization. The auth/login and credentials/authorize explicit-auth endpoints are not implemented because the EUDI ecosystem mandates OAuth2.
The most architecturally significant aspect of the CSC API is its two-tier OAuth2 authorization model:

Tier 1 — Service Authorization authorizes the application (Wallet or RP) to access the RSSP's general capabilities (listing credentials, querying info). The scope is service. The output is a service access token valid for credential listing and metadata operations.

Tier 2 — Credential Authorization authorizes the use of a specific signing credential for signing specific documents. The scope is credential. For SCAL2 (the mandated level per QES_23), the document hashes are embedded in the authorization request via Rich Authorization Requests (RAR) or scope parameters. The output is a credential access token cryptographically bound to the document hashes.

This two-tier model means that an attacker who compromises the service access token cannot sign documents — they can only list credentials. And an attacker who compromises the credential access token cannot sign different documents, because the token is bound to specific hashes (SCAL2).
When connecting to an RSSP, the first call is always GET /csc/v2/info. The response reveals:
CSC API version (e.g., "2.0.0.0")
Supported auth types: oauth2code, external, basic, digest, tls
OAuth2 Authorization Server metadata URL (.well-known/openid-configuration)
Which of the 14 CSC methods this RSSP supports
Whether async signing (polling) is supported
Whether OCSP/CRL data is returned with signatures
The EUDI reference implementation enforces that the base URI ends with /csc/v2 and uses HTTPS:
Sole Control Assurance Level (SCAL) determines the security binding between the User's authorization and the signing operation:
Authorization grants access to credential for N signatures
❌ Hashes NOT bound to authorization
Authorization is bound to specific document hashes
✅ RSSP verifies signHash hashes match authorized hashes

In the EUDI reference implementation, SCAL2 is enforced at the type level — the signHash method on CredentialAuthorized.SCAL2 does not require the caller to re-specify document hashes (they are already bound in the token):
The EUDI RQES library ecosystem consists of four packages:
CSC API v2.0 client — core protocol
CSC API v2.0 client — core protocol
Pre-built signing UI screens + flow
Pre-built signing UI screens + flow
The core CSCClient interface composes 8 capability traits via Kotlin delegation — each trait maps to a distinct phase of the signing flow:
The reference wallets configure a demo QTSP endpoint:
Key integration points in the reference wallets:
Android: DocumentSign navigation screen, QrScanFlow.Signature flow type, DeepLinkType.RQES and DeepLinkType.RQES_DOC_RETRIEVAL deep link handlers
iOS: EudiRQESUi Swift Package, rqes://oauth/callback deep link scheme

⚠️ Stability warning: The Document Retrieval protocol is not part of the CSC API specification. The EUDI reference implementation marks it with: "This flow may be removed in future versions of the library." RPs should implement it but be prepared for protocol evolution.

The Document Retrieval protocol bridges the gap between RP-initiated signing (Scenario B) and the CSC API signing flow. It solves the question: how does the RP tell the Wallet which documents to sign, and how does the Wallet return the signed results?
The protocol uses an OpenID4VP-like request/response pattern:
The RP publishes a request_uri (via QR code or deep link)
The Wallet resolves the URI to a JAR-signed request object
The request object contains document locations, hashes, and access methods
After signing, the Wallet dispatches results back to the RP
1. Relying Party Server presents QR code or deep link to User

The RP generates a QR code (for cross-device flow) or a deep link (for same-device flow) containing a request_uri that points to a JAR-signed request object. The URI follows the mdoc-openid4vp scheme defined in the EUDI reference implementation:

The RP must pre-compute the document hash and embed it in the request object before generating the QR code. The request_uri is a one-time URL — it should expire after a short TTL (e.g., 5 minutes) to prevent replay attacks.
Artifact Produced: One-time QR code payload containing JAR request_uri.
2. User scans QR code into EUDI Wallet

The User opens their EUDI Wallet and scans the QR code (cross-device) or taps the deep link (same-device). The Wallet recognises the mdoc-openid4vp scheme and extracts the request_uri and client_id parameters. At this point, no document content has been transferred — the Wallet only has a pointer to the RP's request object.
Artifact Produced: Extracted request_uri + client_id tuple in Wallet memory (User layer).
3. EUDI Wallet fetches JAR-signed request object from Relying Party Server
The Wallet performs an HTTP GET on the request_uri to fetch the request object. The RP returns a JAR-signed JWT (RFC 9101) containing the full request parameters:

The RP responds with the signed request object. The Wallet validates the JAR signature against the RP's X.509 certificate (resolved via the client_id_scheme — see §32.4.4 for the three supported schemes).
Artifact Produced: Raw JAR-signed JWT fetched from request_uri (Network layer).
4. Relying Party Server returns request object with document locations and hashes
The request object contains document metadata — locations, hashes, access methods, and labels:

The hash field is critical — it binds the request to a specific document version. The access_method tells the Wallet how to authenticate when downloading (see §32.4.3 for the full list of supported methods).
Artifact Produced: JAR-signed JWT containing explicit document hashes mapping to unauthenticated URIs.
5. EUDI Wallet parses and validates request object from Relying Party Server
The Wallet performs structural and cryptographic validation of the request object:
JAR signature verification — validate the JWT signature against the RP's certificate
Certificate chain validation — verify the RP's X.509 certificate chains to a trusted root
client_id matching — confirm the client_id in the JWT payload matches the SAN in the signing certificate
Document metadata parsing — extract documents[] array with URIs, hashes, and access methods
Supported format check — confirm the Wallet can handle the requested signing format
If any validation step fails, the Wallet rejects the request and displays an error to the User.
Failure Path: If client_id does not match the SAN in the X.509 certificate, the Wallet aborts with a DOCUMENT_REQUEST_INVALID_CLIENT error and does not proceed to document download.
Artifact Produced: Validated request object binding the RP identity, document hashes, and response_uri (Cryptographic layer).
6. EUDI Wallet downloads documents from Relying Party Server
The Wallet downloads each document from its uri using the specified access_method. For the common Public method, this is a simple HTTP GET:

For OAuth2 access, the Wallet uses a bearer token provided in the request object. For BasicAuth, it uses credentials from the request. The Wallet stores the downloaded document(s) in its secure local storage.
Artifact Produced: Raw document bytes stored in Wallet secure local storage (Device layer).
7. EUDI Wallet computes document hashes and verifies integrity against Relying Party Server JAR

After download, the Wallet computes the cryptographic hash of each document and compares it to the hash value from the request object. This is the primary defence against document substitution attacks:

If any hash mismatch is detected, the Wallet immediately aborts the flow and alerts the User. This prevents an attacker from modifying the document between when the RP computed the hash and when the Wallet downloads it.
Artifact Produced: Hash-verified document binding proof (Cryptographic layer).
8. EUDI Wallet performs full CSC API signing flow against Remote QTSP

The Wallet executes the full CSC API v2.0 signing protocol as detailed in §32.1 — service-level OAuth2 authorization, credential listing, document hash computation (PAdES-specific), credential-level SCAL2 authorization with hash binding, signHash invocation, and signed document assembly. For the complete step-by-step breakdown of this phase, see the Scenario B walkthrough (§32.2.2, steps 4–10).
Artifact Produced: PKCS#1 signature value returned from Remote QTSP QSCD (Protocol layer).
9. EUDI Wallet dispatches signed documents to Relying Party Server
After signing, the Wallet sends the results back to the RP at the response_uri specified in the request object using direct_post:

The RP can request either documentWithSignature (complete signed PDF — the Wallet embeds the CMS signature into the PDF) or signatureObject (detached CMS signature bytes — the RP assembles the final PDF server-side). See §32.4.5 for the full dispatch specification.
Artifact Produced: Signed PAdES document dispatched to Relying Party response_uri (Network layer).
10. Relying Party Server acknowledges receipt and validates PAdES signature
The RP validates the received signed document (PAdES signature verification per ETSI EN 319 102-1), stores it, and sends an acknowledgement response to the Wallet:
The Wallet displays a confirmation to the User indicating that the signing flow is complete. The RP logs the signing event per §32.8 (transaction logging requirements).

Failure Path: If the provided document_id does not map to an active signing session managed by the RP, the inbound callback is forcefully terminated with an HTTP 404 and anomalous behavior metric incremented.
Audit Telemetry: RP_QES_RECEIVED recorded by the main accounting ledger linking the session reference.
The request URI format follows the mdoc-openid4vp scheme:
